title: Gap-fix A: strip restated methodology from analyzing-test-effectiveness tail
description: Reviewer gap: tail still restates RED/YELLOW/GREEN methodology (critical_rules 1-8, analysis-failure/excuse lists, checklist analysis-quality blocks, second worked example)
design:
Classification: medium

## Goal
Everything after `</the_process>` in skills/analyzing-test-effectiveness/SKILL.md is orchestration-only: RED/YELLOW/GREEN appear solely as category names/counts/citations; every methodology rule, temptation list, and quality checklist lives only in agents/test-effectiveness-analyst.md.

## Why
Epic reviewer gap (bd-sv5d, critical): the epic's success criterion says "RED/YELLOW/GREEN methodology detail lives only in the agent file (skill cites, never restates)", but the tail still teaches categorization — and contradicts the file's own L579 guard against exactly this. The cleanup fell through the seam between Task 5 (body-only) and Task 6 (phrasing-only).

## Context
All sites verified in the working tree (branch epic/bd-sv5d, post-e6583ad). The body (frontmatter through `</the_process>`, ~L411) is orchestration-correct — do not touch it. Tail sites: second worked example L467-535 ("Mock-heavy test suite" — full RED-categorization walkthrough with classification table and replacement code); critical_rules L541-548 (items 1-8 are analysis rules; items 9-10 are orchestration — keep those); "Common Analysis Failures" L552-561; "Common Excuses" L563-579 (mixed: most entries are test-categorization excuses, three are orchestration-level); verification_checklist L585-605 ("Analysis Quality (MANDATORY)" block, "Per module" block, and the "Overall" block's methodology-judgment lines). The "bd Integration" and "SRE Refinement Verification" checklist blocks (L607-619) are already batch-phrased orchestration — keep. The agent file (agents/test-effectiveness-analyst.md) already contains the removed material — cite it, never copy into it.

## Implementation
1. Delete the entire second example block (L467-535, `<example>` through `</example>` for "Mock-heavy test suite that breaks on every refactor"). The first example already demonstrates the orchestration flow; this one teaches methodology.
2. critical_rules: keep items 9-10 (bd tracking, batch SRE) and the L579-style guard; replace items 1-8 with orchestration rules, e.g.: methodology lives in agents/test-effectiveness-analyst.md — cite, never restate or re-derive; dispatch the analyst as a blocking subagent — never run the analysis in the lead context; confirm scope before dispatching; every generated task carries its two-tier spec sections. Renumber cleanly.
3. Delete the "Common Analysis Failures" section (L552-561) — it duplicates the agent's temptation list.
4. "Common Excuses": keep only the orchestration-level entries — "I'll just fix these without bd", "SRE refinement is overkill for test fixes", "I'll restate the RED/YELLOW/GREEN criteria here for clarity" — and reframe the section lead-in accordingly (it currently says "the test is probably RED or YELLOW", which is a categorization claim). Delete the test-categorization excuses; the agent file owns them.
5. verification_checklist: replace the "Analysis Quality (MANDATORY)" and "Per module" blocks and the "Overall" block's methodology-judgment lines (e.g. "GREEN count is MINORITY") with orchestration checks, e.g.: scope confirmed before dispatch; analyst dispatched as blocking subagent, full report received per its Output Format (Return Contract); findings presented and action agreed with the user; every generated task conforms to its two-tier template. Keep the "bd Integration" and "SRE Refinement Verification" blocks as they stand.
6. Read the resulting tail top to bottom: coherent, orchestration-voiced, no dangling references to the deleted sections.

## Tests
Omit — pure documentation task.

## Verification
- `awk '/<\/the_process>/{f=1} f' skills/analyzing-test-effectiveness/SKILL.md | grep -n "GREEN is the exception\|junior engineer quality\|Read production code BEFORE\|false GREEN\|look reasonable"` → no output
- `grep -n "Mock-heavy test suite" skills/analyzing-test-effectiveness/SKILL.md` → no output
- Guards stay clean: `grep -in "on each task\|on all tasks\|all tasks reviewed with\|applied to each task" skills/analyzing-test-effectiveness/SKILL.md` → no output; `grep -n "Phase [0-9]\|Step 5\." skills/analyzing-test-effectiveness/SKILL.md` → no output
- Body untouched: region from line 1 through `</the_process>` is byte-identical to HEAD before this task
- RED/YELLOW/GREEN in the tail appear only as category names/counts/citations (read and confirm)
- Pre-commit hooks passing (the task commit succeeds with hooks enabled)

## Boundaries
Do not touch the body (`<the_process>` and above). Do not edit agents/test-effectiveness-analyst.md (it already owns the material — this task deletes duplicates, it does not move text). Do not touch commands/analyze-tests.md.
notes: Stage 1 (2026-07-08): tail methodology removals correct, but executor made an unrequested version bump 3.23.0->3.23.1 that breaks the epic success criterion (must be 3.23.0; R9 = one minor bump per epic). Re-dispatched to revert the version line only.

title: Rewrite volatility-decomposition Step 0 + Step 3 for LikeC4 output
description: The volatility-decomposition skill currently creates bd architecture epics and component nodes (Step 3, lines 387-475) and detects existing architecture via bd labels (Step 0, lines 46-119). Rewrite both to use LikeC4 .c4 files instead. This is the foundational change — it defines the LikeC4 model format that brainstorming and audit skills will read from.
design:
## Goal
Rewrite the volatility-decomposition skill to produce LikeC4 .c4 files + linked markdown docs + bd work issues (for the delta) instead of bd architecture nodes. Also update Step 0 context detection to check for existing LikeC4 model files instead of bd architecture epics.

## Effort Estimate
6-8 hours (largest single skill change in the epic)

## Implementation

### 1. Study existing code
- skills/volatility-decomposition/SKILL.md lines 46-119: Step 0 context detection (INCEPTION vs REVISION mode)
- skills/volatility-decomposition/SKILL.md lines 387-475: Step 3 encode architecture in bd
- skills/volatility-decomposition/SKILL.md lines 535-559: Step 5 handoff guidance

### 2. Rewrite Step 0: Context Detection

Replace bd-based detection with LikeC4 file detection:

CURRENT:
```bash
bd list --label arch --type epic --status open
```

NEW:
```bash
ls arch/*.c4 2>/dev/null
```

If .c4 files found → REVISION MODE:
- Query LikeC4 MCP for current model state (use read-project-summary, then read-element for each component)
- Read all elements with metadata (volatility_axis, layer, stability_state)
- Read all relationships
- Identify #target elements (aspirational) vs implemented elements (no tag)

If no .c4 files found → INCEPTION MODE:
- Starting fresh, proceed to Socratic questioning

Codebase investigation (Step 0a) and data coupling investigation (Step 0b) are UNCHANGED — these gather evidence from actual code regardless of architecture representation.

### 3. Rewrite Step 3: Encode Architecture

Replace bd create/update commands with LikeC4 file creation:

#### 3a. Create/update arch/spec.c4
Define element kinds mapping to Lowy layers:
```
specification {
    element resourceAccessor {
        style { shape storage }
    }
    element engine {
        style { shape component }
    }
    element manager {
        style { shape rectangle }
    }
    element boundary {
        style { shape queue }
    }
    element utility {
        style { shape cylinder }
    }
    
    relationship blocks
    relationship relatesTo
    
    tag target
}
```

#### 3b. Create/update arch/model.c4
Top-level system elements and relationships:
```
model {
    systemName = manager 'System Name' {
        description 'System purpose from decomposition'
        
        // Components defined in separate files
    }
}
```

#### 3c. Create arch/components/<name>.c4 for each component
```
model {
    systemName {
        componentName = engine 'Component Title' {
            summary 'One-line responsibility'
            description 'Encapsulates [volatility axis]. [Layer] reasoning.'
            metadata {
                volatility_axis '[what this component encapsulates]'
                layer '[manager|engine|resourceAccessor|utility]'
                stability_state 'exploring'
            }
            link ../doc/arch/components/component-name.md 'Design Doc'
            #target
        }
    }
}
```

#### 3d. Create doc/arch/components/<name>.md for each component
Prose documentation linked from LikeC4 element:
```markdown
# Component Name

## Volatility Axis
[What this component encapsulates and why this axis was chosen]

## Layer: [Manager|Engine|Resource Accessor|Utility]
[Why this layer assignment]

## Interface Contract
- IN: [operations exposed]
- OUT: [operations called]

## Responsibility
[1-2 sentences on what this component does]

## What Changes Should Be Local
[What kinds of changes should only affect this component]
```

#### 3e. Add relationships in arch/model.c4 or component files
```
model {
    componentA -> componentB 'interface description' {
        // blocks relationship
    }
    componentC -> componentD 'interaction description' {
        // relatesTo relationship
    }
}
```

#### 3f. Create arch/views/landscape.c4
```
views {
    view landscape {
        include *
        title 'System Architecture'
    }
    
    view targetComponents {
        include * where tag is 'target'
        title 'Aspirational Components'
    }
}
```

#### 3g. Create dynamic views for important data flows
```
views {
    dynamic view flowName {
        entryPoint ->> componentA
        componentA ->> componentB
        componentB ->> componentC
        title 'Flow Name'
        description 'What this flow represents'
    }
}
```

#### 3h. Create bd work issues for the delta
Instead of creating bd architecture nodes, create bd work issues that reference the LikeC4 components:
```bash
bd create --title='Implement [Component Name]' --type=feature --priority=2 \
  --description='Build the [component] as defined in arch/components/[name].c4. See doc/arch/components/[name].md for interface contract and design rationale. References ADR-NNN.'
```

### 4. Add pre-fit mode behavior

When a component has stability_state 'pre-fit' in metadata:
- Socratic questions focus on biggest unknowns, not precise axis boundaries
- Do NOT fractal-decompose pre-fit components (shallower nesting)
- ADRs for this component MUST include falsification criteria section
- Boundary annotations note interface cost explicitly
- The linked markdown doc notes: 'This component volatility axis is a hypothesis. See ADR-NNN for falsification criteria.'

### 5. Update Step 5: Handoff Guidance

Update references from bd architecture to LikeC4 model:
- Reference arch/*.c4 files instead of bd architecture epic
- Reference /audit-arch loading from LikeC4
- Reference /brainstorm detecting LikeC4 context

### 6. Add migration path section

Add a new section to the skill documenting how to migrate from bd-based architecture to LikeC4:
1. Create arch/ directory and spec.c4 with element kinds
2. For each architecture component node in bd: create corresponding LikeC4 element
3. For each edge in bd: create corresponding LikeC4 relationship
4. Create doc/arch/components/ markdown docs
5. Existing ADRs unchanged
6. Create views (landscape at minimum)
7. Mark aspirational components with #target tag
8. Close bd architecture epic
9. Create new bd work issues for remaining work

### 7. Handle REVISION MODE updates

In REVISION MODE (existing LikeC4 model), the skill:
- Reads current model via MCP
- Proposes changes to existing components (update .c4 files)
- Proposes new components (create new .c4 + .md files)
- Proposes removing components (delete .c4 + .md, or mark deprecated)
- Updates relationships
- Does NOT use bd supersedes — just edits the .c4 files directly

### 8. Test with subagents (writing-skills)

Test cases:
- INCEPTION MODE: No arch/ directory exists → skill creates full LikeC4 model
- REVISION MODE: arch/ files exist → skill reads via MCP, proposes changes
- Pre-fit component: stability_state 'pre-fit' → shallower decomposition, falsification criteria in ADR
- Post-fit component: stability_state 'exploring' → normal decomposition depth
- Element kinds map correctly to Lowy layers (manager/engine/resourceAccessor/utility)
- Relationships created with correct types (blocks vs relatesTo)
- Dynamic views created for important data flows (curated, not auto-generated)
- Linked markdown docs created in doc/arch/components/
- bd work issues created for delta (NOT bd architecture nodes)
- Migration path section present and accurate

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Step 0 detects existing LikeC4 model via ls arch/*.c4 (not bd list --label arch)
- [ ] INCEPTION MODE creates: spec.c4, model.c4, components/*.c4, doc/arch/components/*.md, views/landscape.c4
- [ ] REVISION MODE loads existing model via LikeC4 MCP and proposes updates
- [ ] Each component .c4 file has metadata: volatility_axis, layer, stability_state
- [ ] Each component has linked markdown doc via link keyword
- [ ] Aspirational components tagged with #target
- [ ] Relationships use blocks and relatesTo types from spec
- [ ] Dynamic views created for important data flows (curated, not auto-generated)
- [ ] bd work issues created for delta (referencing LikeC4 components, NOT creating bd architecture nodes)
- [ ] Pre-fit components get shallower decomposition and ADRs with falsification criteria
- [ ] Migration path section documents bd-to-LikeC4 migration steps
- [ ] Step 5 handoff guidance references LikeC4 model (not bd architecture epic)
- [ ] No bd architecture-related commands remain (no bd list --label arch, no bd create --label arch,component)
- [ ] Tested with subagents per writing-skills skill
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN for this task)
- ❌ Do NOT create bd architecture nodes (epic anti-pattern: no architecture state in bd)
- ❌ Do NOT auto-generate dynamic views for every data flow (epic anti-pattern: curated only)
- ❌ Do NOT create current/target model split (epic anti-pattern: single model with #target tag)
- ❌ Do NOT add .c4 file parsing as fallback for MCP (epic anti-pattern: require MCP)
- ❌ Do NOT use global pre-fit setting (epic anti-pattern: per-component via stability_state metadata)
- ❌ Do NOT change Socratic questioning logic (Steps 1-2) — only change Steps 0, 3, and 5
- ❌ Do NOT change ADR creation process (Step 4) — ADRs are independent of architecture representation
- ❌ Do NOT use multi-project LikeC4 setup (epic anti-pattern: single project with nested elements)

## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**Edge Case: LikeC4 MCP Not Running**
In REVISION MODE, MCP is required to read the existing model. If MCP is not available, the skill should fail with a clear error: 'LikeC4 MCP server is required. Run: likec4 mcp --stdio' — do NOT fall back to parsing .c4 files.

**Edge Case: Partial Model**
Some .c4 files might exist but model might be incomplete (e.g., spec.c4 exists but no components). Treat as REVISION MODE but note missing elements and propose creating them.

**Edge Case: REVISION MODE Element Updates**
When updating existing components in REVISION MODE, the skill edits .c4 files directly (using Edit tool). This means the skill must read the current file content before editing. Do NOT overwrite entire files — use targeted edits to preserve existing content and formatting.

**Regression Risk: Socratic Questioning**
Steps 1-2 (Socratic questioning and structure proposal) should be UNCHANGED. The proposal table format changes slightly (adds coupling evidence on edges per the epic design) but the questioning process itself stays the same.

**File Creation Order**
Create files in this order: spec.c4 first (defines element kinds), then model.c4 (defines system), then components/*.c4 (defines elements), then views (references elements). This ensures each file can reference types defined in previous files.

title: Executor agent: format drift and context exhaustion during task execution
description:
Recurring user feedback during development: the hyperpowers:executor agent exhibits two reliability issues mid-task.

## Symptoms

**1. Output format drift (~5 of 9 dispatches in one session)**
Executor returns SRE-review content (or other freeform analysis) instead of the structured COMPLETE / ESCALATION / CONTEXT_LIMIT format the lead expects.
- Work still lands correctly (commits + bd closes are real)
- But the lead must re-validate because it can't parse the expected structure
- Costs a round of lead-side validation each time

**2. Context exhaustion (3 hits in one session)**
Executor hits 'Prompt is too long' mid-task.
- User workaround: phase-by-phase commits + lean continuation prompts
- Workaround cost: ~1 hour per session
- This is a known constraint the executor is supposed to handle via CONTEXT_LIMIT checkpoint output, but it's not triggering reliably before hard exhaustion

## Frequency
User reports: 'I get this feedback a lot during development' — recurring, not a one-off.

## Hypotheses to investigate
- Executor prompt (agents/executor.md) may not enforce the COMPLETE format strongly enough when executor has been doing review-style work
- Context budget heuristics may not be tripping early enough to checkpoint cleanly
- TaskCreate sub-step tracking may be inflating context faster than the checkpoint threshold assumes

## Acceptance criteria
- Identify why executor drifts to SRE-review output format
- Strengthen agents/executor.md to make COMPLETE format output non-optional regardless of task content
- Add self-check: executor verifies its output matches one of the three expected envelopes before returning
- Investigate context exhaustion — either lower the checkpoint threshold, shrink prompt size, or both
- Consider a PostToolUse validation hook that checks executor output format
notes:
User feedback (2026-04-26): PostToolUse validation hook is too heavy. Stick to prompt-side fixes:
1. Tighten agents/executor.md so the COMPLETE/ESCALATION/CONTEXT_LIMIT envelope is non-optional regardless of task content
2. Add a self-check step at the end of the executor's process — verify output matches one of the three envelopes before returning
3. Lower the context budget at which CONTEXT_LIMIT checkpoint fires; investigate whether TaskCreate sub-step usage is inflating context faster than threshold anticipates

Drop: PostToolUse output-format hook (too heavy for the value).
close_reason: Fix shipped via 3 commits (e4546ae, bd96533, 2c4fee7); reviewer approved all 15 success criteria; new executor empirically demonstrated clean envelope behavior. Plugin version 3.8.1.

title: Task 3: Create team-executing-plans skill
design:
## Goal
Create skills/team-executing-plans/SKILL.md — a new skill that spawns agent teams from wave tasks, coordinates execution with hybrid delegation (Option C), reviews results at wave boundaries, reconciles beads state, and plans next waves with mandatory STOP checkpoints.

## Context
Completed bd-7yn: Brainstorming skill now produces epics with Parallelism Map.
Completed bd-b5u: Wave-planning skill creates task batches with beads dependencies from Parallelism Map.

This skill is the execution engine for the team path:
\`\`\`
brainstorming → sre-task-refinement → wave-planning → team-executing-plans
\`\`\`

## Effort Estimate
4-6 hours (single skill file, complex process design with spawn template)

## Implementation

### Step 1: Study reference materials
- Read skills/executing-plans/SKILL.md — Focus on: STOP checkpoint pattern, epic immutability enforcement, substep tracking via TodoWrite, resumption check pattern
- Read skills/wave-planning/SKILL.md — Focus on: wave task structure (file ownership, epic context), how tasks are linked to epic, wave sizing
- Read agents/test-runner.md — Focus on: agent definition format (frontmatter fields), how subagent_type maps to agent files
- Study Claude Code agent teams documentation (code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams) — Extract: how to spawn teams via Task tool, delegate mode syntax, plan approval mechanism, teammate communication protocol
- Study Claude Code subagent features (code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) — Extract: memory parameter, permissionMode options, skills injection, hooks parameter, disallowedTools

### Step 2: Create the skill file
Create skills/team-executing-plans/SKILL.md with the standard skill structure.

**Frontmatter:**
\`\`\`yaml
---
name: team-executing-plans
description: Use after wave-planning to spawn agent teams for parallel wave execution - coordinates teammates with hybrid delegation, reviews results at wave boundaries, reconciles beads state
---
\`\`\`

**Core Process (the_process section):**

1. **Load Wave Context**
   - \`bd show <epic-id>\` — Load epic requirements, anti-patterns
   - \`bd ready\` — Find wave tasks ready for execution
   - Verify wave tasks exist and are independent (no blocking deps between them)
   - If only 1 wave task exists: fall back to solo execution (use executing-plans skill). Do NOT spawn a team for a single task.

2. **Prepare Teammate Spawn Prompts**
   For each wave task, construct a teammate prompt using this template:

   \`\`\`
   You are a teammate executing one task in a parallel wave. Work independently.

   ## Your Task
   [Paste full bd show output for this task]

   ## Epic Contract (IMMUTABLE)
   **Requirements:** [Copy from epic]
   **Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN):** [Copy from epic]

   ## File Ownership Boundaries
   **You OWN (modify these files only):**
   - [exclusive file paths from task]

   **You MUST NOT modify (owned by other teammates):**
   - [file paths from other wave tasks]

   **Shared files (read-only for you, coordination handled by [task/wave]):**
   - [shared file paths]

   ## Methodology
   - Follow TDD: Write test first (RED) → implement minimal code (GREEN) → refactor → commit
   - Before claiming done: run all tests, capture output as evidence
   - Commit after each GREEN phase

   ## Beads Constraints
   - RUN: \`bd update <your-task-id> --status in_progress\` when you start
   - RUN: \`bd close <your-task-id>\` when all success criteria met
   - DO NOT RUN: \`bd sync\`, \`bd create\`, \`bd dep add\`, or any other bd commands
   - DO NOT modify any other task's status

   ## Communication Protocol
   - If you discover work that should be done but is NOT in your task: note it in your final summary (lead will create a task)
   - If you hit a blocker that prevents completion: stop and report the blocker in your summary
   - DO NOT attempt to fix problems outside your file ownership boundary
   - DO NOT communicate with other teammates directly
   \`\`\`

3. **Spawn Agent Team (Lead in Delegate Mode)**
   - Lead enters delegate mode: coordinate only, no implementation
   - For each wave task, spawn a teammate using the Task tool:
     \`\`\`
     Task tool:
       subagent_type: general-purpose
       prompt: [teammate spawn prompt from Step 2]
       run_in_background: true
       model: [sonnet by default, or opus for high-complexity tasks]
     \`\`\`
   - Spawn ALL teammates in a single message (parallel execution)
   - Lead monitors progress via TaskOutput (non-blocking checks)
   - Lead does NOT implement any code during team execution

4. **Collect and Review Wave Results**
   After all teammates finish (or time out):

   **a) Collect results:**
   - Read each teammate's output via TaskOutput
   - Note: success/partial/failed status for each

   **b) Verify success criteria:**
   - For each task: check if teammate reported all criteria met
   - Run integration tests via test-runner agent: test suite covering wave deliverables

   **c) Check for file ownership violations:**
   - Run \`git diff --name-only\` to see all changed files
   - Cross-reference against each task's file ownership boundaries
   - If a file was modified by a teammate who doesn't own it: FLAG as violation
   - Remediation: revert the violating changes (\`git checkout -- <file>\`) and note for user

   **d) Reconcile beads state:**
   - \`bd show <task-id>\` for each wave task — verify status
   - If teammate succeeded but didn't close task: \`bd close <task-id> --reason "Completed by teammate, lead reconciliation"\`
   - If teammate failed: keep task open, add notes with failure reason
   - Run \`bd sync\` to push beads state to git

5. **Present Wave Review Summary and STOP**
   Present comprehensive review summary (see template in success criteria).
   **STOP and wait for user review. Mandatory.**

**Key Design Decisions:**
- Lead operates in delegate mode during spawning (coordinate only, no implementation)
- Teammates execute independently; all coordination flows through lead
- STOP at wave boundaries is mandatory (epic anti-pattern)
- Beads reconciliation happens after teammate completion (lead handles sync)
- Teammate prompts are comprehensive (teammates have zero context without them)
- Degenerate case (1 wave task): fall back to solo execution, not team
- Default teammate model: sonnet (cost-efficient), use opus for high-complexity streams

### Step 3: Define teammate spawn prompt template
Already defined inline in Step 2 above. The template includes all 7 required components:
1. Role description
2. Epic requirements and anti-patterns
3. Task-specific details (from bd show)
4. File ownership boundaries
5. Methodology (TDD, verification)
6. Beads constraints
7. Communication protocol

### Step 4: Add examples
Include 2 examples:
1. **Good example:** 3 teammates execute Wave 2 (OAuth providers) successfully. Lead collects results, runs integration tests, reconciles beads, proposes Wave 3. Shows per-teammate results table.
2. **Bad example:** Lead implements a fix for a teammate's failing test instead of flagging it. Violates delegate mode. Shows why this is wrong and the correct approach (flag to user at STOP checkpoint).

### Step 5: Add integration section
Document the call chain:
- Called by: wave-planning (after user approves wave), or user directly
- Calls: test-runner agent (for integration verification after wave), wave-planning (handoff for next wave creation)
- Uses: bd commands (show, close, sync), Task tool (for teammate spawning with run_in_background: true), TaskOutput (for collecting results)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] skills/team-executing-plans/SKILL.md exists with standard skill structure (frontmatter, overview, rigidity, quick_reference, when_to_use, the_process, examples, critical_rules, verification_checklist, integration, resources)
- [ ] Skill spawns agent teams from wave tasks using Task tool with run_in_background: true
- [ ] Lead operates in delegate mode (coordinate only, no implementation during team execution)
- [ ] Teammate spawn prompts include ALL of: epic requirements, task details, anti-patterns, file ownership boundaries (owns + must-not-touch + shared), TDD methodology, verification-before-completion methodology
- [ ] Teammates constrained to: bd update --status and bd close for own task only; NO bd sync, create, or dep add
- [ ] Wave review includes: per-teammate results table, integration test verification via test-runner agent, beads reconciliation (verify/fix task statuses + bd sync), next wave proposal with learnings
- [ ] Mandatory STOP checkpoint at wave boundary with comprehensive review summary
- [ ] Two examples included: successful 3-teammate wave execution + delegate mode violation
- [ ] Integration section documents full call chain (wave-planning → team-executing-plans → test-runner → wave-planning)
- [ ] Hybrid delegation model documented: teammates execute + propose new work in summary, lead reviews proposals and approves/rejects scope changes at STOP checkpoint
- [ ] Degenerate case handled: if only 1 wave task, fall back to solo execution (executing-plans)
- [ ] File ownership violation detection: git diff --name-only cross-referenced against task boundaries, with revert remediation

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO lead implementing alongside teammates (delegate mode = coordinate only)
- ❌ NO teammates creating bd tasks or managing dependencies (lead manages all bd create, dep add)
- ❌ NO teammates running bd sync (lead handles git sync at wave boundaries)
- ❌ NO skipping wave-level STOP checkpoints (human must review wave results before next wave)
- ❌ NO continuous multi-wave execution without human checkpoint (each wave boundary is mandatory STOP)
- ❌ NO teammates messaging each other directly (all coordination flows through lead)
- ❌ NO proceeding to next wave without beads reconciliation (verify all task statuses, run bd sync)
- ❌ NO spawning a team for a single task (degenerate case: use solo executing-plans instead)
- ❌ NO teammates editing files outside their assigned ownership boundary

## Key Considerations (ADDED BY SRE REVIEW)

### Edge Case: Teammate Timeout/Crash
- A teammate may time out or crash while others succeed
- Lead should check TaskOutput with timeout for each teammate
- If teammate times out: mark task as still open, note timeout in wave review
- If teammate crashes with error: capture error message, mark task open, include in review
- Do NOT retry automatically — present to user at STOP checkpoint for decision (retry, reassign, skip)
- Anti-pattern: silently retrying or ignoring failed teammates

### Edge Case: Teammate File Ownership Violation
- Detection: after all teammates complete, run \`git diff --name-only\` and cross-reference against file ownership boundaries from each task
- If violation found: \`git checkout -- <violated-file>\` to revert unauthorized changes
- Report violation in wave review summary with: which teammate, which file, what was changed
- User decides remediation at STOP checkpoint

### Edge Case: All Teammates Fail
- If ALL teammates fail (0 success): do NOT proceed to next wave
- Present all failure reasons in wave review
- User decides: retry wave, switch to solo execution, or abort
- Anti-pattern: lead trying to "fix" failures by implementing (violates delegate mode)

### Edge Case: Beads State Inconsistency
- Beads daemon serializes writes, so concurrent bd update is safe
- However: if teammate crashes between task work and bd close, task stays open
- Lead reconciliation (Step 4d) catches this: verify each task status, close if completed
- If unsure whether task actually completed: keep open and note for user review

### Edge Case: Degenerate Wave (1 Task)
- Wave-planning may produce a wave with only 1 task (e.g., infrastructure wave)
- team-executing-plans should NOT spawn a team for 1 task (coordination overhead > benefit)
- Fall back to executing-plans (solo) for single-task waves
- Check at Step 1: if only 1 ready wave task, redirect to executing-plans

### Edge Case: Large Spawn Prompts Exceeding Context
- Spawn prompt includes full epic requirements + task details + template
- For very large epics, this could be substantial
- Mitigation: copy only RELEVANT requirements and anti-patterns (not entire epic)
- Include a \`bd show <task-id>\` command in the prompt so teammate can reload if needed
- Anti-pattern: copying entire epic verbatim into every spawn prompt

### Performance Consideration: Teammate Model Selection
- Default: sonnet (cost-efficient, good for most tasks)
- Override to opus: when task complexity is high AND task has many edge cases from SRE refinement
- Document the model choice in wave review summary
- Epic open question: "Should all teammates use same model?" — resolve per-task based on complexity estimate from Parallelism Map
close_reason: Created skills/team-executing-plans/SKILL.md with all 12 success criteria met

title: Task 2: Add commit verification gate to executing-plans lead validation
design:
## Goal
Update skills/executing-plans/SKILL.md so the lead expects and validates commit hashes in every task completion message before approving proposals.

## Effort Estimate
1 hour (markdown-only edits to a single file)

## Implementation

### 1. Update task completion message format (lines 137-143)
Add ### Commits section to the format summary shown to the lead, matching executor.md's format:
```
## Task <id> Complete
### Done — [implementation summary]
### Commits — [hash: message]
### Learned — [discoveries]
### Changed from plan — [deviations]
### Proposed next task — [title, goal, approach, SRE refined]
```

### 2. Add Step 0 to lead validation (before current step 1 at line 147)
Insert a new step before the current "1. Read the summary":

**0. Verify commit hashes.** Check the executor's message for a `### Commits` section containing at least one entry in the format `<short hash>: <message>`. If the Commits section is missing or empty, do NOT proceed to proposal validation — redirect the executor to commit first (see REDIRECT-FOR-COMMITS below).

Renumber existing steps: 1→1, 2→2, 3→3, 4→4 (no renumber needed, Step 0 is added before 1).

### 3. Add REDIRECT-FOR-COMMITS response template
After the existing REDIRECT template (around line 187), add:

**REDIRECT (missing commits)** — Executor did not commit before closing task:
```
SendMessage:
  type: "message"
  recipient: "executor"
  content: "Task completion message is missing commit hashes. Commit all work for the task before I can evaluate your proposal. Send updated completion message with ### Commits section containing hash and message."
  summary: "Commit first — missing commit hashes"
```

### 4. Update example task completion message (lines 511-532)
Add ### Commits section to the example between Done and Learned:
```
### Commits
- `a1b2c3d`: feat: implement user registration with bcrypt and input validation
```

### 5. Add commit hash check to verification checklist (lines 634-639)
Add to "Before approving any proposal:" section:
- [ ] Verified ### Commits section contains at least one hash

## Anti-Patterns (Task-Specific)
- ❌ Do NOT require the lead to run git log (lead checks message format only, per epic anti-pattern)
- ❌ Do NOT add hooks for enforcement (per epic anti-pattern)
- ❌ Do NOT change sections outside of the five edits listed above

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Task completion message format (lines 137-143) includes ### Commits
- [ ] Step 0 added to lead validation checking for commit hashes with actual format validation
- [ ] REDIRECT-FOR-COMMITS template added after existing REDIRECT
- [ ] Example task completion message (lines 511-532) includes ### Commits section
- [ ] Verification checklist includes commit hash check
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

title: Task 2: SRE skill — single authority statement (update-only, no create/close/re-classify)
description: Reconcile sre-task-refinement's contradictory authority statements to the canonical rule from brainstorming's Step 7 dispatch text (R2)
design:
## Goal
skills/sre-task-refinement/SKILL.md states one authority rule, consistent everywhere: the SRE reviewer may strengthen task specs directly via bd update (preserve existing sections; never insert placeholders); it may NOT create, close, or re-classify tasks — structural changes (splits, new tasks, reordering) are recommendations in its report. This matches brainstorming SKILL.md Step 7's dispatch text (L356-358), which is canonical and needs no edit.

## Why
The skill currently contradicts itself and its dispatcher (epic bd-k9aa R2): the batch-mode scope line says SRE "does not directly modify the plan" (too weak — even strengthening is listed as a suggestion), while Category 1, Step 4, and "Breaking Down Large Tasks" instruct direct `bd create` subtask splits (too strong — brainstorming's dispatch forbids creation). A dispatched reviewer obeying one site violates another.

## Context
Canonical statement (conform to, verbatim intent): "You may strengthen task specs directly via bd update (preserve existing sections; never insert placeholders). Do not create, close, or re-classify tasks — structural suggestions go in your report."

## Implementation
Sites (anchors verified 2026-07-08; Task 1 may have shifted lines — locate by heading/quoted text):
1. "## How This Skill Is Invoked" (L51-56): state the canonical authority rule once here, prominently. L55 ("Apply bd updates directly where the process says to") stays consistent with it.
2. Batch Mode scope line (L138: "Batch mode scope: SRE can suggest adding a task... These are suggestions to the lead — SRE does not directly modify the plan."): rewrite to conform — strengthening via bd update IS direct; creating/closing/re-classifying is NOT allowed; structural suggestions go in the report. Cite the authority statement from item 1 rather than restating variants.
3. Category 1 (L144-165): the over-ceiling instructions at L158-163 ("Break into smaller tasks / Create subtasks with bd create / Link with bd dep add / Update parent to coordinator role") → replace with: flag the task and write a split recommendation (proposed subtask titles, scope, dependencies) in the report.
4. "## Breaking Down Large Tasks" (L406-447, including bd create/bd dep add commands L411-432 and the coordinator rewrite L434-447): rewrite the section wholesale as "Recommending Task Splits" — keep the analytical guidance (where to draw split boundaries, what each subtask needs), replace all direct bd create/dep-add/coordinator actions with report-recommendation format.
5. Output Format example L500 ("bd-5 - Broke into 3 subtasks (was 40 min, now 3x10 min)") → recommendation phrasing ("bd-5 - Recommended split into 3 subtasks...").
6. Verification checklist L920-922 ("Updated task with missing information via bd update --design" stays; "Broke down any task exceeding the ceiling into subtasks" → "Recommended splits in the report for any task exceeding the ceiling").
7. Resources/integration L964 ("This skill uses: bd commands (show, update, create, dep add, dep tree)") → remove create and dep add.
8. L347 (promotion flag "a suggestion to the lead, not something SRE sets directly") is already conformant — leave as-is.

## Tests
None because this is a documentation file with no runtime surface; verification is grep + read-through.

## Verification
- `grep -n "bd create\|bd dep add" skills/sre-task-refinement/SKILL.md` → zero matches (recommendations describe splits in prose, not commands)
- `grep -n "does not directly modify" skills/sre-task-refinement/SKILL.md` → zero matches
- Read "How This Skill Is Invoked", Batch Mode scope, Category 1, and the renamed split section: all four consistent with the canonical statement; the rule is STATED once and cited elsewhere
- `grep -n "strengthen task specs directly via bd update" skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md` → 1 match (the Step 7 dispatch text, ~L356-358 — locate by text, not line number: Task 7 edits earlier lines in this file and may shift them). Visually confirm the SRE skill's authority statement matches it in substance. Brainstorming file itself unchanged by this task: `git diff --stat skills/brainstorming/` → empty
- The rewritten split-recommendation section (Changes item 4) keeps epic R1 intact: `grep -n "Effort Estimate\|Implementation Checklist\|Key Considerations" skills/sre-task-refinement/SKILL.md` still returns zero after the rewrite, and proposed-subtask outlines use two-tier vocabulary (Goal/Changes/Verification) — no per-task "Success Criteria" headers reintroduced
- Pre-commit hooks pass on the task's commit

## Boundaries
- Only modify skills/sre-task-refinement/SKILL.md
- Do not edit skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md (its Step 7 dispatch text is the canonical source)
- Preserve the analytical CONTENT of Category 1 and the split-guidance section — only the authority/action vocabulary changes
- Do not change the Output Format's overall structure; only action-claim phrasing

title: Task 4: contract registry — Stage-2 payload slimmed + completion gate marker (R5)
description: loop-interfaces.md + agents/code-reviewer.md atomically: Stage-2 returns verdict + concern list only; gate-state block gains the fixed Verdict: APPROVED marker line
design:
## Goal
Both definition sites of the Stage-2 return contract state the slimmed payload (verdict line + concern list only) identically, and loop-interfaces defines the machine-checkable completion gate marker line.

## Why
The Stage-2 code-reviewer currently returns the full structured review, so the lead accumulates N review bodies per epic — contradicting the context-isolation principle (epic settled decision: verdict + concern list only). The two sites MUST change in this one task: splitting them creates a split-brain seam where the agent and the registry state different contracts (epic anti-pattern). The marker line is the producer side of the finishing gate Task 5 consumes.

## Context
Files: skills/common-patterns/loop-interfaces.md and agents/code-reviewer.md (read both whole).
- loop-interfaces.md "## Verdict Contracts" (lines 67-87 at v3.25.0): the Stage-2 entry reads "leading verdict line `PASS` or `CONCERNS: <summary>`, followed by the structured review." The third entry is labeled "**Completion reviewer → lead**".
- loop-interfaces.md "## Gate-State Block" (lines 8-30): format block with three sections (Where we are / Decided / Needs you); no machine-checkable verdict marker exists.
- agents/code-reviewer.md line 52 (final paragraph): "**Stage-2 verdict contract** … begin your final message with a leading verdict line — `PASS` or `CONCERNS: <one-line summary>` — then the structured review. Contract single-sourced in `skills/common-patterns/loop-interfaces.md` (Verdict Contracts)."
- The executor→lead entry (DONE-hash contract, lines 72-78) is bd-go7m's landed work — do not touch it.
- Marker line exact text (also cited by Tasks 3 and 5 — must match byte-for-byte): Verdict: APPROVED (end-of-epic reviewer, <date>)

## Implementation
1. loop-interfaces.md, Stage-2 entry → "…leading verdict line `PASS` or `CONCERNS: <one-line summary>`, followed by the concern list only — one line per concern: `<file>:<line> — <what and why>`. Never the full structured review: the lead's context must not accumulate per-task review bodies."
2. loop-interfaces.md, relabel the third entry "**Completion reviewer → lead**" → "**End-of-epic reviewer → lead (completion)**" so the vocabulary matches executing-plans and finishing prose. Content of that entry otherwise unchanged.
3. loop-interfaces.md, Gate-State Block: after the format block (and before "Gate questions must be answerable…"), add a short paragraph: "Completion gate-states — emitted when the end-of-epic reviewer returns APPROVED — MUST additionally include the fixed marker line `Verdict: APPROVED (end-of-epic reviewer, <date>)`. It is machine-checkable: finishing-a-development-branch greps the epic's bd notes for it before integrating."
4. agents/code-reviewer.md, final paragraph → "**Stage-2 verdict contract** (when dispatched by executing-plans for per-task review): your final message is the verdict line — `PASS` or `CONCERNS: <one-line summary>` — followed by the concern list only, one line per concern: `<file>:<line> — <what and why>`. Do NOT return the full structured review in this mode; the structured-output guidance above applies to other dispatch contexts only. Contract single-sourced in `skills/common-patterns/loop-interfaces.md` (Verdict Contracts)."
Commit the change when done.

## Verification
- `grep -n 'followed by the structured review' skills/common-patterns/loop-interfaces.md` → no matches
- `grep -n 'then the structured review' agents/code-reviewer.md` → no matches
- `grep -n 'concern list only' skills/common-patterns/loop-interfaces.md agents/code-reviewer.md` → ≥ 1 hit in each
- `grep -n 'Verdict: APPROVED (end-of-epic reviewer' skills/common-patterns/loop-interfaces.md` → 1
- `grep -ci 'end-of-epic reviewer' skills/common-patterns/loop-interfaces.md` → ≥ 2 (case-insensitive: the relabeled contract heading is capitalized "End-of-epic reviewer", while the marker paragraph's mentions are lowercase — a case-sensitive grep would see only 1)
- `git diff` shows NO change to the executor→lead entry (DONE/BLOCKED/NEEDS_HELP lines 72-78)

## Boundaries
Out of scope: the executor→lead verdict entry (bd-go7m's contract — byte-identical); any executing-plans or finishing edits (Tasks 1-3, 5 own those; they cite this marker); code-reviewer.md's numbered review methodology sections 1-6 (only the final Stage-2 paragraph changes); no new verdict vocabulary words; version bump (Task 6).

title: Create executor agent definition
design:
## Goal
Create agents/executor.md — the agent prompt that defines how the executor teammate behaves when spawned by the lead during /execute-plan.

## Why First
The executor agent definition is the foundation. The lead skill (executing-plans rewrite) and the reviewer agent both reference executor behavior. Starting here establishes the message protocol, TDD expectations, and escalation rules that everything else builds on.

## Effort Estimate
4-6 hours

## Implementation

### 1. Study existing agent patterns
Read these files to understand the agent definition format:
- agents/code-reviewer.md — frontmatter fields (name, description with examples, model, memory, skills), prompt structure
- agents/codebase-investigator.md — how agents receive context, how they report findings
- agents/test-runner.md — how agents report results back, permissionMode, disallowedTools

Note the frontmatter fields used: name, description (with XML examples), model, permissionMode, memory, skills, disallowedTools.

### 2. Study the executing-plans skill for behavior to port
Read skills/executing-plans/SKILL.md and extract:
- The resumption check pattern (Step 0) — how to find active epic and in-progress tasks
- The TDD cycle: write test → run RED → implement → run GREEN → refactor → commit
- How SRE refinement is invoked on new tasks (Skill tool call)
- How obstacles are handled (check Design Discovery, read anti-patterns)
- How next tasks are created (bd create with detailed design field)
- The "check TodoWrite before closing" pattern

### 3. Study the agent teams API
Understand available tools for the executor:
- SendMessage (type: "message", recipient: lead name) — for structured summaries
- bd CLI — for reading epic/task state (bd show, bd ready, bd update, bd create, bd close, bd dep add)
- Skill tool — for invoking TDD, SRE refinement skills
- Task tool — for dispatching test-runner subagent
- All standard tools (Read, Edit, Write, Bash, Grep, Glob)

### 4. Write the executor agent definition
Create agents/executor.md with these exact sections:

**Frontmatter (required fields):**
```yaml
---
name: executor
description: "Use this agent..." with XML examples showing lead spawning executor for epic execution
model: sonnet
permissionMode: bypassPermissions
memory: project
skills:
  - test-driven-development
  - verification-before-completion
  - sre-task-refinement
---
```

**Prompt body — Section a: Role**
- You are an executor agent spawned by a lead to implement bd tasks
- You work continuously through tasks, sending structured reports to your lead
- You follow TDD discipline (red-green-refactor-commit) for all implementation
- You never violate epic anti-patterns or requirements

**Prompt body — Section b: Startup Protocol**
1. Read the epic from bd: `bd show <epic-id>` (epic ID provided in spawn prompt)
2. Extract and internalize: Requirements (IMMUTABLE), Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN), Design Discovery
3. Read the current task: `bd show <task-id>` or `bd ready`
4. If in-progress task exists from previous run: resume it (don't create new)
5. Mark task in-progress: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`

**Prompt body — Section c: Execution Loop**
For each task:
1. Read task details and create TodoWrite for all substeps
2. Execute with TDD:
   - Write failing test (RED) — specific test for the task's deliverable
   - Run test via test-runner agent, confirm it fails
   - Implement minimal code to pass (GREEN)
   - Run test via test-runner agent, confirm it passes
   - Refactor if needed (keep tests green)
   - Commit with descriptive message
3. After task complete, verify all TodoWrite substeps done
4. Close task: `bd close <id>`
5. Run SRE refinement on proposed next task (invoke skill)
6. Send structured summary to lead (see Message Protocol below)
7. Wait for lead approval before creating next bd task and continuing

**Prompt body — Section d: Message Protocol**
Task completion message format (send via SendMessage to lead):
```
## Task <id> Complete

### Done
- [1-3 bullet summary of implementation]

### Learned
- [Discoveries that affect future tasks]
- [Things that differed from assumptions]

### Changed from plan
- [What deviated from task description and why]
- [Or "None — executed as planned"]

### Proposed next task
Title: [task title]
Goal: [what it delivers — one clear outcome]
Approach: [how, informed by learnings]
SRE refined: yes/no
```

Escalation message format:
```
## Escalation: [obstacle summary]

### Problem
[What's blocking — specific error, constraint, or design conflict]

### Epic context
[Which anti-pattern or requirement is relevant]
[Quote the specific text from epic Design Discovery]

### Options
A. [option] — [tradeoff, complexity estimate]
B. [option] — [tradeoff, complexity estimate]

### My recommendation
[Which option and why, referencing epic requirements]
```

**Prompt body — Section e: Escalation Triggers**
MUST escalate to lead when:
1. Implementation would require violating an epic anti-pattern
2. A library/API doesn't support what the design assumed
3. Discovery invalidates a rejected approach (the "DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS" condition might be met)
4. Task scope expands beyond what was approved
5. Test failures suggest a design flaw (not just a bug)
6. Two consecutive TDD cycles fail to go green

MUST NOT escalate (handle autonomously):
1. Normal test failures during RED phase (expected)
2. Refactoring that stays within task scope
3. Minor implementation details not specified in task
4. Choosing between equivalent approaches within anti-pattern bounds

**Prompt body — Section f: Completion Protocol**
When all epic success criteria appear met:
1. Re-read epic: `bd show <epic-id>`
2. Check each success criterion against implementation
3. Send completion message to lead:
```
## Epic Completion Report

### Success Criteria Status
- [x] Criterion 1 — [evidence]
- [x] Criterion 2 — [evidence]
...

### Summary
[2-3 sentence overview of entire implementation]

### Recommendation
Ready for review-implementation.
```

**Prompt body — Section g: Rules (No Exceptions)**
1. TDD is mandatory — never skip RED phase
2. Never violate epic anti-patterns — escalate instead
3. Never create next task without lead approval
4. Never skip SRE refinement on proposed tasks
5. Always use test-runner agent for test execution (context preservation)
6. Always send structured messages (never ad-hoc)
7. If context getting exhausted: send status to lead and request continuation guidance

### 5. Verify
- Read agents/executor.md back and confirm:
  - [ ] Frontmatter has all fields (name, description with examples, model, permissionMode, memory, skills)
  - [ ] Startup protocol specifies exact bd commands
  - [ ] TDD cycle has all 6 steps (write test, RED, implement, GREEN, refactor, commit)
  - [ ] Both message formats complete (task completion + escalation)
  - [ ] All 6 escalation triggers listed with concrete examples
  - [ ] All 4 "handle autonomously" cases listed
  - [ ] Completion protocol includes success criteria checklist
  - [ ] All 7 rules listed
  - [ ] No placeholder text ("[detailed above]", "[as specified]", etc.)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] agents/executor.md exists with complete frontmatter (name, description, model, permissionMode, memory, skills)
- [ ] Startup protocol specifies exact bd commands for epic and task loading
- [ ] TDD cycle explicitly defined with all 6 steps (write test, RED, implement, GREEN, refactor, commit)
- [ ] Task completion message format defined with all 4 sections (Done, Learned, Changed, Proposed next)
- [ ] Escalation message format defined with all 4 sections (Problem, Epic context, Options, Recommendation)
- [ ] 6 concrete escalation triggers listed (anti-pattern violation, API mismatch, rejected approach revisit, scope expansion, design flaw, consecutive failures)
- [ ] 4 "handle autonomously" cases listed
- [ ] Completion protocol includes success criteria checklist with evidence
- [ ] 7 rules with no exceptions listed
- [ ] No placeholder text in any section
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO placeholder instructions in agent prompt ("follow TDD" without specifying the cycle steps)
- ❌ NO undefined message fields (every field in protocol must have description and example)
- ❌ NO vague escalation triggers ("when things go wrong" — must be specific conditions)
- ❌ NO missing frontmatter fields (all fields from existing agents must be considered)
- ❌ NO "subagent_type" in frontmatter (that's a Task tool parameter, not an agent definition field)

## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**Edge Case: Epic not found in bd**
- Executor receives epic ID in spawn prompt
- If bd show returns error: send escalation to lead immediately
- Do NOT attempt to proceed without epic context

**Edge Case: Executor context exhaustion**
- Long-running tasks may exhaust executor context
- If approaching limits: send status message to lead with current progress
- Lead can shut down executor and spawn fresh one with state in bd

**Edge Case: Resumption from previous failed run**
- Check for in-progress tasks before starting fresh: `bd list --status in_progress`
- If found: resume that task (don't create duplicate)
- If task was partially implemented: assess state before continuing

**Edge Case: Lead doesn't respond**
- Executor sends proposal and waits
- If no response: executor is idle (this is normal in agent teams)
- Lead will respond when ready — do NOT resend or escalate about silence

**Edge Case: Skill invocation**
- Executor needs TDD and SRE refinement skills loaded via frontmatter
- If skill not available: send escalation to lead
- Do NOT attempt to follow skill from memory — must load via Skill tool

**Reference: Existing agent patterns to follow**
- agents/code-reviewer.md: frontmatter structure, XML examples in description
- agents/test-runner.md: permissionMode, reporting format, context isolation pattern

title: Phase 1: Skill Rules Configuration
design:
## Goal
Create skill-rules.json configuration defining triggers for all hyperpowers skills.

## Effort Estimate
6-8 hours

## Success Criteria
- [ ] skill-rules.json created with valid JSON (verified with jq . skill-rules.json)
- [ ] All hyperpowers skills have defined rules (19 skills confirmed)
- [ ] Each skill has 3-8 keywords and 2-5 intent patterns
- [ ] Priority levels appropriately assigned (max 3-4 critical skills)
- [ ] JSON validates successfully with jq
- [ ] No regex patterns fail catastrophic backtracking test
- [ ] Sample prompts match expected skills (90%+ accuracy on test set)
- [ ] File size under 50KB
- [ ] Documentation comments explain each pattern's purpose

## Implementation Steps (ADDED BY writing-plans)

### Step Group 1: Initial Setup and Skill Inventory

**Files:**
- Create: hooks/skill-rules.json
- Reference: skills/skills-auto-activation/resources/skill-rules-examples.md

**Step 1: Count and list all skills**

Run:
```bash
cd /Users/ryan/src/hyper
ls -1 skills/*/SKILL.md | wc -l
```
Expected: 19 skills

**Step 2: Extract all skill names for reference**

Run:
```bash
cd /Users/ryan/src/hyper
for skill in skills/*/SKILL.md; do 
  dirname "$skill" | sed 's|skills/||'
done | sort
```
Expected output: List of 19 skills (brainstorming through writing-skills)

### Step Group 2: Create Core Configuration Structure

**Step 3: Create initial skill-rules.json with schema documentation**

Create hooks/skill-rules.json:
```json
{
  "_comment": "Skill activation rules for hyperpowers plugin - 19 skills total",
  "_schema": {
    "description": "Each skill has type, enforcement, priority, and triggers",
    "type": "process|domain|workflow", 
    "enforcement": "suggest",
    "priority": "critical|high|medium|low",
    "promptTriggers": {
      "keywords": "Array of case-insensitive strings",
      "intentPatterns": "Array of regex patterns for action+object"
    }
  }
}
```

**Step 4: Verify JSON syntax**

Run:
```bash
cd /Users/ryan/src/hyper
jq . hooks/skill-rules.json
```
Expected: Valid JSON output with formatted structure

### Step Group 3: Add Core Workflow Skills (5 skills)

**Step 5: Add test-driven-development skill rules**

Add to hooks/skill-rules.json:
```json
"test-driven-development": {
  "type": "process",
  "enforcement": "suggest",
  "priority": "critical",
  "promptTriggers": {
    "keywords": ["test", "testing", "TDD", "spec", "unit test", "integration test", "test first", "red green refactor"],
    "intentPatterns": [
      "(write|add|create|implement).*?(test|spec|unit test)",
      "test.*(first|before|driven)",
      "(implement|build|create).*?(feature|function|component)",
      "red.*(green|refactor)",
      "(bug|fix|issue).*?reproduce"
    ]
  }
}
```

**Step 6: Add debugging-with-tools skill rules**

Add to hooks/skill-rules.json:
```json
"debugging-with-tools": {
  "type": "process",
  "enforcement": "suggest",
  "priority": "high",
  "promptTriggers": {
    "keywords": ["debug", "debugging", "error", "bug", "crash", "fails", "broken", "not working", "issue"],
    "intentPatterns": [
      "(debug|fix|solve|investigate|troubleshoot).*?(error|bug|issue|problem)",
      "(why|what).*?(failing|broken|not working|crashing)",
      "(find|locate|identify).*?(bug|issue|problem|root cause)",
      "reproduce.*(bug|issue|error)",
      "stack.*(trace|error)"
    ]
  }
}
```

**Step 7: Add refactoring-safely skill rules**

Add to hooks/skill-rules.json:
```json
"refactoring-safely": {
  "type": "process",
```

### Step Group 6: Test and Validate Configuration

**Step 16: Validate complete JSON file**

Run:
```bash
cd /Users/ryan/src/hyper
jq . hooks/skill-rules.json > /dev/null && echo "✓ Valid JSON" || echo "✗ Invalid JSON"
```
Expected: ✓ Valid JSON

**Step 17: Check file size**

Run:
```bash
ls -lh hooks/skill-rules.json | awk '{print $5}'
```
Expected: < 50KB (should be around 15-20KB)

**Step 18: Count skills in configuration**

Run:
```bash
jq 'keys - ["_comment", "_schema"] | length' hooks/skill-rules.json
```
Expected: 19

**Step 19: Test keyword matching with sample prompts**

Create test script:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Test skill activation patterns
prompt="I want to write a test for the login function"
jq --arg prompt "$prompt" '
  to_entries | 
  map(select(.value.promptTriggers.keywords as $kw | 
    $prompt | ascii_downcase | 
    test($kw | map(. | ascii_downcase) | join("|"))
  )) | 
  map(.key)
' hooks/skill-rules.json
```
Expected: Should match "test-driven-development"

**Step 20: Commit the configuration**

Run:
```bash
git add hooks/skill-rules.json
git commit -m "feat(bd-2): create skill-rules.json configuration

Implements bd-2: Skill Rules Configuration
- Defines activation rules for all 19 hyperpowers skills
- Includes keywords and intent patterns for each skill
- Sets appropriate priority levels (critical/high/medium/low)
- Validated JSON syntax and file size < 50KB"
```

### Step Group 7: Document Pattern Testing

**Step 21: Create regex testing documentation**

Create hooks/REGEX_TESTING.md:
```markdown
# Regex Pattern Testing for skill-rules.json

## Tested Patterns

All regex patterns in skill-rules.json have been tested on regex101.com for:
- Catastrophic backtracking (10000 'a's input)
- Unicode handling
- Performance

### Test Results

| Pattern | Backtracking Safe | Unicode Safe | Avg Time |
|---------|------------------|--------------|----------|
| (create|add).*?(feature|component) | ✓ | ✓ | <1ms |
| (debug|fix).*?(error|bug) | ✓ | ✓ | <1ms |
[... document all patterns ...]
```

**Step 22: Final validation**

Run:
```bash
cd /Users/ryan/src/hyper
echo "=== Skill Rules Validation ==="
echo "1. JSON valid: $(jq . hooks/skill-rules.json > /dev/null 2>&1 && echo '✓' || echo '✗')"
echo "2. Skill count: $(jq 'keys - ["_comment", "_schema"] | length' hooks/skill-rules.json) (expected: 19)"
echo "3. File size: $(ls -lh hooks/skill-rules.json | awk '{print $5}')"
```
Expected: All validations pass

## Key Considerations (ADDED BY SRE REVIEW)
- Pattern Design: Intent patterns should be broad enough to catch variations but specific enough to avoid false positives
- Priority Balance: Don't overuse "critical" - reserve for verification and safety-critical workflows
- Regex Performance: Keep patterns simple; avoid catastrophic backtracking
- Maintenance: Document reasoning for each pattern to help future updates
- File Size: Large JSON files can slow parsing - keep under 50KB
- Testing Regex: MUST test all patterns on regex101.com with pathological inputs (10000 'a's)
- Unicode Handling: Patterns should handle Unicode input gracefully
- Case Sensitivity: All matching must be case-insensitive

## Anti-patterns to Avoid
- ❌ Overly broad keywords that match too many prompts (e.g., "code", "file", "data")
- ❌ Complex regex patterns that are hard to maintain or have backtracking issues
- ❌ Marking too many skills as "critical" priority (dilutes importance)
- ❌ Missing common variations in intent patterns
- ❌ Untested regex patterns (MUST test on regex101.com)
- ❌ No comments explaining pattern intent
- ❌ File size over 50KB (performance impact)
