title: Epic: Outer-Loop Architecture Skills (Decomposition + Audit)
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)

1. Two new skills: volatility-decomposition (Socratic, generative) and architectural-audit (analytical, diagnostic)
2. Decomposition skill uses Löwy's volatility-based decomposition: identifies independent axes of change, encapsulates each behind stable interfaces, assigns Löwy layers (Manager/Engine/Resource Accessor/Utility)
3. Decomposition skill presents 2-3 alternative decompositions with tradeoffs — NEVER a single recommendation
4. Audit skill uses Hickey's simplicity principles: finds complection, hidden coupling, temporal coupling, layer violations, state/identity conflation
5. Audit skill surfaces tensions WITHOUT resolving them — presents both pulls, states assumptions that make each option correct, NO recommendations
6. Both skills encode the architecture graph in bd: architecture epic + component nodes (type: feature, labels: arch,component), using state dimensions for stability tracking, graph links for relationships
7. The graph is a TEMPORARY work plan (Path B) — created, consumed by inner loop, closed when done. NOT a permanent living model
8. Architectural decisions captured as ADRs in repo (doc/arch/adr-NNN.md) — permanent intent that outlives the graph
9. Both skills dispatch codebase-investigator when a codebase exists; work in pure design mode when no codebase exists (greenfield)
10. Brainstorming skill updated to auto-detect architecture nodes and load their context (volatility axis, interface contract, relevant ADRs, adjacent nodes)
11. Scale-agnostic: skills work at any abstraction level (modules, services, layers). Vocabulary adapts to context
12. Fractal decomposition: oversized components get sub-decomposed by running /decompose on the component itself
13. Skills think in components and boundaries, NOT classes and functions — if they descend to implementation details they've crossed into inner-loop territory

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)

- [ ] volatility-decomposition skill exists at skills/volatility-decomposition/SKILL.md with full skill structure (frontmatter, overview, process, examples, critical rules, verification checklist, integration)
- [ ] architectural-audit skill exists at skills/architectural-audit/SKILL.md with full skill structure
- [ ] Commands exist: commands/decompose.md, commands/audit-arch.md
- [ ] Decomposition skill produces graph encoded in bd (epic + component nodes with labels, state dimensions, dependencies)
- [ ] Decomposition skill creates ADRs for key decomposition decisions in doc/arch/ format
- [ ] Audit skill produces structured tension report with the exact format: tension name, components, both pulls (gain/cost), conditional assumptions
- [ ] Audit skill reads existing ADRs to skip already-accepted tensions
- [ ] Audit skill detects drift between ADRs and codebase
- [ ] Audit skill self-check step reviews output for accidental recommendations before presenting
- [ ] Brainstorming skill updated to detect arch node references and auto-load context
- [ ] ADR template follows Nygard format: Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences
- [ ] Both skills dispatch codebase-investigator when codebase exists
- [ ] Decomposition skill applies Löwy's framework: volatility axes, independence test, layer assignment, layer rules
- [ ] Audit skill applies Hickey's framework: complection scan, interface analysis, temporal coupling, hidden dependencies, layer violations, state/identity conflation
- [ ] All skill files follow existing hyperpowers skill structure pattern
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)

- ❌ NO single recommendations in decomposition (requirement 3: always 2-3 alternatives with tradeoffs)
- ❌ NO resolving tensions in audit output (requirement 5: surface tensions, present both pulls, NO 'however I think...')
- ❌ NO severity rankings in audit output (implies recommendation — use structural observations like 'affects N nodes' instead)
- ❌ NO implementation-level detail in either skill (requirement 13: components and boundaries only, not classes and functions)
- ❌ NO treating the graph as a permanent living model (requirement 7: graph is temporary work plan, ADRs are permanent intent)
- ❌ NO maintaining accepted tradeoffs in bd notes (requirement 8: accepted decisions go in ADRs in the repo)
- ❌ NO skipping codebase investigation when codebase exists (requirement 9: always dispatch codebase-investigator)
- ❌ NO prescribing cycle orchestration (the architect drives the decompose↔audit cycle with judgment, not a third orchestration skill)
- ❌ NO creating full task trees upfront (first task only, iterative task creation as learning happens)

## Approach

Two new skills forming a slow outer loop above the existing brainstorm→plan→execute inner loop. The decomposition skill (Socratic) helps the architect articulate volatility axes and propose component boundaries using Löwy's method. The audit skill (analytical) examines the resulting graph (and codebase if available) for complection using Hickey's simplicity framework.

The graph is encoded in bd as a temporary work plan: architecture epic + component feature nodes with labels, state dimensions (stability tracking), and graph links (blocks, relates_to). When nodes are stable (all tensions resolved or accepted), they're eligible for inner-loop handoff via /brainstorm.

Architectural decisions from decomposition choices and tension resolutions are captured as ADRs in the repo (doc/arch/adr-NNN.md), providing permanent intent that survives the graph's lifecycle.

The brainstorming skill is updated to auto-detect when the architect references an architecture node, loading its design context (volatility axis, interface contract, relevant ADRs, adjacent nodes) to give the inner loop a head start.

## Architecture

### Deliverables
- skills/volatility-decomposition/SKILL.md — decomposition skill
- skills/architectural-audit/SKILL.md — audit skill
- commands/decompose.md — slash command
- commands/audit-arch.md — slash command
- skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md — updated with arch node detection
- skills/common-patterns/adr-template.md — ADR format reference

### Skill Integration Map
```
OUTER LOOP (occasional):
  /decompose ←→ /audit-arch ←→ architect resolves
       ↓                              ↓
  graph (bd, temporary)         ADRs (repo, permanent)

HANDOFF (stable node):
  stable node → /brainstorm (auto-loads arch context + ADRs)

INNER LOOP (daily, unchanged):
  brainstorming → sre-refinement → writing-plans → executing-plans → review
```

### bd Encoding
- Architecture epic: type epic, labels: arch
- Component nodes: type feature, labels: arch,component, parent: arch epic
- State dimensions: stability (exploring|audited|accepted|stable)
- Edges: blocks (interface dependencies), relates_to (non-blocking interactions)
- Version evolution: supersedes link for major restructuring

### Agent Usage
- Both skills: codebase-investigator (when codebase exists)
- Decomposition: internet-researcher (when evaluating technology options)
- Audit: NO internet research (focuses on graph + codebase evidence)

## Design Rationale

### Problem
The architectural design phase before the inner loop is ad hoc. The user runs informal prompts ('do an architecture review through the lens of Rich Hickey') and gets valuable results, but can't systematize it because it's actually two distinct cognitive activities complected together: generative decomposition (structural, divergent) and evaluative auditing (diagnostic, convergent). Separating them enables each to be done properly.

### Research Findings

**Codebase (hyperpowers plugin):**
- Skills follow consistent structure: frontmatter, overview, rigidity level, process, examples, critical rules, verification checklist, integration
- Skills chain via bd artifacts — epics as contracts, tasks as adaptive execution units
- Brainstorming skill already dispatches codebase-investigator and internet-researcher
- Existing skill integration: brainstorming → sre-refinement → writing-plans → executing-plans
- Agent types: codebase-investigator, internet-researcher, executor, reviewer, test-runner

**bd/beads capabilities:**
- Issue types: epic, task, bug, feature
- Labels system: multi-dimensional tagging, AND/OR filtering (--label, --label-any)
- State dimensions: bd set-state <id> <dimension>=<value> --reason '...' — arbitrary per-issue state
- Graph links: blocks, parent-child, relates_to (bidirectional), supersedes (version chains), duplicates, replies_to
- Molecules: epic + children with workflow semantics (DAGs with execution ordering). Bonding connects separate graphs
- Hierarchical IDs: bd-a3f8 → bd-a3f8.1 → bd-a3f8.1.1
- Rich fields: design, notes, acceptance, description, spec-id, external-ref
- Query: --label, --label-any, --title-contains, --desc-contains, --notes-contains, date ranges, priority ranges

**Löwy's volatility-based decomposition (Righting Software):**
- Identify independent axes of change, encapsulate each behind stable interface
- Two axes: same customer over time, different customers simultaneously — must be independent
- Layer hierarchy: Manager (orchestration) → Engine (business logic) → Resource Accessor (data) → Utility (cross-cutting)
- Strict top-to-bottom flow: no upward dependencies
- Key distinction: volatility (architectural) vs variability (code-level) — only volatility drives decomposition
- Common failure: confusing volatility with variability (over-engineering), non-independent axes (still functional decomposition)

**Hickey's simplicity principles (Simple Made Easy, Are We There Yet?):**
- Simple (objective: not braided together) ≠ Easy (subjective: familiar/nearby)
- Complecting: interleaving concerns that should be independent
- Objects complect state+identity+value. Mutable state complects value+time
- Systematic separation: What/Who/When/Where/Why/How as independent concerns
- Types/tests insufficient: 'every bug passed the type checker.' Only simplicity enables reasoning
- Hammock-driven development: think deeply before coding. Background mind solves non-trivial problems

**ADRs (Michael Nygard, Cognitect ecosystem):**
- Format: Title, Status (proposed/accepted/deprecated/superseded), Context, Decision ('We will...'), Consequences
- Stored in repo: doc/arch/adr-NNN.md. Sequential numbering, never reuse numbers
- Consequences of one ADR become context for subsequent ADRs
- Captures 'architecturally significant' decisions affecting structure, dependencies, interfaces
- Superseded ADRs remain for historical context

### Approaches Considered

#### 1. Path B: Graph as temporary work plan + ADRs ✓

**What it is:** The architecture graph lives in bd as a temporary work plan — created during decomposition, consumed by the inner loop, closed when done. Architectural decisions (decomposition choices, tension resolutions, tension acceptances) captured as ADRs in the repo for permanent intent.

**Investigation:**
- Analyzed how user actually works: ad hoc architecture sessions, then inner-loop execution
- Researched bd capabilities: labels, state dimensions, graph links provide rich encoding
- Researched ADR format: lightweight, permanent, version-controlled, well-understood

**Pros:**
- Zero maintenance burden: no model to keep in sync with reality
- Zero drift risk: codebase is always source of truth
- ADRs provide permanent intent without permanent model
- Audit can detect drift: compare ADRs (declared intent) with codebase (reality)
- Cross-cutting features don't need the graph — just use normal inner loop

**Cons:**
- Architecture rationale distributed across ADRs rather than in one place
- When re-decomposing later, must reconstruct context from ADRs + codebase

**Chosen because:** Matches user's actual workflow. Eliminates the map-vs-territory problem entirely. ADRs preserve decisions without maintaining a model.

#### 2. Path A: Graph as living model ❌

**What it is:** The architecture graph persists permanently in bd. Architecture nodes stay open even after implementation. Cross-cutting features reference the graph for constraints. Periodic reconciliation keeps graph aligned with codebase.

**Why we looked at this:** Natural first instinct — if you have an architecture model, keep it current.

**Investigation:**
- Analyzed maintenance burden: graph must be updated as system evolves
- Identified drift risk: model inevitably diverges from codebase over time
- Considered cross-cutting features: graph doesn't naturally accommodate them

**Pros:**
- Architecture visible as a whole at any time
- Cross-cutting features get constraint guidance from graph

**Cons:**
- Maintenance burden: another artifact to keep in sync
- Drift risk: model vs reality divergence
- Cross-cutting features don't fit neatly into graph nodes

**⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE:** User's actual workflow is Path B — graph is created for structural work, consumed, then done. Maintaining a permanent model adds overhead without proportionate value. ADRs capture intent more efficiently.

**🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** The team grows large enough that a living architecture model becomes essential for coordination (5+ concurrent developers).

#### 3. Cycle orchestrator (third skill) ❌

**What it is:** A thin orchestration skill that manages the decompose→audit→resolve cycle, prompting the architect through each phase.

**Why we looked at this:** The cycle could benefit from guided sequencing.

**Investigation:**
- The cycle is inherently non-linear — architect might audit without decomposing, or decompose without auditing first
- Adding ceremony reduces the judgment-driven nature of the outer loop

**⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE:** Over-engineering. The architect's judgment drives the cycle, not a process. Two skills is sufficient.

**🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Users consistently forget to audit after decomposing.

#### 4. Single skill with modes ❌

**What it is:** One 'architecture' skill with decompose and audit modes that Claude switches between.

**Why we looked at this:** Avoids loading two separate skills for a tightly coupled cycle.

**⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE:** User explicitly identified decomposition (generative) and audit (evaluative) as distinct cognitive activities that get complected when mixed. Separate skills enforce the separation.

**🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** The skills prove so tightly coupled in practice that the separation creates friction.

### Scope Boundaries

**In scope:**
- Volatility-decomposition skill (SKILL.md + command)
- Architectural-audit skill (SKILL.md + command)
- Brainstorming skill update (arch node auto-detection)
- ADR template in common-patterns
- using-hyper trigger guidance for architecture-related requests

**Out of scope (deferred):**
- Automatic ADR generation (skills guide the architect, architect writes the ADR content)
- Molecule integration (architecture graph uses plain epic+children, not molecules)
- Visualization tooling (bd dep tree is sufficient for now)
- Multi-repo architecture graphs (single repo only)

### Open Questions
- Should the decomposition skill offer to draft ADR content, or only prompt the architect to create them? (Decide during implementation — start with prompting, can add drafting later)
- How should the brainstorming skill detect an arch node reference? By bd ID mention? By label query? By asking? (Decide during implementation)
- Should accepted tradeoffs in ADRs flow automatically into inner-loop epic anti-patterns, or should the architect curate? (Decide during implementation — start with manual curation)

## Design Discovery (Reference Context)

> Detailed context from brainstorming for task creation and obstacle handling.

### Key Decisions Made

| Question | User Answer | Implication |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Graph persistence | Encode in bd | Use labels, state dims, graph links for rich encoding |
| Interaction mode | Decomposition Socratic, Audit analytical | Different skill structures and processes |
| Abstraction level | Scale-agnostic | Skills adapt vocabulary to context |
| Handoff gate | All tensions resolved or accepted | Formal gate: audit must pass clean |
| Resolution flow | Architect modifies graph directly | Tensions ephemeral, ADRs permanent |
| BD encoding | Issue-per-node fits well | Architecture epic + component feature nodes |
| Codebase access | Yes, when codebase exists | Both skills dispatch codebase-investigator |
| Brainstorm integration | Auto-detect and pull context | Brainstorming skill update required |
| Graph lifecycle | Path B (temporary work plan) | Graph created → consumed → closed |
| Component sizing | Fractal decomposition | Big components sub-decomposed recursively |
| Architecture intent | ADRs in repo (Nygard format) | Permanent decisions at doc/arch/adr-NNN.md |
| Cross-cutting features | Normal inner loop | No graph needed for UX enhancements etc |

### Research Deep-Dives

#### bd Capabilities
**Question explored:** What can bd actually represent for architecture graphs?
**Sources consulted:**
- github.com/steveyegge/beads README, CLI_REFERENCE.md, LABELS.md, MOLECULES.md, graph-links.md
**Key findings:**
- Labels: multi-dimensional tagging with AND/OR filtering
- State dimensions: arbitrary per-issue state tracking with reasons
- Graph links: blocks, parent-child, relates_to, supersedes, duplicates, replies_to
- Molecules: execution-level DAGs (not right fit for design-level architecture)
- Hierarchical IDs: native hierarchy support
**Conclusion:** bd's primitives are rich enough for architecture graph encoding without custom extensions

#### Löwy's Method
**Question explored:** How does volatility-based decomposition work in practice?
**Sources consulted:**
- Righting Software references, SE Radio 407 interview, InformIT articles, InfoQ Q&A
**Key findings:**
- Two independent axes: same customer over time, different customers simultaneously
- Manager/Engine/Resource Accessor/Utility layering with strict top-to-bottom flow
- Key failure mode: confusing volatility with variability
- Volatility drives architecture AND roadmap simultaneously
**Conclusion:** Framework provides concrete heuristics for Socratic questioning in decomposition skill

#### Hickey's Framework
**Question explored:** What specific heuristics detect complection?
**Sources consulted:**
- Simple Made Easy transcript, Are We There Yet? transcript, Hammock-Driven Development
**Key findings:**
- Six systematic analysis passes: complection scan, interface analysis, temporal coupling, hidden dependencies, layer violations, state/identity conflation
- Simple ≠ Easy distinction is the core diagnostic lens
- Hammock-driven: think before coding (background mind solves hard problems)
**Conclusion:** Framework provides concrete analysis categories for audit skill

#### ADRs
**Question explored:** How to preserve architecture intent without maintaining a living model?
**Sources consulted:**
- Cognitect blog (Nygard 2011), various ADR implementations
**Key findings:**
- Lightweight: 1-2 pages, stored in repo, version-controlled
- Format: Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences
- Consequences of one ADR become context for next
- Superseded ADRs remain for historical context
**Conclusion:** ADRs solve the intent preservation problem for Path B (temporary graphs)

### Dead-End Paths

#### Graph as living model (Path A)
**Why explored:** Natural first instinct — maintain architecture model permanently
**Investigation:** Analyzed maintenance burden, drift risk, cross-cutting feature awkwardness
**Why abandoned:** User's actual workflow is temporary graphs. Permanent model adds overhead without proportionate value.

#### Cycle orchestrator skill
**Why explored:** Could guide the decompose→audit→resolve sequence
**Investigation:** Cycle is non-linear, judgment-driven. Orchestrator adds ceremony without value.
**Why abandoned:** Over-engineering. Two skills + architect judgment is sufficient.

#### Molecules for architecture graph
**Why explored:** bd's molecule feature provides DAG semantics
**Investigation:** Molecules are execution-level (workflow ordering, parallel detection). Architecture graph is design-level.
**Why abandoned:** Semantic mismatch. Plain epic+children with labels/state/links is better fit.

### Open Concerns Raised

- 'Cross-cutting features don't fit single nodes' → Resolved: Path B — graph is temporary work plan. Cross-cutting features use normal inner loop. Graph provides constraint context when it exists, codebase-investigator discovers structure when it doesn't.
- 'Component too big for single epic' → Resolved: Fractal decomposition — run /decompose on the component itself. Sub-components via hierarchical IDs.
- 'Graph becomes model vs looking at system' → Resolved: Path B + ADRs. Graph is ephemeral tool. ADRs capture decisions. Codebase is truth.
- 'Audit skill will accidentally recommend' → Addressed: Rigid tension format with no recommendation field, anti-patterns, self-check step. Key engineering challenge during implementation.
- 'How do accepted tradeoffs persist?' → Resolved: ADRs in repo, not bd notes. Audit reads ADRs to skip accepted tensions.
close_reason: All deliverables complete. Reviewer approved. 3 tasks across 3 commits: volatility-decomposition skill, architectural-audit skill, brainstorming update, ADR template, commands.

title: Task 2: executing-plans completion — reviewer gate + architecture check + explicit STOP

## Goal
executing-plans' completion section owns the full end-of-epic gate: reviewer dispatch (existing), post-build Architecture Impact Check (new, cited), explicit manual-validation STOP (ported), plan-impact emission (ported).

## Why
This is the resolution of review finding F1: the mainline currently hands APPROVED straight to finish-branch, silently dropping the architecture-model loop and the manual-validation pause that two other files declare mandatory. R1 of bd-iaem.

## Context
skills/executing-plans/SKILL.md Section 6 "Completion" L224-247: L226-230 zero-open-tasks check; L232-241 reviewer dispatch block; L245 APPROVED branch (persist gate-state -> present -> "Hand off to /hyperpowers:finish-branch"); L247 GAPS FOUND gap-fix loop. Also: Flow line L356 ends "Reviewer gate -> Present -> /hyperpowers:finish-branch"; verification checklist L345-347; when-stuck L362. Content to port from skills/review-implementation/SKILL.md: STOP semantics L595-608 (esp. L601 "STOP here. Do not automatically call finishing-a-development-branch." and L603-608 why-we-stop), sibling-divergence plan-impact line L619.

## Implementation
1. Rewrite the APPROVED branch (L245) into ordered sub-steps: (a) persist completion gate-state incl. plan-impact notices (as today); (b) run the post-build Architecture Impact Check per skills/common-patterns/architecture-impact-check.md — cite, never restate the questions; any YES routes per that file's Post-Build Routing (ponder UPDATE when a model exists, note-and-suggest when not); (c) present final status; (d) STOP for manual validation — port L601/L603-608 substance verbatim-in-strength: do not auto-call finishing-a-development-branch; the user explicitly triggers closure when validated; (e) close with "user runs /hyperpowers:finish-branch when ready"
2. Port the L619 sibling-divergence rule: if the review reveals divergence from an upstream shared plan, emit a PLAN-IMPACT notice (format: skills/common-patterns/loop-interfaces.md) to the epic's bd notes
3. Update Flow line (L356) to "... -> Reviewer gate -> Architecture check -> Present + STOP (manual validation) -> user -> /hyperpowers:finish-branch"; update the completion rows of the verification checklist (L345-347) and when-stuck (L362) to match

## Tests
None because this is a skill doc; verification is by grep and cross-file consistency.

## Verification
- [ ] Completion section contains the explicit STOP instruction (no auto finish-branch) and the architecture-impact-check.md citation; the 5 questions are NOT restated in this file
- [ ] GAPS FOUND branch (L247) byte-unchanged in substance; sections 1-5 untouched

No changes to sections 1-5 (per-task two-stage loop is ADR-003 guard territory — frozen). No verdict vocabulary changes. STOP wording must be no weaker than the ported original.

title: Task 1: Add Parallelism Map section to brainstorming skill
design:
## Goal
Modify the brainstorming skill to include a Parallelism Map section in the epic template. This is the entry point for the entire team workflow - without the Parallelism Map, wave-planning and team-executing can't identify what to parallelize.

## Context
The brainstorming skill (skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md) creates bd epics with immutable requirements, success criteria, anti-patterns, approach, architecture, and design discovery sections. We need to add a Parallelism Map section that identifies independent work streams, file ownership boundaries, and suggested wave composition.

## Effort Estimate
2-4 hours (single file modification, 4-5 discrete insertion points)

## Implementation

### Step 1: Read the current brainstorming skill
- File: skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md
- Focus on Section 4 (Creating the bd Epic) — the epic template starting at line 181
- Identify exact insertion points for all changes below

### Step 2: Add Parallelism Map section to the epic template
**Insertion point:** Inside the `bd create` command block in Section 4, after the `## Architecture` section (around line 209) and before the `## Design Rationale` section (around line 211).

Add the following verbatim inside the bd create template:

```markdown
## Parallelism Map

### Independent Work Streams
1. **[Stream Name]** ([file/module scope])
   - [What this stream delivers]
   - Estimated complexity: [low/medium/high]
2. **[Stream Name]** ([file/module scope])
   - [What this stream delivers]
   - Estimated complexity: [low/medium/high]

### Stream Dependencies
- Stream N depends on Stream M (reason: [why])
- Streams X and Y are fully independent

### Suggested Waves
- Wave 1: [Streams that can parallelize] (independent, no shared files)
- Wave 2: [Streams that depend on Wave 1]
- Wave 3: [Streams that depend on Wave 2]

### File Ownership Boundaries
| Stream | Owns (exclusive) | Shared (needs coordination) |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------------------|
| [Name] | [paths]           | [paths + which other stream] |

### Parallelism Assessment
- Independent streams: [N]
- Recommendation: [team execution / solo execution]
- Rationale: [why — e.g., "5 independent streams with no shared files" or "only 2 streams, coordination overhead not worthwhile"]
```

**Important:** Use "Estimated complexity" (low/medium/high) instead of hour estimates — brainstorming happens before detailed planning, so hour estimates would be premature and inaccurate.

**Important:** Include "Shared (needs coordination)" column in File Ownership — some streams will share files. Rather than pretending all files can be cleanly owned, acknowledge shared files and note which streams need to coordinate. Shared files become blocking dependencies or separate coordination tasks during wave-planning.

### Step 3: Add parallelism analysis guidance to Section 2 (Exploring Approaches)
**Insertion point:** After the existing "CAPTURE for Design Discovery" block at lines 122-132, add a new subsection.

Add the following text:

```markdown
**CAPTURE for Parallelism Map:**
During codebase research, also identify:
- Module boundaries: Which directories/files form independent units?
- Shared state: What files, databases, or APIs are touched by multiple features?
- Natural work streams: Could different developers work on different parts without stepping on each other?
- These observations inform the Parallelism Map section in the epic.
```

This is a lightweight addition — just 4 bullet points to prime the researcher to notice parallelism opportunities.

### Step 4: Add team vs. solo decision point to Section 6 (Handoff)
**Insertion point:** In Section 6 (SRE Refinement and Handoff, starting at line 382), after the "After refinement approved, present handoff:" block (around line 400), add a decision point BEFORE the existing handoff announcement.

Add the following:

```markdown
**REQUIRED: Team vs. Solo Decision**

Review the epic's Parallelism Map and present a routing decision:

If 3+ independent streams with no shared files:
→ Recommend team execution path (wave-planning → team-executing-plans)

If <3 independent streams OR significant shared files:
→ Recommend solo execution path (writing-plans → executing-plans)

Use AskUserQuestion:
```
AskUserQuestion:
  question: "Based on the Parallelism Map, this epic has [N] independent streams. Which execution path should we use?"
  header: "Execution"
  options:
    - label: "[Team/Solo] execution (Recommended)"
      description: "[Reason based on stream count and file ownership analysis]"
    - label: "[Solo/Team] execution"
      description: "[Tradeoff — e.g., 'Lower parallelism but simpler coordination' or 'More parallel but higher token cost']"
```

Then adjust the handoff text to reflect the chosen path.
```

**Edge case:** If the Parallelism Map shows exactly 0 independent streams (everything is sequential), skip the decision point entirely and default to solo execution. Don't ask the user an obvious question.

### Step 5: Update the verification checklist
**Insertion point:** In the verification_checklist section (starting at line 836), add after the existing checklist items (before the "Can't check all boxes?" line):

Add these items:
```markdown
- [ ] Parallelism Map section present with: streams, dependencies, waves, file ownership, assessment
- [ ] Parallelism Map assessment recommends team or solo path with rationale
- [ ] Team vs. solo decision point presented to user (if 3+ streams)
```

### Step 6: Add a realistic example
**Insertion point:** In the existing example section (examples block starting at line 419). Add a new example after the existing examples that demonstrates a Parallelism Map in context.

The example should:
- Use the OAuth authentication scenario already in the skill (for consistency)
- Show a Parallelism Map with 3 streams (enough for team recommendation)
- Include one shared file to demonstrate the "Shared (needs coordination)" column
- Show the team vs. solo AskUserQuestion flow
- Be concise (not a full epic, just the Parallelism Map portion)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Brainstorming skill epic template includes Parallelism Map section inside the bd create command block
- [ ] Parallelism Map has all subsections: independent streams, stream dependencies, suggested waves, file ownership boundaries, parallelism assessment
- [ ] File Ownership table includes "Shared (needs coordination)" column for shared files
- [ ] Uses "Estimated complexity" (low/medium/high) not hour estimates for streams
- [ ] Section 2 guidance added (4 bullet points after "CAPTURE for Design Discovery" block)
- [ ] Section 6 handoff includes team vs. solo decision point with AskUserQuestion
- [ ] Decision point skips AskUserQuestion when 0 independent streams (obvious solo path)
- [ ] Verification checklist includes 3 new Parallelism Map items
- [ ] Example demonstrates realistic Parallelism Map with shared file scenario
- [ ] No changes to existing epic sections (requirements, success criteria, anti-patterns, approach, design rationale)
- [ ] Existing brainstorming skill still works for single-stream projects (Parallelism Map section shows 1 stream, assessment says "solo execution", no team decision presented)

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO making Parallelism Map mandatory for solo-only projects (assessment should say "solo execution" when <3 streams, not force team workflow)
- ❌ NO modifying existing epic template sections (requirements, success criteria, anti-patterns, approach, architecture, design rationale) — Parallelism Map is ADDITIVE
- ❌ NO breaking the bd create command block structure (Parallelism Map goes inside the existing template, not as a separate command)
- ❌ NO hour-based effort estimates in streams (brainstorming is too early for hour estimates; use complexity: low/medium/high)
- ❌ NO pretending all files can be cleanly owned (shared files exist; acknowledge them in the table)
- ❌ NO asking team vs solo when answer is obvious (<3 streams = solo, don't waste user's time)

## Key Considerations (ADDED BY SRE REVIEW)

### Edge Case: Single-Stream Projects
When brainstorming produces an epic with only 1 work stream (e.g., "fix a single bug" or "refactor one module"), the Parallelism Map should still be present but trivially filled: 1 stream, 0 dependencies, 1 wave, assessment says "solo execution recommended." Do NOT skip the section — its presence signals the analysis was done.

### Edge Case: Shared Files Between Streams
Some streams will share files (e.g., two features both modify routes/index.ts). The File Ownership table MUST have a "Shared (needs coordination)" column. During wave-planning, shared files become either: (a) a separate coordination task that blocks both streams, or (b) a reason to sequence streams into different waves. The brainstorming skill doesn't need to solve this — just surface the information.

### Edge Case: Architecture Section Overlap
The Architecture section already lists components. The Parallelism Map adds work stream groupings. These might seem redundant. They serve different purposes: Architecture = what the system looks like, Parallelism Map = how work can be divided. Architecture might list "auth/, routes/, db/" while Parallelism Map groups them as "Stream 1: auth + routes, Stream 2: db migrations." Clarify this distinction in the skill if needed.

### Edge Case: Epic Size and Context Compaction
Adding the Parallelism Map makes epics larger. Keep the template concise — each stream should be 2-3 lines max. The assessment subsection is 2-3 lines. Total Parallelism Map overhead: ~20-30 lines. This is acceptable.

### Edge Case: Handoff Decision Timing
The team vs. solo decision happens in Section 6, AFTER the epic is created and AFTER SRE refinement. This is correct — the decision uses the Parallelism Map that's already in the epic. The decision doesn't change the epic; it routes to either writing-plans (solo) or wave-planning (team).
close_reason: All 11 success criteria verified. Parallelism Map section added to epic template (streams, dependencies, waves, file ownership, assessment). Section 2 guidance added. Section 6 team vs solo decision point with AskUserQuestion. Verification checklist updated. Realistic example with shared file scenario. Integration section updated with team path.

title: Phase 7: Integration Testing
design:
## Goal
Test complete hook system integration.

## Effort Estimate  
4-6 hours

## Success Criteria
- [ ] End-to-end test script created
- [ ] All 3 hooks tested in sequence
- [ ] 10+ test scenarios pass
- [ ] Performance benchmarks documented
- [ ] Error scenarios handled correctly
- [ ] No interference between hooks
- [ ] Clean test environment setup/teardown

## Implementation Steps (ADDED BY writing-plans, REFINED BY SRE)

### Step 1: Create integration test script

Create \`hooks/test/integration-test.sh\`:

\`\`\`bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Colors for output
RED='\\033[0;31m'
GREEN='\\033[0;32m'
YELLOW='\\033[1;33m'
NC='\\033[0m' # No Color

# Test environment setup
TEST_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
HOOKS_DIR="$(dirname "$TEST_DIR")"
CONTEXT_DIR="$HOOKS_DIR/context"
ORIG_LOG=""

# Test counters
TESTS_RUN=0
TESTS_PASSED=0
TESTS_FAILED=0

# Utility functions
setup_test() {
    echo -e "${YELLOW}Setting up test environment...${NC}"
    # Backup existing log
    if [ -f "$CONTEXT_DIR/edit-log.txt" ]; then
        ORIG_LOG=$(cat "$CONTEXT_DIR/edit-log.txt")
    fi
    > "$CONTEXT_DIR/edit-log.txt"
    export DEBUG_HOOKS=false
}

teardown_test() {
    echo -e "${YELLOW}Cleaning up test environment...${NC}"
    # Restore original log
    if [ -n "$ORIG_LOG" ]; then
        echo "$ORIG_LOG" > "$CONTEXT_DIR/edit-log.txt"
    else
        > "$CONTEXT_DIR/edit-log.txt"
    fi
}

run_test() {
    local test_name="$1"
    local test_cmd="$2"
    local expected="$3"
    
    TESTS_RUN=$((TESTS_RUN + 1))
    echo -n "Test $TESTS_RUN: $test_name... "
    
    if eval "$test_cmd" 2>/dev/null | grep -q "$expected" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo -e "${GREEN}PASS${NC}"
        TESTS_PASSED=$((TESTS_PASSED + 1))
    else
        echo -e "${RED}FAIL${NC}"
        echo "  Expected: $expected"
        echo "  Command: $test_cmd"
        TESTS_FAILED=$((TESTS_FAILED + 1))
    fi
}

# Performance test utility
measure_performance() {
    local hook_name="$1"
    local test_input="$2"
    local hook_script="$3"
    
    local start=$(date +%s%N)
    echo "$test_input" | $hook_script > /dev/null 2>&1
    local end=$(date +%s%N)
    
    local duration_ms=$(((end - start) / 1000000))
    echo "$duration_ms"
}

# Main test execution
main() {
    echo "━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━"
    echo "🧪 HOOKS INTEGRATION TEST SUITE"
    echo "━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━"
    echo ""
    
    setup_test
    
    # Test 1: UserPromptSubmit - Skill activation
    echo -e "\\n${YELLOW}Testing UserPromptSubmit Hook...${NC}"
    
    run_test "TDD prompt activates skill" \\
        "echo '{\"text\": \"I want to write a test for login\"}' | node $HOOKS_DIR/user-prompt-submit/10-skill-activator.js" \\
        "test-driven-development"
    
    run_test "Empty prompt returns continue" \\
        "echo '{\"text\": \"\"}' | node $HOOKS_DIR/user-prompt-submit/10-skill-activator.js" \\
        '{"decision":"continue"}'
    
    run_test "Malformed JSON handled" \\
        "echo 'not json' | node $HOOKS_DIR/user-prompt-submit/10-skill-activator.js" \\
        '{"decision":"continue"}'
    
    # Test 2: PostToolUse - Context tracking
    echo -e "\\n${YELLOW}Testing PostToolUse Hook...${NC}"
    
    run_test "Edit tool logs file" \\
        "echo '{\"tool\": {\"name\": \"Edit\", \"input\": {\"file_path\": \"/test/file1.ts\"}}}' | bash $HOOKS_DIR/post-tool-use/01-track-edits.sh && tail -1 $CONTEXT_DIR/edit-log.txt" \\
        "file1.ts"
    
    run_test "Write tool logs file" \\
        "echo '{\"tool\": {\"name\": \"Write\", \"input\": {\"file_path\": \"/test/file2.py\"}}}' | bash $HOOKS_DIR/post-tool-use/01-track-edits.sh && tail -1 $CONTEXT_DIR/edit-log.txt" \\
        "file2.py"
    
    run_test "Invalid tool ignored" \\
        "echo '{\"tool\": {\"name\": \"Read\", \"input\": {\"file_path\": \"/test/file3.ts\"}}}' | bash $HOOKS_DIR/post-tool-use/01-track-edits.sh" \\
        '{"decision":"continue"}'
    
    # Test 3: Stop - Gentle reminders
    echo -e "\\n${YELLOW}Testing Stop Hook...${NC}"
    
    # Add source file edit for TDD reminder
    echo "$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | test | Edit | /src/main.ts" >> "$CONTEXT_DIR/edit-log.txt"
    
    run_test "TDD reminder shows" \\
        "echo '{\"text\": \"Feature implemented\"}' | bash $HOOKS_DIR/stop/10-gentle-reminders.sh" \\
        "TDD"
    
    run_test "Completion triggers verify" \\
        "echo '{\"text\": \"All done and working!\"}' | bash $HOOKS_DIR/stop/10-gentle-reminders.sh" \\
        "Run tests"
    
    # Add multiple files for commit reminder
    for i in {1..5}; do
        echo "$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S") | test | Edit | /src/file$i.ts" >> "$CONTEXT_DIR/edit-log.txt"
    done
    
    run_test "Many edits triggers commit" \\
        "echo '{\"text\": \"Refactoring\"}' | bash $HOOKS_DIR/stop/10-gentle-reminders.sh" \\
        "commit"
    
    # Test 4: End-to-end workflow
    echo -e "\\n${YELLOW}Testing End-to-End Workflow...${NC}"
    
    # Clear log for clean test
    > "$CONTEXT_DIR/edit-log.txt"
    
    # Simulate full workflow
    echo "Step 1: User prompt with TDD intent"
    result1=$(echo '{"text": "I need to implement authentication with tests"}' | \\
              node "$HOOKS_DIR/user-prompt-submit/10-skill-activator.js")
    
    if echo "$result1" | grep -q "test-driven-development"; then
        echo -e "  ${GREEN}✓ Skill activated${NC}"
    else
        echo -e "  ${RED}✗ Skill not activated${NC}"
        TESTS_FAILED=$((TESTS_FAILED + 1))
    fi
    
    echo "Step 2: Edit files (triggers context tracking)"
    echo '{"tool": {"name": "Edit", "input": {"file_path": "/src/auth.ts"}}}' | \\
        bash "$HOOKS_DIR/post-tool-use/01-track-edits.sh" > /dev/null
    
    if grep -q "auth.ts" "$CONTEXT_DIR/edit-log.txt"; then
        echo -e "  ${GREEN}✓ Edit tracked${NC}"
    else
        echo -e "  ${RED}✗ Edit not tracked${NC}"
        TESTS_FAILED=$((TESTS_FAILED + 1))
    fi
    
    echo "Step 3: Response triggers reminder"
    result3=$(echo '{"text": "Authentication implemented successfully!"}' | \\
              bash "$HOOKS_DIR/stop/10-gentle-reminders.sh")
    
    if echo "$result3" | grep -q "TDD\\|test"; then
        echo -e "  ${GREEN}✓ Reminder shown${NC}"
    else
        echo -e "  ${RED}✗ No reminder${NC}"
        TESTS_FAILED=$((TESTS_FAILED + 1))
    fi
    
    # Test 5: Performance benchmarks
    echo -e "\\n${YELLOW}Performance Benchmarks...${NC}"
    
    perf1=$(measure_performance "UserPromptSubmit" \\
            '{"text": "I want to write tests"}' \\
            "node $HOOKS_DIR/user-prompt-submit/10-skill-activator.js")
    
    perf2=$(measure_performance "PostToolUse" \\
            '{"tool": {"name": "Edit", "input": {"file_path": "/test.ts"}}}' \\
            "bash $HOOKS_DIR/post-tool-use/01-track-edits.sh")
    
    perf3=$(measure_performance "Stop" \\
            '{"text": "Done"}' \\
            "bash $HOOKS_DIR/stop/10-gentle-reminders.sh")
    
    echo "UserPromptSubmit: ${perf1}ms (target: <100ms)"
    echo "PostToolUse: ${perf2}ms (target: <10ms)"
    echo "Stop: ${perf3}ms (target: <50ms)"
    
    # Check performance targets
    if [ "$perf1" -lt 100 ] && [ "$perf2" -lt 10 ] && [ "$perf3" -lt 50 ]; then
        echo -e "${GREEN}✓ All performance targets met${NC}"
        TESTS_PASSED=$((TESTS_PASSED + 1))
    else
        echo -e "${RED}✗ Performance targets not met${NC}"
        TESTS_FAILED=$((TESTS_FAILED + 1))
    fi
    
    teardown_test
    
    # Summary
    echo ""
    echo "━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━"
    echo "📊 TEST RESULTS"
    echo "━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━"
    echo "Total: $TESTS_RUN"
    echo -e "Passed: ${GREEN}$TESTS_PASSED${NC}"
    echo -e "Failed: ${RED}$TESTS_FAILED${NC}"
    
    if [ "$TESTS_FAILED" -eq 0 ]; then
        echo -e "\\n${GREEN}✅ ALL TESTS PASSED!${NC}"
        exit 0
    else
        echo -e "\\n${RED}❌ SOME TESTS FAILED${NC}"
        exit 1
    fi
}

# Run tests
main
\`\`\`

### Step 2: Make test executable and run

Run:
\`\`\`bash
chmod +x hooks/test/integration-test.sh
bash hooks/test/integration-test.sh
\`\`\`

Expected output:
- All tests pass (green checkmarks)
- Performance within targets
- No errors or warnings

### Step 3: Create error scenario tests

Create \`hooks/test/error-test.sh\`:

\`\`\`bash
#!/bin/bash
set -e

echo "=== Testing Error Scenarios ==="

# Test 1: Missing skill-rules.json
echo "Test 1: Missing skill-rules.json"
mv hooks/skill-rules.json hooks/skill-rules.json.bak 2>/dev/null || true
result=$(echo '{"text": "test"}' | node hooks/user-prompt-submit/10-skill-activator.js 2>/dev/null)
[ "$result" = '{"decision":"continue"}' ] && echo "✓ Handled gracefully" || echo "✗ Failed"
mv hooks/skill-rules.json.bak hooks/skill-rules.json 2>/dev/null || true

# Test 2: Corrupted edit-log.txt
echo "Test 2: Corrupted log file"
echo "invalid|data|format" > hooks/context/edit-log.txt
bash hooks/stop/10-gentle-reminders.sh </dev/null >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo "✓ Handled gracefully" || echo "✗ Crashed"

# Test 3: Permission denied on log
echo "Test 3: Permission denied"
chmod 000 hooks/context/edit-log.txt 2>/dev/null || true
echo '{"tool": {"name": "Edit", "input": {"file_path": "/test.ts"}}}' | \\
  bash hooks/post-tool-use/01-track-edits.sh >/dev/null 2>&1
[ $? -eq 0 ] && echo "✓ Continued despite error" || echo "✗ Failed"
chmod 644 hooks/context/edit-log.txt 2>/dev/null || true

# Test 4: Concurrent access
echo "Test 4: Concurrent writes"
for i in {1..10}; do
    echo '{"tool": {"name": "Edit", "input": {"file_path": "/test'$i'.ts"}}}' | \\
      bash hooks/post-tool-use/01-track-edits.sh &
done
wait
lines=$(wc -l < hooks/context/edit-log.txt)
[ "$lines" -ge 8 ] && echo "✓ Most writes succeeded" || echo "✗ Lost writes"

echo "=== Error Tests Complete ==="
\`\`\`

### Step 4: Run error tests

Run:
\`\`\`bash
chmod +x hooks/test/error-test.sh
bash hooks/test/error-test.sh
\`\`\`

Expected: All error scenarios handled gracefully

### Step 5: Create performance stress test

Create \`hooks/test/stress-test.sh\`:

\`\`\`bash
#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Performance Stress Test ==="

# Test with very long prompt (10KB)
long_prompt=$(python3 -c "print('test ' * 2000)")
time echo "{\"text\": \"$long_prompt\"}" | node hooks/user-prompt-submit/10-skill-activator.js > /dev/null

# Test with 100 rapid edits
time for i in {1..100}; do
    echo '{"tool": {"name": "Edit", "input": {"file_path": "/test'$i'.ts"}}}' | \\
      bash hooks/post-tool-use/01-track-edits.sh
done

echo "Stress test complete"
\`\`\`

### Step 6: Document test results

Create \`hooks/test/TEST-RESULTS.md\`:

\`\`\`markdown
# Hook System Test Results

Date: [Current Date]
Version: 1.0.0

## Integration Tests
- ✅ 10/10 scenarios pass
- ✅ End-to-end workflow verified
- ✅ Performance targets met

## Error Handling
- ✅ Missing files handled
- ✅ Corrupted data handled
- ✅ Permission errors handled
- ✅ Concurrent access safe

## Performance
| Hook | Target | Actual | Status |
|------|--------|--------|--------|
| UserPromptSubmit | <100ms | XXms | ✅ |
| PostToolUse | <10ms | XXms | ✅ |
| Stop | <50ms | XXms | ✅ |

## Stress Test
- 10KB prompt: XXXms
- 100 rapid edits: XXXms total
\`\`\`

### Step 7: Commit tests

Run:
\`\`\`bash
git add hooks/test/*.sh hooks/test/TEST-RESULTS.md
git commit -m "test(bd-8): comprehensive integration tests

Implements bd-8: Integration Testing
- End-to-end workflow tests
- Error scenario handling
- Performance benchmarks
- Stress testing
- All tests passing"
\`\`\`

## Key Considerations (ADDED BY SRE REVIEW)

**Test Coverage**:
- Happy path (normal operation)
- Error cases (missing files, bad data)
- Edge cases (empty input, huge input)
- Concurrent access
- Performance under load

**Test Isolation**:
- Save/restore original state
- Clean environment for each test
- No side effects between tests

**Realistic Scenarios**:
- Test actual user workflows
- Include timing/performance checks
- Verify hook interaction

**Maintenance**:
- Tests must be repeatable
- Clear failure messages
- Easy to add new test cases

## Anti-patterns
- ❌ Only testing happy path
- ❌ Tests that depend on external state
- ❌ No performance validation
- ❌ Unclear failure messages
- ❌ Tests that can't be run repeatedly

title: Hook plane teardown: two-survivor end state
description: Delete the non-functional suggestion/reminder/deny hook tiers, harden and contract-test the two surviving hooks (session-start, version-bump-guard), and rewrite the docs that prescribed hooks as the default fix. Born from the 2026-07-07 six-dimension critical review.
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- R1: hooks/hooks.json wires exactly two hooks: SessionStart -> session-start.sh and PreToolUse (Bash matcher) -> pre-tool-use/02-version-bump-guard.py.
- R2: No other hook scripts, rules files, or utilities exist under hooks/. Deleted: user-prompt-submit/, post-tool-use/, stop/, utils/ (all four), context/ (incl. git-tracked edit-log.txt), pre-tool-use/01-block-pre-commit-edits.py, block-beads-direct-read.py, skill-rules.json, REGEX_TESTING.md, test/integration-test.sh.
- R3: skills/skills-auto-activation/ removed entirely; using-hyper routing table row removed; no repo file outside .beads/.git references the deleted machinery.
- R4: version-bump-guard closes its three verified bypasses: multi-line continuation pushes, wrapper-prefixed commands (time/command/env), and non-main/master default branches (origin/HEAD resolution, fail-open when unresolvable).
- R5: Contract tests exist for both survivors using current Claude Code hook schemas (SessionStart input; PreToolUse tool_name/tool_input.command; hookSpecificOutput / permissionDecision outputs) and pass, including the guard deny/allow matrix.
- R6: HOOKS.md documents only the two surviving hooks with correct I/O contracts and states the hook keep-criterion (mechanical at a tool boundary + recurrent despite prompt-level instruction + cheap to verify against the real contract).
- R7: CLAUDE.md no longer prescribes hook creation as the default response to behavior complaints; it states the deny-tier criterion; the false "no tests, linters, or build commands" claim is corrected.
- R8: building-hooks SKILL.md and resources reference no deleted repo hook files (examples become self-contained).
- R9: plugin.json bumped to 3.20.0 before final push.

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)
- [ ] hooks/ contains only: hooks.json, session-start.sh, pre-tool-use/02-version-bump-guard.py, test/ (contract runner + fixtures); hooks.json has exactly 2 entries
- [ ] Repo-wide grep for deleted names (skill-rules, skill-activator, skill-matcher, format-output, gentle-reminders, track-edits, edit-log, block-bd-truncation, block-pre-commit, block-pre-existing, block-beads-direct-read, skills-auto-activation, REGEX_TESTING) is clean outside .beads/ and .git/
- [ ] Contract runner passes: multi-line push DENIED, time/command/env-prefixed push DENIED, non-main default branch handled, feature-branch and version-bumped pushes ALLOWED, session-start emits valid hookSpecificOutput JSON
- [ ] All tests passing (contract runner)
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing
- [ ] plugin.json version = 3.20.0

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO rebuilding any deleted hook "while we're here" (each failed the keep-criterion by explicit architect decision on 2026-07-07; teardown means teardown)
- ❌ NO making tests pass by shaping fixtures to hook assumptions (fixtures encode Claude Code's real contract — legacy self-confirming fixtures are exactly how the rot stayed invisible)
- ❌ NO softening prompt-level MUST rules to compensate for removed hooks (this is relocation of enforcement, not disarmament — hard rules stay)
- ❌ NO leaving files unwired-but-present (zombie files are worse than honest deletion)
- ❌ NO opportunistic fixes of other review findings (review seam, satellite skills, README general staleness — separate epics; scope fence)

## Approach
Surgical single-epic teardown in dependency order: delete the dead tiers and their whole reference graph, harden the one hook with demonstrated recurring value (version-bump-guard), replace the self-confirming test suite with real-schema contract tests, then rewrite the five doc surfaces that described or prescribed the deleted machinery. Chosen over a two-phase neutralize-then-delete (leaves zombies, splits a small diff) and over a greenfield hooks/ rewrite (scope creep into building-hooks pedagogy).

The keep-criterion driving every disposition: a hook earns its place only when the failure is (a) mechanically checkable at a tool boundary, (b) recurrent despite prompt-level instruction, and (c) cheap to verify against Claude Code's real I/O contract. Version-bump-guard passes all three (repeated forgotten bumps documented). Skill activation fails (a)(c); pre-commit guards and bd-truncation fail (b) — root causes fixed upstream or single-incident; beads-read fails (b).

## Architecture
Surviving plane: SessionStart (context injection — the routing system, not enforcement) + one PreToolUse Bash deny (version-bump-guard). Pattern-prevention responsibility moves to prompt-level rules in skills/CLAUDE.md, documented explicitly. Affected file groups: hooks/* (deletions + hardening + tests), skills/skills-auto-activation/* (delete), skills/using-hyper/SKILL.md (routing row), skills/building-hooks/* (example sweep), HOOKS.md (rewrite), CLAUDE.md (Translation Guide + no-tests claim), README.md (hook mentions only), .claude-plugin/plugin.json (3.20.0).

## Architecture Impact
- [ ] Creates a new component/module — NO
- [x] Changes public interface of existing component — YES (runtime hook surface shrinks 9 -> 2)
- [x] Adds/removes cross-component dependency — YES (skills-plane <-> hook-plane links removed: skills-auto-activation, routing row, skill-rules.json)
- [ ] Creates new request path through 2+ components — NO
- [x] Moves responsibility between components — YES (pattern-prevention moves from hooks to prompt-level rules)

Result: 3 boxes checked. /intuition was offered and deferred (architect judged the 2026-07-07 six-dimension review — empirical per-hook behavior tests + full blast-radius mapping — equivalent evidence).

## Provenance
None because idea-first entry: scope originates from the 2026-07-07 in-session critical review of v3.19.0; no upstream plan document governs this area.

## Design Rationale

### Problem
The deterministic hook plane claims nine protections; empirically, most never worked. The skill-activator reads a field Claude Code does not send; three "blocking" PostToolUse hooks emit a PreToolUse-only field after execution; the Stop hook reads a nonexistent field; test fixtures encode the hooks' wrong assumptions so everything passes green. Dead determinism created false confidence while the docs (HOOKS.md, CLAUDE.md) institutionalized "create a hook" as the reflex answer to behavior complaints.

### Research Findings
**Codebase:**
- hooks/user-prompt-submit/10-skill-activator.js:197 — reads prompt.text; real-format input produces {} (empirically reproduced); output shape also wrong (top-level additionalContext)
- hooks/post-tool-use/02,03,04 — emit permissionDecision:"deny" from PostToolUse (PreToolUse-only field, post-execution); doubly inert, reproduced
- hooks/pre-tool-use/02-version-bump-guard.py:49,60,87 — passed full single-line deny/allow matrix; bypassed by multi-line continuation, time/command prefixes, non-main/master default branch (all reproduced end-to-end)
- hooks/pre-tool-use/01-block-pre-commit-edits.py:37 — relative-path bypass reproduced; user elected deletion over fix (single non-recurrent incident)
- Blast radius (grep-verified): suggestion tier referenced by CLAUDE.md, README.md, HOOKS.md, hooks.json, using-hyper, building-hooks SKILL+3 resources, skills-auto-activation SKILL+2 resources; PostToolUse blockers + beads-read referenced only by hooks.json + HOOKS.md
- hooks/context/edit-log.txt — git-tracked with unexpanded literal test junk in HEAD
**External:**
- Current Claude Code hook I/O contracts verified empirically against live harness during the review (prompt field for UserPromptSubmit; tool_name/tool_input for PreToolUse; hookSpecificOutput wrapper; permissionDecision valid only on PreToolUse) — session-start.sh already conforms and serves as the plumbing canary

### Key Decisions Made

| Question | User Answer | Implication |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Keep the enforcement plane at all? | Kill suggestion tier, keep small verified deny tier | Two-survivor end state; keep-criterion becomes documented policy |
| bd-truncation guard disposition | Delete outright (no PreToolUse rebuild) | Root cause fixed upstream by two-tier specs; R2 includes it |
| Pre-commit guards disposition | Delete BOTH Edit/Write and Bash blockers | No pre-commit hook protection remains; prompt-level rules only; stronger than reviewer recommendation, architect chose recurrence criterion strictly |
| beads-read nudge disposition | Delete | No recurrence evidence; removes per-Read/Grep latency |
| Doc scope | Approved full doc scope (not trimmed) | CLAUDE.md Translation Guide + building-hooks sweep in THIS epic |
| /intuition after 3-YES impact check | Proceed without | Recorded as offered-and-deferred |

### Approaches Considered

#### 1. Surgical single-epic teardown (chosen)
**What it is:** Delete all dead tiers + full reference graph, harden version-bump-guard, real-schema contract tests, rewrite five doc surfaces, gated final sweep.
**Investigation:** Blast-radius greps confirm every reference site; review supplies per-hook empirical verdicts.
**Pros:** Atomic end state; matches repo's proven RW-epic pattern (file-group tasks + gated sweep); removes false-confidence surface immediately.
**Cons:** Touches five doc surfaces in one epic — mitigated by scope fence anti-pattern limiting README/CLAUDE.md edits to hook-related content.
**Chosen because:** Diff is small enough for one epic; zombie-free end state is a requirement, not a preference.

#### 2. Two-phase: neutralize then delete (rejected)
**What it is:** Phase 1 unwires everything from hooks.json and ships; phase 2 deletes files and rewrites docs.
**Why explored:** Instant risk reduction with minimal first diff.
**Investigation:** hooks.json is the single wiring point, so unwiring is trivial — but files remain.
**Pros:** Smallest possible first change.
**Cons:** Leaves unwired-but-present zombie scripts and stale docs — a worse lie than today's (docs would describe files that exist but never run).
**REJECTED BECAUSE:** Violates the no-zombies principle; two epics for one small diff.
**DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Teardown diff unexpectedly exceeds a single epic's review capacity.

#### 3. Greenfield hooks/ + building-hooks rewrite (rejected)
**What it is:** Rebuild the hooks directory and building-hooks skill from scratch around the two survivors.
**Why explored:** Cleanest conceivable end state; building-hooks examples need updating anyway.
**Investigation:** building-hooks is 609 lines + 3 resources; only its repo-hook example references are stale, not its pedagogy.
**Pros:** Maximum coherence.
**Cons:** Rewrites a working general-purpose skill to fix reference staleness; churn without behavior change.
**REJECTED BECAUSE:** Reference sweep achieves R8 at ~10% of the effort.
**DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** building-hooks pedagogy is found wrong on its own merits in a future review.

### Scope Boundaries
**In scope:** Everything in R1-R9.
**Out of scope:**
- All other 2026-07-07 review findings: end-of-epic review seam contradiction, sre-task-refinement legacy templates, fixing-bugs RED gate, ponder view paths, README general staleness, context-economics dedup (each needs its own epic)
- BUG-brainstorming-skips-questions.md and blog-with-ideas.txt at repo root (hygiene, not hook plane)
- building-hooks pedagogical content beyond deleted-file references

### Open Questions
- Exact origin/HEAD resolution strategy when the remote HEAD ref is absent locally (git symbolic-ref vs git remote show fallback chain) — resolve in the hardening task; fail-open is the required backstop either way
- Whether the wrapper-prefix allowlist (time/command/env) should also cover nice/nohup — decide in the hardening task; anti-pattern fence: no regex-based command parsing, extend the existing shlex token-skip

title: Task 3: refactoring trio bd-issue transport + rewrite routing
description: One bd issue per refactor: diagnosis creates it with the report in --design, design appends the spec, safely Step 0 gates on bd show; rewrite verdict routes to brainstorming
design:
Classification: medium

## Goal
The refactoring trio's artifacts (diagnosis report, design spec) travel in one bd issue per refactor whose design field accumulates both, making refactoring-safely's Step 0 gate satisfiable in a cold session; the rewrite verdict routes explicitly to hyperpowers:brainstorming.

## Why
R6: refactoring-diagnosis and refactoring-design produce required-format reports with no storage instruction and zero bd commands; refactoring-safely Step 0 demands both artifacts with no location — unsatisfiable cold. Also absorbs refactoring-safely L74's invalid `--priority P2` (R2). Settled decision: bd design field is the transport (repo canon: bd-commands.md "--design carries the full detailed spec"); no in-repo report files.

## Context
All verified at HEAD e4b39ee: skills/refactoring-diagnosis/SKILL.md (130 lines — Step 5 rewrite rule L58-62, Step 6 report format L64-107, integration L127-130); skills/refactoring-design/SKILL.md (121 lines — Step 1 inputs L33-34, Step 7 spec format L63-99, integration L118-121); skills/refactoring-safely/SKILL.md (Step 0 gate L44-50, Step 2 bd commands L67-101 incl. invalid P2 at L74). Canon: skills/common-patterns/bd-commands.md (numeric priority, design-field rule). Note: `bd update --design` REPLACES the whole field — instructions must say re-emit existing sections when appending.

## Implementation
1. refactoring-diagnosis Step 6 (after the L67-107 report template): add the transport instruction — create the refactor's single tracking issue with the report in the design field:
   bd create "Refactor: [target]" --type task --priority 2 \
     --description "[one-line refactor summary]" \
     --design "$(cat <<'INNEREOF'
   ## Diagnosis Report
   [the report in the required format above]
   INNEREOF
   )"
   State: this issue is the refactor's single tracking issue for the whole trio; its id is the handoff token to refactoring-design; design and execution update this same issue.
2. refactoring-diagnosis Step 5 (L58-62): give the rewrite branch a destination — "consider rewrite → route to hyperpowers:brainstorming with the diagnosis report as input (a rewrite is new build work, not a refactor); do not proceed to refactoring-design."
3. refactoring-diagnosis integration (L127-130): add the brainstorming rewrite route and the bd-issue handoff.
4. refactoring-design Step 1 (L33-34): make inputs concrete — "Input is the refactor bd issue from refactoring-diagnosis: `bd show <refactor-id>` must contain a `## Diagnosis Report` section. Missing → stop, run refactoring-diagnosis first."
5. refactoring-design Step 7 (after the L66-99 spec template): add transport — `bd update <refactor-id> --design` writing the field with BOTH sections: the existing `## Diagnosis Report` re-emitted, then `## Refactor Design Spec` appended (bd update --design replaces the field; both must be present after the update).
6. refactoring-design integration (L118-121): reflect the bd-issue flow.
7. refactoring-safely Step 0 (L44-50): concrete gate — "You must have the refactor bd issue: `bd show <refactor-id>` showing both `## Diagnosis Report` and `## Refactor Design Spec`. No issue → run refactoring-diagnosis; issue without the design spec → run refactoring-design."
8. refactoring-safely Step 2 (L67-101): replace the create-a-new-task flow (bd create L72-75 with its invalid P2, plus the L77-98 design write) with working the existing refactor issue: `bd update <refactor-id> --status in_progress`, retitle the step accordingly. The goal/why/approach content the old block duplicated already lives in the issue's two artifact sections.

## Verification
- `grep -n "bd create" skills/refactoring-diagnosis/SKILL.md` → exactly one, with numeric priority
- `grep -c "Diagnosis Report" skills/refactoring-diagnosis/SKILL.md skills/refactoring-design/SKILL.md skills/refactoring-safely/SKILL.md` → ≥1 in each (create / preserve+append / gate)

Do not touch refactoring-safely's transformation catalog (L105 onward). No new files or directories (no docs/refactors/). No epic-typed issues — one task-typed issue per refactor is the unit.

title: Task 1: Add data coupling dispatch to volatility-decomposition Step 0
description: Add Step 0b to volatility-decomposition skill — a second codebase-investigator dispatch that gathers data coupling evidence at module level, using Step 0a structural results to target questions.
design:
## Goal
Add a second investigator dispatch (Step 0b) to the volatility-decomposition skill that gathers data coupling evidence across module boundaries discovered in Step 0a.

## Effort Estimate
2-4 hours

## Implementation

1. Study existing Step 0 in skills/volatility-decomposition/SKILL.md
   - Lines 57-65: REVISION MODE (loads existing graph)
   - Lines 67-78: INCEPTION MODE (codebase investigator dispatch)
   - Line 79: Greenfield case (no codebase — pure design mode)
   - Lines 81-86: ADR check (unchanged)
   - Step 1 (Socratic questioning) starts at line 90
   - Step 2 (Structure proposal) starts at line 285

2. Add Step 0b AFTER the codebase detection block (after line 79), BEFORE the ADR check (line 81)
   - GATING CONDITION: Only dispatch Step 0b when codebase exists (same condition as Step 0a)
   - In greenfield mode, skip Step 0b entirely — no data to analyze
   - Second dispatch to codebase-investigator, parameterized by Step 0a structural results

3. Step 0b prompt (included in skill as template text Claude parameterizes at runtime):

   INCEPTION MODE prompt:
   'Based on these structural findings: [include Step 0a results — modules, dependencies, boundaries]
   For each pair of modules that share a dependency edge:
   1. What data shapes cross this boundary? (types, structures, DTOs at module level)
   2. Which entry points produce data that flows across this boundary?
   3. Are there modules that share data shapes despite no direct import? (hidden coupling through intermediaries)
   Report at module level. For each coupling: source module, destination module, data shape description, triggering entry points.'

   REVISION MODE prompt:
   'Based on these structural findings: [include Step 0a results]
   And this existing architecture graph: [include graph nodes and edges from bd]
   For each boundary in the graph:
   1. What data shapes cross this boundary? Does actual data coupling match the declared edge?
   2. Are there data flows between graph nodes with no declared edge? (hidden coupling)
   3. Which entry points produce data that flows across each boundary?
   Report at module level. Reference graph node names.'

4. Weave coupling evidence into Step 1 (Socratic questioning, line 90+)
   - Add instruction after line 93 (REQUIRED: Use AskUserQuestion): include data coupling evidence as input to questions
   - When probing isolation ('If you replace X, what else changes?'), reference coupling evidence: 'Codebase investigation found modules A and B share User data across their boundary.'
   - When probing rate-of-change, note which boundaries have heavy data coupling vs light
   - For REVISION MODE: reference where actual coupling differs from declared graph edges

5. Weave coupling evidence into Step 2 (Structure proposal, line 285+)
   - Add instruction in proposal format (around line 290): each proposed boundary must note data coupling cost
   - Edge list format gains coupling annotation:
     Current: '- [Node A] blocks [Node B] (interface: [what flows between them])'
     New: '- [Node A] blocks [Node B] (interface: [what flows between them], coupling: [N shared data shapes, triggered by M entry points])'
   - Tradeoff analysis should reference coupling: 'This boundary splits modules with 4 shared data shapes — interface cost is real'

## Implementation Checklist
- [ ] Step 0b section added after line 79 (codebase detection), before line 81 (ADR check)
- [ ] Step 0b gated on codebase existence (same condition as Step 0a — skipped in greenfield)
- [ ] INCEPTION MODE dispatch prompt present with parameterization reference
- [ ] REVISION MODE dispatch prompt present with graph context reference
- [ ] Step 1 instructions include coupling evidence usage (after line 93)
- [ ] Step 2 proposal format includes coupling annotation on edges (around line 290)
- [ ] Step 2 tradeoff analysis references coupling evidence
- [ ] No changes to Step 0a structural dispatch (lines 71-78 unchanged)
- [ ] No changes to ADR creation process (Step 4 unchanged)
- [ ] No changes to greenfield mode (line 79 still skips investigation)
- [ ] Module-level resolution maintained in prompt text
- [ ] Quick reference table (line 17) updated: Step 0 deliverable updated to include data coupling

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ Do NOT modify the existing Step 0a dispatch prompt (lines 71-78)
- ❌ Do NOT make Step 0b fire in greenfield mode (no codebase = no data to analyze)
- ❌ Do NOT change the Socratic questioning format in Step 1 (AskUserQuestion tool usage, question families)
- ❌ Do NOT change the ADR template or ADR creation process in Step 4
- ❌ Do NOT modify the codebase-investigator agent prompt
- ❌ Do NOT add function/class-level detail to the dispatch prompt (stay module-level)
- ❌ Do NOT make coupling evidence replace structural evidence (additive only)

## Key Considerations (SRE REVIEW)

**Edge Case: Minimal Structural Results**
If Step 0a returns few modules or boundaries (small codebase), Step 0b coupling analysis will also be thin. This is acceptable — thin input produces thin output. Do not add complexity to handle this; the Socratic questioning in Step 1 fills gaps through dialogue.

**Edge Case: Revision Mode with Stale Graph**
In revision mode, the graph may not match current codebase. Step 0b compares actual data coupling against declared edges. Discrepancies (data flows between nodes with no declared edge) are valuable evidence for the revision — they surface where the graph has drifted from reality.

**Edge Case: Step 0b Returns No Coupling**
If modules are truly independent (no shared data shapes), Step 0b returns empty coupling evidence. This is a positive signal — proposed boundaries that split these modules have low interface cost. Do not treat absence of coupling as an error.

**Mechanism: Prompt Parameterization**
The skill file contains template text. At runtime, Claude substitutes Step 0a results into the prompt — same mechanism used for the existing Step 0a dispatch. The results are in Claude's context from the first dispatch; the second dispatch references them.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Step 0b section present between codebase detection and ADR check
- [ ] Step 0b has explicit gating condition: only fires when codebase exists
- [ ] Step 0b has separate prompts for INCEPTION and REVISION modes
- [ ] REVISION mode prompt references graph node names from bd
- [ ] Step 1 contains instruction to use coupling evidence in Socratic questions
- [ ] Step 2 edge format includes coupling annotation
- [ ] Step 2 tradeoff section references coupling evidence
- [ ] Existing Step 0a dispatch unchanged (diff shows no modifications to lines 71-78)
- [ ] Quick reference table reflects Step 0 change
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

title: Review fixes: catalogs & hygiene
description: Make human-facing catalogs match reality (README/CLAUDE.md), delete stale root bug-note, fix dangling links in root-cause-tracing and writing-plans
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)

**Out of scope:** finishing-a-development-branch hygiene items (owned by the spine epic bd-42cc); any skill-body content changes beyond the named lines.

## Dependencies

## Provenance

- Machine routing (using-hyper table) is already complete — this epic touches only human-facing catalogs; using-hyper is not edited.

## Open for Design

- root-cause-tracing: whether instrumentation-patterns/defense-patterns deserve stub resources or link removal.

## Context for Brainstorming
Anchors: README Skills 9-47, Commands 49-62 (53-62 list), Agents 64-73, Hooks 77-83 (verified accurate — leave); CLAUDE.md agent list at 99 and Agent System 148-158, bd examples ~144; root-cause-tracing resources block 550-559; writing-plans 57, 495. Disk inventory at review time: 26 skills (excl. common-patterns), 14 commands, 8 agents.
Contracts: none — purely descriptive surfaces. Release framing: last epic; runs after bd-42cc merges; version bump per convention (patch — docs only) unless bundled with other pending changes.

Initiative Architecture Impact (from preordain Step 4 — identical across this initiative's leaf epics):
