title: Task 10: CLAUDE.md — canonical pipeline, stale model refs, tracker wording
description: CLAUDE.md's workflow section reflects shape A (brainstorm owns tree+SRE; writing-plans as utility), drops both Opus 4.1 references, cites the unified threshold, and uses tracker-aware checklist wording.
design:
## Goal
CLAUDE.md describes the same pipeline as the skills: brainstorm (verified tree + batch SRE inside) then executing-plans, with writing-plans as the off-mainline repair utility; no stale model or threshold references.

## Why
CLAUDE.md currently lists SRE before Writing Plans (a third ordering variant), says SRE "uses Opus 4.1" twice, and carries the "~10 tasks or 3-4 components" threshold variant (findings F2/F7). CLAUDE.md is injected context — its contradictions reach every session.

## Context
File: CLAUDE.md. Sites (read before editing): "Feature Development (Greenfield)" numbered list in Core Workflows; "SRE Task Refinement" list item ("using Opus 4.1"); Important Notes bullet "The sre-task-refinement skill uses Opus 4.1 for deep analysis"; Brainstorming list item's escalation threshold ("~10 tasks or 3-4 components"); "bd Integration" section lines saying tasks are "created by writing-plans"; the mandatory-workflows/TodoWrite bullet ("Create TodoWrite todos for checklists"). Reference: skills/common-patterns/pipeline-constants.md.

LEAD RULING (post-SRE): citation-only in file text — threshold numbers may appear in THIS spec as context but must NOT be written into CLAUDE.md; the citation to pipeline-constants.md carries the values (epic anti-pattern: re-embedded numbers recreate drift).

## Implementation
1. Feature Development list becomes: 1 Preordain (unchanged text), 2 Brainstorming — refines requirements, creates the complete VERIFIED task tree, and runs batch SRE review (escalates to preordain per common-patterns/pipeline-constants.md thresholds), 3 Executing Plans (unchanged text), 4 Review Implementation, 5 Finishing Branch. Remove the standalone SRE and Writing Plans numbered items; add an unnumbered note: "writing-plans: off-mainline utility that expands or repairs specs for tasks lacking them (reviewer gap-fixes, amendments)."
2. Important Notes: delete the Opus 4.1 sentence; replace with "The sre-task-refinement skill runs once against the full task tree during brainstorming Step 7."
3. bd Integration section: tasks are created by brainstorming (executed via executing-plans); fix both mentions.
4. TodoWrite bullet: "Create bd/TodoWrite todos per the repo's tracker (bd when beads is present)".
5. Threshold text: replace "~10 tasks or 3-4 components" with "the thresholds in skills/common-patterns/pipeline-constants.md" — no numbers in the replacement text.

## Verification
- [ ] rg 'Opus 4.1' CLAUDE.md returns zero
- [ ] rg '3-4 components' CLAUDE.md returns zero
- [ ] rg 'created by writing-plans' CLAUDE.md returns zero
- [ ] rg 'more than 10 tasks' CLAUDE.md returns zero (citation-only ruling)
- [ ] Feature Development list has 5 numbered items with SRE inside item 2
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Boundaries
- Do NOT edit other CLAUDE.md sections (translation guide, philosophy, hooks docs)

title: RW5 T2: Create skills/portent/phase-doc-template.md (colleague-facing template)
description:
Tier: medium. Executor: lead-direct (D2-AMENDED).

## Goal
Create skills/portent/phase-doc-template.md — the copyable phase-document template: phase overview + repeating per-service slice skeleton.

## Why
Epic requirement: phase-doc template reads as a normal engineering planning doc; colleagues consume it directly. This file is the Mode-1 rendering of the schema in skills/common-patterns/brainstormable-unit.md.

## Context
Template is copied verbatim into planning repos. HARD CONSTRAINT: zero plugin vocabulary in the body — no tool names, no plugin/skill/agent/tracker jargon, no references back to this repo. Machine legibility comes from consistent headings only. Bracketed placeholders carry authoring guidance in plain engineering language.

## Implementation
Structure:
1. Title: 'Phase [N] — [Phase Name]'.
2. Overview section: Goal (one-two sentences); Context (what earlier phases established); Services in this phase; Cross-service ordering (or 'None').
3. One example slice under '## [service-name]' with the seven sections as '###' headings, exact names: 'Deliverable & Requirements' (what ships + 'Must hold when done' testable list); 'Contracts' (Exposes: interface — consumed by sibling, one line on shape; Consumes: interface — owned by sibling, status; note tying contracts to deployable units); 'Dependencies' (Depends on / Blocks, 'None' allowed); 'Boundaries' ('Not this service's job in this phase' + owning sibling); 'Settled Decisions' (decision — rationale, what was rejected; 'None' allowed); 'Open for Design' (questions the implementing team answers; 'None' means fully settled); 'Release Framing' (flag/dark-launch/lockstep-with-named-service/ordering; 'Independently deployable' if unconstrained).
4. Closing marker showing the slice block repeats per service.
Placeholders state that absent content must be declared ('None') rather than omitted.

## Verification (gates — abort on failure)
- rg -qiE 'hyperpowers|portent|subagent|\bskill\b|claude|\bbd\b|bd-[a-z0-9]|\bepic\b|brainstorm|cold-session|cantrip' skills/portent/phase-doc-template.md && ABORT (colleague-facing purity)
- Seven '###' section names appear verbatim (rg -F each) || ABORT
- rg -q 'Must hold when done' skills/portent/phase-doc-template.md || ABORT
- rg -qE '[0-9]+ tasks|[0-9]+ min' skills/portent/phase-doc-template.md && ABORT

## Boundaries
Template only — no authoring process (T3 owns that), no schema rationale (T1 owns that). The template never explains WHY sections exist; it shows WHAT a finished doc looks like.
close_reason: Template created; purity gate + seven headings + requirements phrasing all green

title: Architecture Representation & Workflow Revision
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)

### Three Artifacts, Three Purposes
- Architecture model lives in LikeC4 .c4 files + linked markdown in doc/arch/components/
- ADRs remain in doc/arch/adr-NNN.md (Nygard format, permanent, never deleted)
- Beads track work only (ephemeral) — NOT architecture structure
- These three artifacts MUST NOT be complected

### LikeC4 Model Conventions
- Single model (no current/target split) — #target tag distinguishes aspirational from implemented
- When #target component is implemented, tag is removed
- Metadata carries Lowy concepts: volatility_axis, layer, stability_state
- Elements link to prose markdown in doc/arch/components/ via link keyword
- Dynamic views are curated (not auto-generated), only flows worth reasoning about
- MCP server required via likec4 mcp --stdio CLI (no parsing fallback)
- Single project with nested elements for fractal decomposition

### Stability State Lifecycle (per-component)
- pre-fit → exploring → audited → stable
- Pre-fit: axis is hypothesis, ADRs include falsification criteria, shallower decomposition
- stability_state is metadata (mutable), #target is tag (removed when implemented) — orthogonal

### Brainstorming Mode Detection
- Thinking mode: user has questions/uncertainty/half-formed ideas — Socratic exploration, NO backlog loading
- Building mode: user references specific feature/bead/component — load context, elaborate, produce epic
- Ambiguous: ask 'Are you exploring an idea or ready to build something specific?'
- Thinking mode can transition to building mode; reverse should not happen

### Architecture Update Checklist (5 questions)
Run after inner-loop work completes:
1. Changed a component's public interface?
2. Added or removed a dependency between components?
3. Created a new component or removed an existing one?
4. Moved responsibility from one component to another?
5. Created a new request path through 2+ components?
If any yes: update .c4 files, update linked markdown, propose dynamic view if Q5, confirm with architect

### Dynamic View Curation
- Decomposition creates initial views for important flows
- Audit Pass 7 detects staleness by comparing documented views to codebase flows
- Brainstorming asks whether new features create request paths worth documenting
- No auto-generation

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)
- [ ] Volatility-decomposition skill produces LikeC4 .c4 files + linked markdown + bd work issues (not bd architecture nodes)
- [ ] Brainstorming skill has mode detection gate (thinking vs building) before any context loading
- [ ] Brainstorming skill loads architecture context from LikeC4 MCP instead of bd
- [ ] Brainstorming skill includes architecture update checklist and dynamic view proposal
- [ ] Architectural audit loads graph from LikeC4 MCP instead of bd
- [ ] Architectural audit has Pass 7: dynamic view drift detection
- [ ] Audit handles pre-fit components (lighter axis scrutiny, heavier boundary cost scrutiny)
- [ ] Pre-fit mode in decomposition adapts depth per component based on stability_state metadata
- [ ] ADR template unchanged (Nygard format)
- [ ] Migration path documented in decomposition skill
- [ ] All skills tested with subagents per writing-skills skill
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO architecture state in bd (separation: bd tracks work, LikeC4 tracks structure)
- ❌ NO auto-generated dynamic views (signal-to-noise: only curated flows worth reasoning about)
- ❌ NO current/target model split (tension: single model with #target tag makes gaps visible)
- ❌ NO parsing .c4 files directly when MCP unavailable (fragility: require MCP, don't build fragile parser)
- ❌ NO global pre-fit setting (granularity: stability_state is per-component in metadata)
- ❌ NO backlog triage in thinking mode (purpose: thinking mode is Socratic exploration, not work planning)
- ❌ NO multi-project LikeC4 setup (simplicity: nested elements in single project until proven insufficient)
- ❌ NO architecture updates without architect confirmation (ownership: checklist surfaces candidates, architect decides)
- ❌ NO ADR changes to accommodate LikeC4 (independence: ADRs are permanent records, format unchanged)

## Approach
Migrate architecture representation from bd issues to LikeC4 .c4 files across three skills. Add mode detection gate to brainstorming. Add dynamic view staleness detection to audit. Add architecture update checklist to review-implementation. Keep ADRs unchanged. Keep agents and commands unchanged.

Three skills changed, everything else untouched:
1. Volatility-decomposition: Step 0 context detection + Step 3 output format (CRITICAL blast radius)
2. Brainstorming: Mode detection gate + context loading + update checklist + dynamic view proposal (HIGH blast radius)
3. Architectural audit: Step 0 graph loading + Pass 7 + pre-fit handling + stability updates (HIGH blast radius)

## Architecture
File structure convention:
- arch/spec.c4 — Element kinds, relationship kinds, tags
- arch/model.c4 — Top-level system elements and relationships
- arch/components/*.c4 — Component detail + nested elements
- arch/views/landscape.c4 — System-level view
- arch/views/data-flows/*.c4 — Dynamic views for curated request paths
- doc/arch/adr-NNN.md — ADRs (unchanged)
- doc/arch/components/*.md — Prose docs linked from LikeC4 elements

LikeC4 MCP tools used by skills:
- search-element: find elements by ID/title/kind/tags/metadata
- find-relationships: find direct and indirect relationships
- read-element: get complete element details
- read-view: fetch diagram definition
- read-project-summary: high-level overview

## Design Rationale

### Problem
Current skills encode architecture in bd as work items. This conflates what the system IS (architecture) with what work needs to happen (backlog). Architecture doesn't close. Components don't have status 'done'. The mismatch creates friction: brainstorming defaults to backlog triage instead of being a thinking tool, architecture context lives in task metadata, nothing writes back to architecture when implementation changes structure, architecture is invisible to humans not reading bd output.

### Research Findings

**Codebase:**
- skills/volatility-decomposition/SKILL.md lines 387-475: Step 3 creates bd architecture epic + component nodes with labels
- skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md lines 52-130: Detects architecture nodes via bd list --label arch
- skills/architectural-audit/SKILL.md lines 42-97: Loads graph from bd list --label arch,component
- Agents: zero architecture references. Commands: thin wrappers, zero changes needed.

**External:**
- LikeC4 v1.48.0 (Jan 2026): custom element kinds, arbitrary metadata (string), custom tags with view filtering, dynamic views with navigateTo, multi-project support, standalone MCP server via CLI
- MCP server: 6 tools (search-element, find-relationships, read-element, read-view, list-projects, read-project-summary)
- link keyword: fully supported on elements, relative file paths with optional labels
- Standalone MCP: likec4 mcp --stdio or @likec4/mcp npm package

### Approaches Considered

#### 1. LikeC4 as single architecture model ✓
Replace bd architecture storage entirely with LikeC4 .c4 files. Metadata carries Lowy concepts. Tags distinguish aspirational from implemented. Linked markdown provides prose depth.

Chosen because: clean separation of concerns (architecture ≠ work items), LikeC4 has all required features (metadata, tags, links, MCP, dynamic views), makes architecture visible to humans via diagrams.

#### 2. Hybrid bd + LikeC4 ❌
Keep bd for architecture metadata, add LikeC4 for visual diagrams only.

⚠️ REJECTED BECAUSE: Complects the two systems. Must sync bd and LikeC4. Architecture metadata lives in two places. Defeats the purpose of separation.

🚫 DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS: LikeC4 metadata support is insufficient for Lowy concepts (verified: it is sufficient).

### Scope Boundaries
**In scope:** Three skill rewrites, mode detection, dynamic view staleness, architecture update checklist, migration path documentation
**Out of scope:** Agent changes (zero blast radius), command changes (thin wrappers), ADR format changes (independent), review-implementation skill rewrite (checklist only addition)

### Open Questions (resolved during brainstorming)
- MCP availability → Require standalone CLI, no fallback (resolved)
- Dynamic view curation → Audit detects staleness + brainstorming proposes (resolved)
- Architecture update granularity → 5-question lightweight checklist (resolved)
- Pre-fit trigger → Per-component via stability_state metadata (resolved)
- Multi-project → Single project with nested elements (resolved)
- link keyword → Confirmed supported in LikeC4 v1.48 (resolved)

## Design Discovery (Reference Context)

### Key Decisions Made

| Question | User Answer | Implication |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Pre-fit scope? | Per-component | stability_state metadata includes pre-fit alongside exploring/audited/stable |
| Dynamic view curation? | Audit detects + brainstorming proposes | New Pass 7 in audit, new question in brainstorming design phase |
| Architecture update trigger? | Lightweight checklist | 5 yes/no questions in review-implementation, not pure judgment |
| Multi-project? | Nested elements, single project | No likec4.config.json multi-project setup |
| First task? | Mode detection | Independent of LikeC4 migration, most important behavioral change |

### Research Deep-Dives

#### LikeC4 Capability Validation
Question: Does LikeC4 support all features the design assumes?
Sources: likec4.dev docs, GitHub releases, MCP documentation
Findings: All features confirmed (metadata, tags, links, dynamic views, standalone MCP). v1.48.0 is current.
Conclusion: Design assumptions are valid, no compromises needed.

#### Blast Radius Analysis
Question: What exactly changes in the codebase?
Sources: All skill files, agent files, command files, common patterns
Findings: 3 skills change (decomposition, brainstorming, audit). 0 agents, 0 commands, 0 ADR template changes.
Conclusion: Clean blast radius, well-contained migration.

### Dead-End Paths

#### Hybrid bd + LikeC4
Why explored: Could reduce migration scope by keeping bd for metadata
Investigation: Analyzed what metadata bd stores vs what LikeC4 metadata supports
Why abandoned: Creates sync problem between two systems, defeats separation of concerns

#### Parsing .c4 files as MCP fallback
Why explored: MCP might require VSCode
Investigation: Found likec4 mcp --stdio CLI and @likec4/mcp npm package
Why abandoned: Standalone MCP confirmed, no fallback needed

### Open Concerns Raised
- None remaining — all open questions resolved during brainstorming
close_reason: All 12 success criteria met. Reviewer approved. Version bumped to 2.14.0 and pushed.

title: Bug: Hooks using invalid 'continue' decision instead of 'approve' or 'deny'
design:
## Root Cause Analysis

The hooks implementation used 'continue' as a decision value, which was incorrect. The fix changed it to 'approve', but that's ALSO incorrect!

According to Claude Code official documentation:

**Valid decision values by hook type:**
- **PreToolUse**: 'allow', 'deny', 'ask'
- **PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, Stop, SubagentStop**: 'block' OR undefined (no decision field)
- **SessionStart/SessionEnd**: No decision field; use additionalContext only

**Our hooks and their issues:**

1. **SessionStart hook** (hooks/session-start.sh:25-32)
   - ✅ CORRECT: Returns hookSpecificOutput with additionalContext, no decision field
   
2. **Stop hook** (hooks/stop/10-gentle-reminders.sh:85-86)
   - ✅ CORRECT: Returns nothing (just exit 0), which is valid for non-blocking reminders
   
3. **PostToolUse hook** (hooks/post-tool-use/01-track-edits.sh:73,79,98,119)
   - ❌ INCORRECT: Returns {"decision": "approve"} 
   - Should: Omit decision field entirely (just tracking edits, not blocking)
   - Correct format: {} or {"hookSpecificOutput": {"hookEventName": "PostToolUse"}}
   
4. **UserPromptSubmit hook** (hooks/user-prompt-submit/10-skill-activator.js:159,167,182,190,197)
   - ❌ INCORRECT: Returns {"decision": "approve", "additionalContext": "..."}
   - Should: Omit decision field (adding context, not blocking prompts)
   - Correct format: {"additionalContext": "..."} or {"hookSpecificOutput": {"hookEventName": "UserPromptSubmit", "additionalContext": "..."}}

**Why 'approve' is wrong:**
- 'approve' is a deprecated legacy value that mapped to 'allow' for PreToolUse
- For PostToolUse and UserPromptSubmit, 'approve' is not a valid decision
- These hooks should either omit the decision field OR use 'block' if they want to halt execution

**Evidence:**
- Official docs: https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks
- Research from hyperpowers:internet-researcher agent (detailed analysis)
- Git history shows 'continue' was changed to 'approve' in 6aaceca, but both are incorrect

## Solution Approach

1. Remove decision field from PostToolUse hook (4 locations in hooks/post-tool-use/01-track-edits.sh)
2. Remove decision field from UserPromptSubmit hook (5 locations in hooks/user-prompt-submit/10-skill-activator.js)  
3. Update all documentation/examples to reflect correct response structure
4. Keep SessionStart and Stop hooks as-is (already correct)
notes:

Fixed hooks to use correct decision values per Claude Code specification.

## Changes Made

- Now returns '{}' (empty object) - correct for non-blocking logging hooks

### 2. UserPromptSubmit Hook (hooks/user-prompt-submit/10-skill-activator.js)

- Now returns '{}' or '{"additionalContext": "..."}' - correct for context injection

- hooks/test/integration-test.sh: Updated expectations

### 4. Documentation Updated

- skills/building-hooks/resources/hook-examples.md: Fixed all examples
- skills/building-hooks/resources/hook-patterns.md: Fixed all patterns
- skills/skills-auto-activation/resources/hook-implementation.md: Fixed expected output

All integration tests passing (13/13).

title: Task 2: Update brainstorming skill — remove Haiku-first philosophy
design:
## Goal
Remove all model-selection language from the brainstorming skill. Classification remains for spec template depth only.

## Why
Brainstorming creates the task tree and currently instructs planners to "minimize Sonnet usage." With Sonnet-first policy, this philosophy is inverted — the planning concern is spec quality, not model optimization.

## Context
- brainstorming/SKILL.md is ~523 lines
- Lines 301-307: "Haiku-first philosophy" section (6 lines of argumentative prose)
- Lines 380-381: Classification guide annotated with "(Haiku)" and "(Sonnet)"
- Line 317: Section header "6c — Create Complete Task Tree" references classification tied to models
- Line 466: Critical Rule 6b explicitly about Haiku-first
- Lines 407-439: Step 8 handoff text references "dynamic model selection" and "Haiku for simple... Sonnet for medium"
- Line 22: Quick reference table says "Haiku-first classification"
- Line 24: Quick reference "simple tasks (Haiku), medium tasks (Sonnet)"
- Line ~494: Verification checklist says "Haiku-first classification"

## Implementation
1. **Delete Haiku-first philosophy (lines 301-307):** Remove the entire paragraph starting "Haiku-first philosophy:" through "...each executor task is a cheap Haiku application of that pattern." Replace with a brief note: "Classification determines spec depth. All executors run on Sonnet regardless of classification."

2. **Update classification guide (lines 380-381):** Remove "(Haiku)" and "(Sonnet)" from the entries. Change to:
   - "Simple (2-5 min): Mechanical changes with exact known edits..."
   - "Medium (5-15 min): Changes requiring judgment or design decisions..."

3. **Remove Critical Rule 6b (line 466):** Delete the rule "Haiku-first default → Every task is Haiku unless you can name the specific judgment it requires; minimize Sonnet usage by decomposing aggressively". Remove the corresponding rationalization "This task touches many files, it needs Sonnet — File count is not a complexity signal; name the judgment required or make it Haiku" from the common excuses section.

4. **Update Step 8 handoff (lines 407-439):** Remove references to "dynamic model selection", "Haiku for simple 2-5 min tasks, Sonnet for medium 5-15 min tasks". Replace with language about dispatching executor subagents on Sonnet. Update the summary line from "[X] simple tasks (Haiku), [Y] medium tasks (Sonnet)" to "[X] simple tasks, [Y] medium tasks" (no model annotations).

5. **Update quick reference table (lines 22-24):** Remove "Haiku-first classification" and model annotations. Change to reflect that classification is about spec depth.

6. **Update Step 6b header/intro:** The section "6b — Decomposition Strategy Proposal" mentions Sonnet justifications — remove model justification language. The decomposition strategy should focus on spec depth (simple vs medium detail level), not model cost optimization.

7. **Update common excuses (line 476):** Remove "This task touches many files, it needs Sonnet — File count is not a complexity signal; name the judgment required or make it Haiku" — no longer relevant.

8. **Update verification_checklist (~line 494):** Change "all tasks created with Haiku-first classification and linked to epic" → "all tasks classified (simple/medium for spec depth) and linked to epic".

## Tests
No automated tests. Verify by reading final file for internal consistency.

## Verification
- [ ] `grep -i "haiku" skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md` returns 0 results
- [ ] `grep -i "dynamic model" skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md` returns 0 results
- [ ] Simple/medium classification still present (for spec templates)
- [ ] Step 8 handoff text is coherent without model language
- [ ] Critical rules renumbered correctly after 6b removal
- [ ] Verification checklist updated (no "Haiku-first")
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Boundaries
- Do NOT remove simple/medium classification (spec templates still differ)
- Do NOT change Steps 1-5 (unrelated to model policy)
- Do NOT change SRE batch review step (Step 7)
- Do NOT touch other files — those are separate tasks
close_reason: All model-selection language removed. Grep confirms no haiku/dynamic-model terms remain. Classification retained for spec depth only.

title: Architectural audit: LikeC4 MCP loading + Pass 7 + pre-fit handling
description: The architectural audit skill currently loads the architecture graph from bd (Step 0, lines 42-97) and updates stability states via bd (Step 3, lines 393-407). Rewrite to use LikeC4 MCP. Add Pass 7 for dynamic view drift detection. Add pre-fit component handling (lighter axis scrutiny, heavier boundary cost scrutiny). This is the last skill change in the epic.
design:
## Goal
Three changes to the architectural audit skill:
1. Replace bd graph loading with LikeC4 MCP queries
2. Add Pass 7: dynamic view drift detection
3. Handle pre-fit components differently in analysis passes

## Effort Estimate
6-8 hours (three distinct changes to one skill)

## Implementation

### 1. Rewrite Step 0: Load Architecture Graph

Replace bd-based graph loading with LikeC4 MCP:

CURRENT:
```bash
bd list --label arch --type epic --status open
bd show <epic-id>
bd list --label arch,component --parent <epic-id>
for each component: bd show <component-id>
bd dep tree <epic-id>
```

NEW:
```
# Check for architecture model
ls arch/*.c4 2>/dev/null

# If not found: report 'No architecture model found. Run /decompose first.'

# If found, use LikeC4 MCP:
# 1. read-project-summary — overview of element kinds, tags, total elements
# 2. search-element (kind, tags) — find all components
# 3. For each component: read-element — get metadata (volatility_axis, layer, stability_state), relationships
# 4. find-relationships — get all edges (blocks, relatesTo)
# 5. For dynamic views: list views, read-view for each data flow view
```

Record same information as before:
- All components with volatility axes, layers, interface contracts (from linked markdown)
- All relationships with descriptions
- Stability states (from metadata)
- Layer assignments (from metadata)
- #target components (aspirational, skip codebase comparison)
- Dynamic views (for Pass 7)

Hard fail if MCP unavailable: 'LikeC4 MCP server required. Run: likec4 mcp --stdio'

ADR loading unchanged: ls doc/arch/adr-*.md

### 2. Update Step 1: Evidence Gathering

Codebase-investigator dispatch stays the same (investigates actual code, not architecture representation). But update the dispatch prompt to describe architecture in LikeC4 terms:

- Reference components by LikeC4 element names (not bd task IDs)
- Reference relationships from LikeC4 (not bd deps)
- Reference interface contracts from doc/arch/components/*.md (not bd design fields)

### 3. Add Pass 7: Dynamic View Drift

After existing 6 analysis passes, add:

```
Pass 7: Dynamic View Drift

For each dynamic view in the LikeC4 model (from read-view):
  1. Identify the documented flow: entry point → component A → component B → ... → exit
  2. Dispatch codebase-investigator:
     'Trace the [flow name] request path starting from [entry point].
      What components does it actually flow through?
      What data shapes cross each boundary?'
  3. Compare actual path vs documented path:
     - Steps differ → tension (documented flow ≠ actual flow)
     - Components renamed/removed → tension
     - New components in path not in view → tension
  4. Skip #target components (aspirational, no code to compare)
  5. Produce tensions in same format as Passes 1-6 (both pulls, no recommendations)
```

Each tension from Pass 7:
```
Name: Dynamic view [flow-name] drift
Components: [components involved]
Analysis Pass: 7 — Dynamic View Drift
Pull A: Documented flow reflects intended architecture; actual code has drifted
Pull B: Actual code reflects evolved requirements; documented flow is stale
Assumptions: [what each pull assumes]
Observation: [specific discrepancy]
Evidence: [from codebase-investigator dispatch]
```

### 4. Handle pre-fit components

For components with stability_state 'pre-fit' in metadata:

In Passes 1-5 (complection, interface, temporal, hidden deps, layer violations):
- LIGHTER axis scrutiny: don't flag axis alignment tensions (axis is known hypothesis)
- HEAVIER boundary cost scrutiny: flag if boundary has high interface surface area relative to uncertainty
- Note in tension: 'Component is pre-fit — volatility axis is a hypothesis'

In Pass 6 (state/identity):
- Normal scrutiny (state management issues don't depend on fit)

In Pass 7 (dynamic view drift):
- Skip pre-fit components (no meaningful drift comparison if axis is uncertain)

In Step 3 (stability state updates):
- Do NOT promote pre-fit components to 'audited' — pre-fit stays pre-fit until architect explicitly changes it
- Components at 'exploring' that pass audit → promote to 'audited'
- Update stability_state in LikeC4 metadata (edit the .c4 file)

### 5. Update Step 3: Stability State Updates

Replace bd-based stability updates with LikeC4 .c4 file edits:

CURRENT:
```bash
bd set-state <component> stability=audited
# or bd label <component> arch:audited
```

NEW:
Edit the component's .c4 file to change metadata:
```
metadata {
    stability_state 'audited'  # was 'exploring'
}
```

### 6. Update tension report format

Add to report:
- 'Target Components' section listing #target elements (aspirational, not audited against code)
- 'Pre-fit Components' section listing pre-fit elements with note about lighter scrutiny
- Pass 7 tensions in the main tensions list

### 7. Test with subagents

Test cases:
- Loads graph from LikeC4 MCP (not bd) — zero bd arch commands
- MCP not running → hard fail with helpful error
- #target components skipped in codebase comparison
- Pre-fit components: lighter axis scrutiny, heavier boundary cost
- Pre-fit components NOT promoted to audited
- Exploring components promoted to audited when clean
- Pass 7: dynamic view with matching codebase flow → no tension
- Pass 7: dynamic view with drifted codebase flow → tension produced
- Pass 7: #target components in dynamic view → skipped
- Stability state updated via .c4 file edit (not bd)
- ADR-covered tensions still detected and skipped
- Six existing passes produce same tension format

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Step 0 loads graph from LikeC4 MCP (not bd)
- [ ] No bd architecture commands remain (no bd list --label arch, no bd set-state)
- [ ] MCP not running → hard fail with 'Run: likec4 mcp --stdio'
- [ ] #target components listed but skipped in codebase comparison
- [ ] Pass 7 detects dynamic view drift (documented vs actual flow)
- [ ] Pass 7 produces tensions in same format as Passes 1-6
- [ ] Pass 7 skips #target components in dynamic views
- [ ] Pre-fit components get lighter axis scrutiny
- [ ] Pre-fit components get heavier boundary cost scrutiny
- [ ] Pre-fit components NOT promoted to audited
- [ ] Exploring components promoted to audited when clean (via .c4 file edit)
- [ ] Tension report includes Target Components and Pre-fit Components sections
- [ ] ADR loading unchanged
- [ ] Tested with subagents per writing-skills skill
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ Do NOT add .c4 file parsing as MCP fallback (epic anti-pattern)
- ❌ Do NOT make recommendations in tension report (audit observes, architect decides)
- ❌ Do NOT auto-generate dynamic views (epic anti-pattern: curated only)
- ❌ Do NOT change the 6 existing analysis passes logic (additive: add Pass 7, modify pass behavior for pre-fit)
- ❌ Do NOT promote pre-fit to audited (pre-fit is architect's explicit choice, not audit's to change)
- ❌ Do NOT skip Pass 7 (it is mandatory like Passes 1-6)

title: Create reviewer agent definition
design:
## Goal
Create agents/reviewer.md — the agent prompt that defines how the reviewer agent behaves when dispatched by the lead to verify implementation against the epic spec. Returns a verdict-only output (APPROVED or GAPS FOUND) without flooding the lead context.

## Why Next
The reviewer agent is the second Wave 1 deliverable (independent of executor agent). Both agent definitions must exist before the lead skill rewrite (Wave 2) can reference them. The executor agent (bd-1y1) is complete, so the reviewer is the natural next step.

## Context from Completed Work
bd-1y1 established the agent definition pattern:
- Frontmatter: name, description (with XML examples), model, permissionMode, memory, skills
- Structured sections: Role, Startup Protocol, Execution steps, Message Protocol, Rules
- The executor uses permissionMode: bypassPermissions, model: sonnet, memory: project

## Implementation

### 1. Study the review-implementation skill for behavior to port
Read skills/review-implementation/SKILL.md and extract:
- The 4-step review process (Load epic → Review each task → Report findings → Gate decision)
- Evidence-based review with confidence scores (0.0-1.0)
- Automated code completeness checks (TODOs, stubs, unwrap, ignored tests)
- Dead code and refactoring remnants audit
- Quality gates via test-runner agent
- Test quality audit (tautological test detection)
- Google Fellow-level code quality review
- Success criteria verification with evidence
- Anti-pattern checking
- APPROVED vs GAPS FOUND verdict format

### 2. Study existing agent patterns
Read agents/executor.md (just completed) for the frontmatter and structure pattern.
Read agents/code-reviewer.md for the existing code review agent pattern.
Read agents/test-effectiveness-analyst.md for another review-oriented agent.

### 3. Write the reviewer agent definition
Create agents/reviewer.md with these sections:

**Frontmatter:**
- name: reviewer
- description: with XML examples showing lead dispatching reviewer for epic verification
- model: sonnet
- permissionMode: bypassPermissions (needs to run tests, read files freely)
- memory: project
- skills: testing-anti-patterns, verification-before-completion

**Prompt body — Section a: Role**
- You are a reviewer agent dispatched by the lead to verify implementation against the epic spec
- You apply Google Fellow-level SRE scrutiny
- You return a structured verdict (APPROVED or GAPS FOUND) — nothing else
- You do NOT fix issues — you identify them for the executor to fix

**Prompt body — Section b: Startup Protocol**
1. Receive epic ID in dispatch prompt
2. Read the epic: bd show <epic-id>
3. List all tasks: bd list --parent <epic-id>
4. Extract: Requirements, Success Criteria, Anti-Patterns

**Prompt body — Section c: Review Process**
For each closed task under the epic:
1. Read task spec: bd show <task-id>
2. Run automated checks (TODOs, stubs, unsafe patterns, ignored tests)
3. Run dead code audit (fallback patterns, unused code, deprecation, orphaned tests)
4. Run quality gates via test-runner agent (tests, format, lint)
5. Read actual implementation files with Read tool (NOT just git diff)
6. Code quality review (error handling, safety, clarity, production readiness)
7. Audit new tests for meaningfulness (tautological test detection)
8. Verify each success criterion with evidence
9. Check each anti-pattern (search for prohibited patterns)
10. Record findings with evidence and confidence scores

**Prompt body — Section d: Verdict Format**
APPROVED verdict format:
```
## Implementation Review: APPROVED

### Tasks Reviewed
- <task-id>: <title> — PASS
...

### Evidence Summary
| Criterion | Status | Confidence | Evidence |
|-----------|--------|------------|----------|
...

### Quality Gates
- Tests: PASS (N passed, 0 failed)
- Format: PASS
- Lint: PASS

### Test Quality Audit
- Meaningful tests: N
- Tautological tests: 0
- Weak tests: 0

Recommendation: Ready for manual validation.
```

GAPS FOUND verdict format:
```
## Implementation Review: GAPS FOUND

### Critical Gaps
1. [gap description with evidence]
...

### Important Gaps
1. [gap description with evidence]
...

### Tasks with Issues
- <task-id>: <title> — [specific gap]
...

Recommendation: Fix gaps before proceeding.
```

**Prompt body — Section e: Rules (No Exceptions)**
1. Read actual files, not just git diff
2. Every claim requires evidence (file:line, command output, test name)
3. Findings below 0.8 confidence must be investigated further
4. Tautological tests = GAPS FOUND (not coverage credit)
5. Never approve with unresolved gaps — even small ones
6. Never fix issues — only identify them
7. Always use test-runner agent for quality gates (context preservation)
8. Report verdict to lead via SendMessage, not direct output

### 4. Verify
Read agents/reviewer.md back and confirm:
- [ ] Frontmatter has all fields (name, description with examples, model, permissionMode, memory, skills)
- [ ] Startup protocol specifies exact bd commands
- [ ] Review process covers all 10 steps from review-implementation skill
- [ ] Both verdict formats complete (APPROVED and GAPS FOUND)
- [ ] Evidence table format defined with confidence scores
- [ ] Test quality audit section included
- [ ] Dead code audit section included
- [ ] All rules listed
- [ ] No placeholder text
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Success Criteria
- [ ] agents/reviewer.md exists with complete frontmatter (name, description, model, permissionMode, memory, skills)
- [ ] Startup protocol specifies exact bd commands for epic and task loading
- [ ] Review process covers all steps from review-implementation skill (automated checks, dead code audit, quality gates, file reading, code quality, test audit, criteria verification, anti-pattern checking)
- [ ] APPROVED verdict format defined with evidence table and confidence scores
- [ ] GAPS FOUND verdict format defined with categorized gaps and evidence
- [ ] Test quality audit integrated (tautological test detection)
- [ ] Dead code audit integrated (fallback patterns, unused code, deprecation)
- [ ] Rules section covers all key constraints
- [ ] No placeholder text in any section
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- NO vague review instructions ('review the code' without specifying what to check)
- NO placeholder evidence formats ('[evidence here]' without defining structure)
- NO skipping test quality audit (tautological tests are a critical check)
- NO skipping dead code audit (refactoring remnants must be caught)
- NO approval without confidence scores (every finding needs quantified confidence)
- NO fix instructions in the reviewer (reviewer identifies, executor fixes)
notes:
## Key Considerations (SRE Review)

**Edge Case: Reviewer dispatched as subagent vs teammate**
- Epic open question: 'Whether reviewer agent should be a teammate or a subagent (subagent likely sufficient for one-shot analysis)'
- Decision: Use subagent (Task tool), NOT teammate. Reviewer is one-shot analysis — dispatched, returns verdict, done. No ongoing conversation needed.
- Implication for agent definition: The reviewer sends its verdict as its final output (returned to lead via Task tool result), NOT via SendMessage. Adjust Section e Rule 8 accordingly.
- The description in the frontmatter must include examples showing Task tool dispatch, not TeamCreate.

**Edge Case: Projects without test suites**
- Some projects (like this plugin repo) have no automated tests
- Reviewer must adapt automated checks to what's available
- If no test suite: quality gates section should note 'No automated tests detected' rather than failing
- If project is markdown-only: skip code-specific checks (unwrap, stubs) and focus on content quality

**Edge Case: Documentation-only tasks**
- Some tasks produce only markdown files (like this task itself)
- Reviewer should still verify: no placeholder text, sections complete, content matches spec
- Don't skip review because 'it's just docs' — docs ARE the deliverable in a plugin project

**Edge Case: bd show returns error for a task**
- A task listed under the epic might have been deleted or have corrupted data
- Reviewer should note the error and continue with remaining tasks
- Include error in findings as 'UNABLE TO REVIEW: <task-id> — bd show returned error'

**Edge Case: Context exhaustion during large epic review**
- Epic with 10+ tasks may exhaust reviewer context
- Reviewer should prioritize: review tasks in dependency order, critical tasks first
- If approaching context limits: send partial verdict with what was reviewed and what remains

**Edge Case: No files changed on branch**
- If git diff shows no changes, reviewer should flag this as a gap
- Implementation claims without code changes = GAPS FOUND

**Effort Estimate: 4-6 hours**

**Subagent dispatch pattern (how lead will use this):**
The lead dispatches the reviewer like this:
```
Task tool with subagent_type='general-purpose':
  prompt: 'You are the reviewer agent. Review epic bd-t4i. Follow agents/reviewer.md exactly.'
```
The reviewer's output is returned directly to the lead — no SendMessage needed.
