- NO automatic stability_state promotion

title: Task 5: finishing-a-development-branch — gate verification + hygiene (R10)
description: Step 1 verifies the persisted Verdict: APPROVED marker before integrating; bd dep tree replaced, cargo-test literal generalized, 3 dangling resource links removed
design:
## Goal
finishing-a-development-branch verifies the persisted APPROVED gate before integrating, and its four hygiene defects are fixed.

## Why
Today nothing verifies the end-of-epic reviewer ever ran — finishing integrates on assumed state, the exact class this epic closes. The marker it must check is defined by Task 4 (run after Task 4). Hygiene: `bd dep tree <epic>` prints no children in bd 0.50.3 (verified live), the test instruction hard-codes cargo, and three resource links point into a resources/ directory that has never existed.

## Changes
File: skills/finishing-a-development-branch/SKILL.md (line numbers at v3.25.0; re-locate by quoted text).
- Step 1, immediately after the Announce line (line 49) and before "**Verify all tasks closed:**", insert:

  **Verify the end-of-epic reviewer gate:**
  ```bash
  bd show bd-1    # epic notes must contain the completion gate-state marker
  ```
  The epic's notes must contain a line beginning `Verdict: APPROVED (end-of-epic reviewer` — persisted by executing-plans' Completion (format: `skills/common-patterns/loop-interfaces.md`).

  **If the marker is absent or not APPROVED:**
  ```
  Cannot proceed with bd-1: no APPROVED end-of-epic reviewer gate is recorded on
  the epic. Run executing-plans' Completion first — the end-of-epic reviewer must
  return APPROVED and the gate-state block (with its Verdict: APPROVED marker)
  must be persisted to the epic's bd notes.
  ```
  **STOP. Do not proceed.**

- Replace every `bd dep tree bd-1` with `bd list --parent bd-1` (4 sites, verified by grep at v3.25.0: Step 1 bash block line 54, Step 5 Option 2 "Get epic info" block line 158, example 1 failure code block line 257, example 1 correction line 291; adjust their trailing comments to "# List child tasks" / keep example prose sensible). After this, `grep -n 'bd dep tree'` on the file must return nothing — re-run the grep rather than trusting the enumeration.
- Step 2 dispatch text (lines 81-84): "Run: cargo test\n(or: npm test / pytest / go test ./...)" → "Run: <project's test command>\n(examples: cargo test / npm test / pytest / go test ./...)". The concrete `cargo test` occurrences inside the <examples> blocks (lines 294, and example correction blocks) stay — they are illustrations of a Rust project, not instructions.
- Delete the "**Detailed guides:**" list in <resources> (lines 505-508): the three links (resources/worktree-guide.md, resources/mr-templates.md, resources/bd-mr-integration.md) target files that do not exist. Keep the "**When stuck:**" list.
- quick_reference Step 1 row → "Verify reviewer gate + epic tasks complete | Marker absent or tasks open → STOP".
- <when_to_use> Prerequisites, first bullet → "The end-of-epic reviewer gate in executing-plans' completion must have returned APPROVED (verified in Step 1 via the persisted `Verdict: APPROVED` marker)".
- verification_checklist: add first item "- [ ] End-of-epic reviewer gate marker verified in epic notes (Step 1)".
Commit the change when done.

## Verification
- `grep -c 'Verdict: APPROVED' skills/finishing-a-development-branch/SKILL.md` → ≥ 2 (Step 1 + prerequisites; failure-message block adds more)
- `grep -n 'bd dep tree' skills/finishing-a-development-branch/SKILL.md` → no matches
- `grep -n 'resources/' skills/finishing-a-development-branch/SKILL.md` → no matches
- `grep -n "Run: <project's test command>" skills/finishing-a-development-branch/SKILL.md` → 1
- `grep -n 'STOP. Do not proceed.' skills/finishing-a-development-branch/SKILL.md` → ≥ 3 (new gate + existing two)
- Pre-commit hooks pass on the commit

title: Redesign execution pipeline: hybrid task model with upfront planning
description: Token consumption and context exhaustion in the current execution pipeline. Executors target 4-8 hour tasks, causing them to hit context limits before completing end-of-agent rituals. This redesign introduces a hybrid task model (simple/Haiku 2-10min + medium/Sonnet 10-30min) with all tasks planned upfront, two-stage per-task review (PR stacking model), dynamic model selection, cybernetic escalation when plans prove wrong, and a grand planner for decomposing large initiatives into leaf epics.
design:
## Requirements (IMMUTABLE)
- All tasks planned upfront in bd before execution begins (no iterative one-at-a-time creation)
- Tasks classified as simple (Haiku, 2-10 min) or medium (Sonnet, 10-30 min) — no task exceeds 30 min estimate
- Each task spec is self-contained with Goal, Why, and Boundaries sections
- Executor agent prompt reduced to ~80-100 lines (from current 369)
- Lead dispatches fresh blocking subagent per task with dynamic model selection
- Two-stage per-task review: lead spec review + Haiku code quality review
- Lead can halt execution and escalate to user when plan proves invalid (cybernetic escalation)
- Reviewer agent validates assembled whole against epic at end
- SRE refinement runs once in batch against full plan (not per-task)
- Grand planner skill decomposes large initiatives into leaf epics
- Brainstorming includes sizing gate that escalates to grand planner when scope is too large
- Intuition/architecture analysis moves to grand planner level

## Success Criteria (MUST ALL BE TRUE)
- [ ] Grand planner skill exists and decomposes initiatives into leaf epics with dependencies
- [ ] Brainstorming skill has sizing gate that triggers grand planner escalation
- [ ] Writing-plans produces complete upfront task tree with simple/medium classification
- [ ] Task specs follow the two-tier format (simple: Goal/Why/Changes/Verification; medium: Goal/Why/Context/Implementation/Tests/Verification/Boundaries)
- [ ] SRE refinement supports batch mode against full plan
- [ ] Executing-plans skill rewritten for upfront task list, dynamic model, escalation protocol
- [ ] Executor agent rewritten to ~80-100 lines with minimal context, DONE/BLOCKED/NEEDS_HELP returns
- [ ] Two-stage per-task review implemented (lead spec check + Haiku code quality)
- [ ] Per-epic reviewer validates assembled whole at end
- [ ] Escalation protocol: lead halts, summarizes, recommends, waits for user
- [ ] CLAUDE.md updated to reflect new pipeline
- [ ] All existing skills that reference old execution model updated
- [ ] Plugin version bumped

## Anti-Patterns (FORBIDDEN)
- ❌ NO tasks exceeding 30 min estimate (token efficiency: smaller tasks prevent context exhaustion)
- ❌ NO iterative task creation during execution (predictability: all tasks planned upfront so lead and user can see the full scope)
- ❌ NO project memory protocol in executor (simplicity: tasks are small enough that git history provides cross-task context)
- ❌ NO SRE refinement in executor (separation: SRE runs once at plan level, not per-task)
- ❌ NO autonomous plan redesign by lead (cybernetic: lead escalates to user with recommendation, never decides alone)
- ❌ NO loading full epic into executor context (efficiency: executor gets task spec + why, not the whole epic)
- ❌ NO single-model execution (cost: Haiku for simple tasks, Sonnet for medium — dynamic selection is load-bearing)
- ❌ NO big-bang review only (quality: two-stage per-task review catches drift early, like PR stacking)

## Approach
Hybrid execution model inspired by obra/superpowers subagent-driven-development, adapted for our bd-based workflow. The core insight is that token consumption scales with task granularity — our current 4-8 hour tasks cause context exhaustion; 2-30 minute tasks with dynamic model selection prevent it.

The pipeline becomes: Grand Planner (large initiatives) → Brainstorming (leaf epics) → Writing Plans (all tasks upfront) → SRE Batch Review → Executing Plans (dispatch per task) → Two-Stage Review Per Task → Epic Review. Planning absorbs ambiguity so execution can be cheap and focused.

A two-tier planning model handles scale: the grand planner decomposes large work into leaf epics, each independently brainstormable and executable. Brainstorming includes a sizing gate that escalates to grand planner when scope exceeds ~10 tasks or 3-4 components.

## Architecture
**New components:**
- Grand planner skill (skills/grand-planner/SKILL.md) — decomposes initiatives into leaf epics
- Grand planner command (commands/grand-plan.md)

**Rewritten components:**
- Executing-plans skill — upfront task list orchestration, dynamic model, escalation
- Executor agent — minimal ~80-100 line prompt, DONE/BLOCKED/NEEDS_HELP returns
- Writing-plans skill — produces complete upfront task tree with simple/medium classification

**Modified components:**
- Brainstorming skill — add sizing gate, simplify for grand-planner-provided requirements, remove intuition routing (moved to grand planner)
- SRE refinement skill — add batch mode for full plan review
- Reviewer agent — focus on assembled-whole validation
- Using-hyper skill — update workflow descriptions

**Data flow:**
Grand Planner → creates leaf epics in bd with dependencies
Brainstorming → creates tasks within leaf epic (all upfront)
Writing-plans → expands task specs to self-contained format
SRE batch → reviews full plan, suggests splits/additions
Executing-plans lead → reads task list, classifies, dispatches executors
Executor → reads task spec, implements, commits, returns status
Lead → spec review (reads diff vs spec)
Haiku reviewer → code quality review per task
Epic reviewer → validates assembled whole at end

## Architecture Impact
(Result of Step 4 Architecture Impact Check)

- [x] Creates a new component/module — YES (grand planner skill)
- [x] Changes public interface of existing component — YES (brainstorming, executing-plans, executor all rewritten)
- [x] Adds/removes cross-component dependency — YES (grand planner ↔ brainstorming escalation)
- [x] Creates new request path through 2+ components — YES (grand planner → brainstorming → planning → SRE batch → execution → two-stage review)
- [x] Moves responsibility between components — YES (intuition to grand planner; SRE from per-task to batch; task creation from executing-plans to planning)

Result: 5 boxes checked. /intuition was offered and deferred — user chose to proceed given thorough Socratic exploration of the design.

## Design Rationale

### Problem
The current execution model targets 4-8 hour tasks, causing executors to exhaust context before completing end-of-agent rituals. This wastes tokens (re-dispatching to finish rituals) and degrades quality (format drift at 55% rate). The pipeline needs to produce smaller tasks with less executor overhead.

### Research Findings
**Codebase:**
- agents/executor.md — 369 lines with TDD lifecycle, project memory protocol, structured output contract, multiple gates. Known format drift issue (bd-0he)
- skills/executing-plans/SKILL.md — 336 lines, lead orchestrates blocking subagent per task, validates complex output envelopes, runs SRE between tasks
- skills/sre-task-refinement/SKILL.md — 890 lines, enforces 4-8 hour task granularity (auto-reject if >16h)
- skills/writing-plans/SKILL.md — 479 lines, expands tasks with detail but doesn't set granularity

**External:**
- obra/superpowers subagent-driven-development — 2-5 minute micro-tasks with complete executable code, fresh subagent per task, context isolation is the key token optimization, two-stage review (spec + code quality), dynamic model selection per task complexity

### Key Decisions Made

| Question | User Answer | Implication |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Primary pain point? | Token cost + context exhaustion (executors hit limit before completion) | Task granularity must shrink dramatically |
| Detailed planning vs lighter executors? | Explore both | Led to hybrid approach |
| Executor as target for reduction? | Yes, but too early to decide specifics; interested in dynamic model choice | Executor redesign is in scope; model selection is load-bearing |
| Full pipeline scope? | Yes — brainstorming through reviewer | All components subject to change |
| Redesign escalation behavior? | Escalate to user (cybernetic); lead provides recommendation | No autonomous redesign decisions |
| Model choice? | Dynamic: Haiku for simple, Sonnet for medium | Lead classifies task complexity |
| Two-tier planning? | Yes — grand planner + leaf epics, gate in brainstorming | New grand planner skill needed |
| Brainstorming when called from grand planner? | Assess requirement confidence and adapt | Not rigid modes; brainstorming is flexible |
| Per-task review depth? | Spec + code quality per task (PR stacking model) | Two-stage review per task, epic review at end |
| Task spec context? | Include 'Why' section so executor understands purpose | Prevents locally-correct but globally-wrong decisions |
| Intuition/architecture? | Moves to grand planner level | Brainstorming simplified |

### Approaches Considered

#### 1. Hybrid with Adaptive Task Sizing (chosen)

**What it is:** Planning phase classifies deliverables as simple (2-10 min, Haiku) or medium (10-30 min, Sonnet). Each task spec is self-contained with a Why section for context. Two-stage per-task review (spec + code quality). Grand planner handles large initiatives.

**Investigation:**
- Researched superpowers execution model — confirmed micro-tasks + context isolation are the key token optimization
- Analyzed current executor — 369 lines of overhead per task, most of it unnecessary for small tasks
- Confirmed format drift root cause: executors run too long, accumulate too much context

**Pros:**
- Optimizes cost per task (right model for right complexity)
- Handles full spectrum from trivial to moderately complex
- Two-stage review catches drift early (PR stacking model)
- Grand planner scales to large initiatives

**Cons:**
- Most complex to implement (lead classifies tasks, dynamic model selection)
- Risk of misclassification (task harder than expected)

**Chosen because:** Real epics contain a mix of trivial and complex deliverables. The hybrid model gets superpowers-style efficiency for simple tasks while allowing Sonnet judgment for medium tasks. The grand planner handles the upper bound of complexity.

#### 2. Superpowers-Style Micro-Tasks (rejected)

**What it is:** Port superpowers model directly. All tasks 2-5 minutes with complete executable code in the spec. Executor is a pure typist.

**Why explored:** Superpowers achieves minimal token consumption with this model.

**Investigation:**
- Fetched and analyzed obra/superpowers subagent-driven-development skill
- Model produces excellent token efficiency for codebases where Opus can write complete code during planning

**Pros:**
- Minimum token per executor (~1-2k context)
- Natural retry and parallelization boundary
- No context exhaustion possible

**Cons:**
- Planning is very expensive (Opus writing complete code for every task)
- Plans become stale if early tasks reveal surprises
- Executor can't self-correct — just a typist
- Doesn't handle medium-complexity work well (30 min tasks don't fit 2-5 min micro-task model)

**REJECTED BECAUSE:** Forces all work into micro-tasks, which means Opus must write complete code during planning. For medium-complexity tasks (new components, cross-file changes), this shifts cost rather than reducing it. The hybrid model uses micro-tasks where they fit and allows Sonnet judgment where needed.

**DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Token cost analysis shows planning phase is cheaper than expected even for medium tasks, or Haiku becomes capable enough to handle medium tasks.

#### 3. Medium-Granularity with Lighter Executors (rejected)

**What it is:** Keep design-level planning but shrink tasks from 4-8h to 15-30 min. Reduce executor prompt but keep the same executor model for all tasks.

**Why explored:** Simpler to implement — no task classification, no dynamic model selection.

**Investigation:**
- Analyzed current executor overhead (369 lines, multiple gates, project memory)
- Estimated token savings from lighter executor alone

**Pros:**
- Simpler implementation (no task classification logic)
- Executors retain judgment capability
- Less planning overhead

**Cons:**
- Still uses Sonnet for trivial tasks (renames, config changes)
- No cost optimization for task complexity
- Some risk of context exhaustion on 30-min tasks without two-stage review

**REJECTED BECAUSE:** Missing dynamic model selection leaves significant cost savings on the table. Many tasks in a typical epic are simple enough for Haiku. Single-model execution is explicitly an anti-pattern in the chosen approach.

**DO NOT REVISIT UNLESS:** Dynamic model selection proves too complex to implement reliably, or Haiku/Sonnet cost gap narrows significantly.

### Scope Boundaries

**In scope:**
- Grand planner skill (new)
- Executing-plans skill (rewrite)
- Executor agent (rewrite)
- Writing-plans skill (modify for upfront task tree + classification)
- Brainstorming skill (add sizing gate, simplify when called from grand planner)
- SRE refinement (add batch mode)
- Reviewer agent (refocus on assembled-whole)
- Using-hyper (update workflow descriptions)
- CLAUDE.md (update pipeline documentation)
- Commands for new skills

**Out of scope:**
- Consider skill — not part of the execution pipeline; unchanged
- Intuition skill internals — only the routing changes (called from grand planner instead of brainstorming); internal passes unchanged
- Ponder skill — architecture model management unchanged
- Debugging/bug-fixing skills — separate workflow, not affected
- Refactoring skills — separate workflow, not affected
- Hook system — no hooks affected by this change

### Open Questions
- What's the right name for the grand planner skill? (working name: "grand-planner")
- Should the grand planner produce leaf epic requirements as bd epic descriptions or as a separate artifact?
- What's the exact threshold for the brainstorming sizing gate? (~10 tasks / ~3-4 components is a starting heuristic, may need tuning)
- Should simple task specs include complete code, or is "exact change description" sufficient for Haiku?
- How should the lead handle a task that was classified as simple but the Haiku executor returns NEEDS_HELP? (re-dispatch as medium/Sonnet? or clarify and retry?)
close_reason: Execution pipeline redesigned: hybrid task model with upfront planning, dynamic model selection, two-stage review. 8 tasks, 10 commits. Preordain skill for initiative decomposition.

title: Task 6: Create grand-planner skill and command
description: Create skills/grand-planner/SKILL.md and commands/grand-plan.md. The grand planner decomposes large initiatives into leaf epics with dependencies, handles initiative-level architecture analysis, and produces output that brainstorming can consume.
design:
## Goal
Create the grand-planner skill and its slash command. This is a new component that decomposes large initiatives into leaf epics, each independently brainstormable and executable.

## Why
The brainstorming sizing gate (Step 6a) escalates to /grand-plan when scope exceeds ~10 tasks or 3-4 components. That command and skill don't exist yet. The grand planner also absorbs initiative-level architecture analysis (intuition routing) that was moved up from brainstorming.

## Effort Estimate
20-30 minutes (create new skill ~150-200 lines, create command file ~10 lines)

## Context
- Brainstorming sizing gate references /grand-plan (Step 6a in skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md)
- Intuition/architecture analysis moved to grand planner level per epic design
- Grand planner produces leaf epics in bd with dependencies
- Each leaf epic gets its own brainstorm→plan→execute cycle
- All leaf epics can contribute to a single branch/MR
- The epic design says: "Grand Planner → creates leaf epics in bd with dependencies"

## Implementation guidance
1. Create skills/grand-planner/SKILL.md with standard skill structure:

   ### Frontmatter
   name: grand-planner
   description: Decomposes large initiatives into leaf epics with dependencies; handles initiative-level architecture analysis

   ### Overview
   - Takes a large initiative that exceeds a single leaf epic's capacity
   - Decomposes into independent leaf epics, each small enough for brainstorm→execute
   - Structures dependencies between leaf epics
   - Handles initiative-level /intuition (architecture analysis)

   ### Process (suggested steps):
   Step 1 — Understanding the Initiative: Socratic questioning (like brainstorming Step 1) to understand the full scope. Use AskUserQuestion.
   Step 2 — Research: Dispatch codebase-investigator and/or internet-researcher to understand current state
   Step 3 — Decomposition: Break the initiative into leaf epics. Each leaf epic should:
     - Be independently brainstormable (has its own clear requirements)
     - Be independently executable (doesn't need concurrent work in another epic)
     - Target ~5-10 tasks (within brainstorming's sizing gate threshold)
     - Have clear boundaries (what's in scope, what belongs to adjacent epics)
   Step 4 — Architecture Impact Check at initiative level: Same 5 questions as brainstorming Step 4, but applied to the entire initiative. Offer /intuition if any YES.
   Step 5 — Create leaf epics in bd: Create each leaf epic with:
     - Requirements (what this leaf epic must achieve)
     - Boundaries (what belongs here vs. adjacent epics)
     - Dependencies on other leaf epics (ordering)
   Step 6 — Handoff: Route each leaf epic to brainstorming. The user can brainstorm and execute them sequentially or pick the order.

   ### Key principles:
   - Each leaf epic must be independently brainstormable and executable
   - Dependencies between leaf epics should be sequential, not circular
   - Architecture analysis happens at initiative level, not per-leaf-epic (unless brainstorming detects new friction)
   - The grand planner doesn't create tasks — that's brainstorming's job
   - The grand planner DOES create epics — that's its core deliverable

   ### Critical rules:
   - Use AskUserQuestion for all clarifying questions
   - Research before decomposing (dispatch agents)
   - Each leaf epic must be small enough to pass brainstorming's sizing gate
   - Offer /intuition at initiative level
   - Cybernetic: user confirms decomposition before creating epics

2. Create commands/grand-plan.md:
   - Follow existing command file format (check commands/execute-plan.md or commands/brainstorm.md for pattern)
   - Invoke the grand-planner skill

## Anti-Patterns
- ❌ NO creating tasks within leaf epics — grand planner creates epics, brainstorming creates tasks
- ❌ NO skipping research — dispatch agents before decomposing
- ❌ NO circular dependencies between leaf epics
- ❌ NO leaf epics that would trigger the sizing gate again (each must be ~5-10 tasks)

## Edge Cases

**Initiative is actually small enough for a single epic:**
- Grand planner recognizes this and routes back to brainstorming directly
- "This initiative fits in a single epic. Routing to /brainstorm."

**Leaf epics have strong ordering dependencies:**
- Create blocking dependencies in bd: later epic depends on earlier epic
- Document which interfaces/artifacts the later epic depends on

**User arrives via brainstorming sizing gate escalation:**
- Grand planner receives the brainstorming work done so far as context
- Doesn't re-ask questions that brainstorming already answered

**Initiative spans multiple codebases/repos:**
- Out of scope — note this and suggest the user manage cross-repo coordination manually

## Success Criteria
- [ ] skills/grand-planner/SKILL.md exists with standard skill structure (frontmatter, overview, process, rules, integration)
- [ ] Process covers: understand initiative, research, decompose into leaf epics, architecture impact check, create epics in bd, handoff to brainstorming
- [ ] Leaf epic creation includes requirements, boundaries, and dependencies
- [ ] Architecture Impact Check at initiative level with /intuition offer
- [ ] commands/grand-plan.md exists and invokes the skill
- [ ] Skill handles escalation from brainstorming sizing gate (receives prior work as context)
- [ ] Anti-patterns prevent grand planner from creating tasks (only epics)
close_reason: Grand planner skill (300 lines) and command created. All 7 criteria verified. Commit 7f9c9c8.

title: Task 2: executing-plans — Stage-1 commit-landed check + range diff + truncation tripwire (R2, R3)
description: Stage 1 verifies DONE hash ancestry + clean tree before reviewing git diff <base>..HEAD; spec checks reject truncation markers; rationalizations entry added
design:
## Goal
Stage 1 verifies the executor's DONE-declared commit actually landed (hash in history + clean tree) before reviewing the committed range, and spec verification rejects truncation/placeholder markers as missing specs.

## Why
An executor that forgets to commit is currently invisible: Stage 1 reviews `git diff <base-SHA>` — a working-tree diff that shows uncommitted work as if it landed — so the lead approves work that exists nowhere in history. Separately, a spec containing "[Remaining steps truncated]" passes the non-empty check and causes incomplete implementations. Task 3 rewrites the region directly after this one; the end-of-epic reviewer gate assumes per-task commits are real.

## Context
Files: skills/executing-plans/SKILL.md (primary; read whole — Task 1 has already landed, so re-locate by quoted text) and skills/common-patterns/common-rationalizations.md (one row).
- Stage 1 block: under "### DONE", "**Stage 1 — Lead epic-coherence check:**" followed by a bash block containing `git diff <base-SHA>` and a two-line comment about multi-commit executors.
- The DONE contract (consumed, not edited): `DONE: <commit-hash> — <summary>`, hash at fixed position immediately after "DONE: " — defined in skills/common-patterns/loop-interfaces.md (Verdict Contracts).
- Startup spec check: "**Verify:** Every task has a non-empty spec (design field)."
- Pre-dispatch Verification item 1: "Task has a non-empty spec (design field)."
- Templates region: "**Stage 1 feedback template**" — the new template goes right after it.
- Example 1 (~270-283): "Stage 1: git diff a1b2c3d → no anti-pattern violations…"
- Critical rule 4 mentions "lead epic-coherence check against the recorded base SHA".
- common-rationalizations.md "## Planning Shortcuts" table (~rows 40-51).

## Implementation
1. Rewrite the Stage 1 opening. Before any diff is read, the commit-landed check:
   ```bash
   git merge-base --is-ancestor <done-hash> HEAD   # exit 0 = the DONE hash landed
   git status --porcelain                          # must print nothing (clean tree)
   ```
   If the hash is not in HEAD's history OR the tree is dirty: do NOT review — re-dispatch using the commit-landed failure template (step 2). Any non-zero exit from the ancestry check counts as not-landed — including a malformed or truncated hash ("fatal: Not a valid object name"); it is a contract failure, not a tooling problem. Untracked files count as a dirty tree (`git status --porcelain` prints `??` lines). Only after both checks pass, review:
   ```bash
   git diff <base-SHA>..HEAD
   ```
   with a comment noting the range diff reads exactly what was committed — the clean-tree check has already ruled out uncommitted leftovers. Remove the old `git diff <base-SHA>` block; keep the existing epic-coherence guidance (anti-patterns, watering-down, contradictions) intact after it.
2. Add after the Stage 1 feedback template:
   **Commit-landed failure template** (DONE's hash missing from history, or dirty tree):
   ```
   Re-execute this task. The prior run returned DONE but the work did not land:
   <the DONE hash <hash> is not in history / the working tree has uncommitted changes>.
   Check git log and git status, complete any remaining work, commit everything
   for this task, and return DONE: <commit-hash> — <summary> with the real hash.

   Task: <bd-task-id>

   Task spec:
   <paste task spec>

   Working directory: <pwd>
   Branch: <working branch>
   ```
3. Extend the Startup "**Verify:**" paragraph: a spec containing bracketed elision/placeholder markers — e.g. "[Remaining steps truncated]", "[see parent]", "[TBD]", "[detailed above]" — is a missing spec. Route it through writing-plans exactly like an empty design field.
4. Pre-dispatch item 1 → "Task has a non-empty spec (design field), free of truncation/placeholder markers." Also fix the sentence directly below that list: "If either check fails, skip the task and note why." → "If any check fails, skip the task and note why." (the list now has three checks; wording drift flagged in review of the prior task).
5. quick_reference Stage 1 row "How" → "`git merge-base --is-ancestor <hash> HEAD` + `git diff <base-SHA>..HEAD`".
6. Example 1: change the Stage 1 line to "Stage 1: hash 3f9a1b2 in history ✓, tree clean ✓, git diff a1b2c3d..HEAD → no anti-pattern violations, requirements intact, no conflict with other completed tasks. ✓".
7. Critical rule 4: append "(commit-landed check, then diff `<base-SHA>..HEAD`)" to the Stage 1 description.

title: Create volatility-decomposition skill (SKILL.md + command)
design:
## Goal
Create the volatility-decomposition skill that guides architects through Löwy's volatility-based decomposition via Socratic dialogue, producing 2-3 alternative graph structures encoded in bd, with key decisions captured as ADRs.

## Effort Estimate
6-10 hours (documentation-heavy: ~400 line SKILL.md + examples + ADR template + command file)

## Implementation

### 1. Study existing skill patterns
Read these files to internalize the structure and conventions:
- skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md — closest analog (Socratic, generative, produces bd artifacts). This is the primary reference.
- skills/writing-plans/SKILL.md — another skill that creates bd artifacts with detailed implementation steps
- commands/brainstorm.md — command file pattern (frontmatter + one-line instruction)
- skills/common-patterns/bd-commands.md — standard bd command examples to reference
- skills/common-patterns/common-anti-patterns.md — shared anti-patterns to reference
- skills/common-patterns/common-rationalizations.md — shared rationalizations to reference

### 2. Create skills/volatility-decomposition/SKILL.md

Follow the standard skill structure exactly:

```yaml
---
name: volatility-decomposition
description: Löwy volatility-based decomposition producing architecture graph in bd with ADRs
---
```

Required sections (in order):
1. `<skill_overview>` — 1-2 sentences: what and why
2. `<rigidity_level>` — HIGH FREEDOM (Socratic questions adapt to domain, but output structure is rigid: always 2-3 alternatives, always bd encoding, always ADRs)
3. `<quick_reference>` — table mapping steps to actions and deliverables
4. `<when_to_use>` — triggers: project inception, major inflection points, when structure fights you, fractal sub-decomposition of large components. NOT for: inner-loop feature work, bug fixes, refactoring
5. `<the_process>` — Steps 0-5 detailed below
6. `<examples>` — 4 examples detailed below
7. `<critical_rules>` — behavioral constraints
8. `<verification_checklist>` — evidence-based completion check
9. `<integration>` — caller/callee relationships and agents
10. `<resources>` — links to common patterns, Löwy references, ADR template

### 3. The Process — Step-by-step content

**Step 0 — Context detection:**
```bash
# Check for existing architecture epic
bd list --label arch --type epic --status open --json

# If found: load it + all component nodes (REVISION MODE)
bd show <epic-id> --json
bd list --label arch,component --parent <epic-id> --json

# If not found: INCEPTION MODE (starting fresh)
```
- If codebase exists: dispatch codebase-investigator with prompt: 'Map the current module/package structure, dependency graph between top-level modules, and any existing architectural boundaries. Stay at module level, not class/function level.'
- If no codebase: pure design mode — all evidence comes from Socratic dialogue

**Step 1 — Socratic questioning (Löwy framework):**
Use AskUserQuestion tool for ALL questions. Do not print questions and wait.

Question families (adapt to domain, but these are the core probes):

Forces discovery:
- 'What are the 3-5 major forces acting on this system? What pushes it to change?'
- 'Which of these forces are correlated (change together) vs independent?'

Volatility identification (per force):
- 'If you had to replace [X], what else would have to change?' (isolation test)
- 'How often does [X] change? Monthly? Yearly? Never?'
- 'Is this volatility (needs architectural encapsulation) or variability (handled with code)?'

Customer axis (Löwy's second dimension):
- 'Do different customers/user types need different [X]?'
- 'Will new customer segments require new behaviors here?'

Layer assignment:
- 'Is [component] orchestrating workflow (Manager), computing business logic (Engine), accessing data/external systems (Resource Accessor), or providing cross-cutting capability (Utility)?'
- Challenge: 'You said this is an Engine, but it accesses the database directly — should that be separated into a Resource Accessor?'

For REVISION MODE, shift to:
- 'What about the current structure feels wrong? Where is it fighting you?'
- 'If you restructure [X], what happens to its interfaces with [Y] and [Z]?'
- 'Does splitting [X] create genuinely independent axes, or will the halves still change together?'

**Step 2 — Structure proposal:**
Present 2-3 alternative decompositions. NEVER present just one.

Each proposal must include:
- Node list: name, responsibility (1 sentence), volatility axis (what it encapsulates), Löwy layer
- Edge list: which nodes have interface dependencies (blocks), which interact but don't block (relates_to)
- What this decomposition optimizes for (e.g., 'maximizes isolation of payment volatility')
- What it trades off (e.g., 'more components = more interfaces to maintain')

Format:
```
### Proposal A: [Name — what it optimizes for]

| Node | Responsibility | Volatility Axis | Layer |
|------|---------------|-----------------|-------|
| ... | ... | ... | Manager/Engine/RA/Utility |

Edges:
- [Node A] blocks [Node B] (interface: ...)
- [Node C] relates_to [Node D] (interaction: ...)

Optimizes for: [what]
Trades off: [what]

### Proposal B: [Name — what it optimizes for]
...
```

After presenting, ask: 'Which structure best matches your understanding of the system's volatility? You can also combine elements.'

**Step 3 — Encode in bd:**
After architect chooses:

```bash
# Create architecture epic
bd create '[System Name] Architecture' --type epic --label arch \
  --design '## System Purpose
[1-2 sentences from Socratic dialogue]

## Löwy Layer Map
- Manager: [components]
- Engine: [components]
- Resource Accessor: [components]
- Utility: [components]

## Global Constraints
[From Socratic dialogue — cross-cutting rules]'

# Create component nodes (one per component)
bd create '[Component Name]' --type feature --label arch,component \
  --parent <epic-id> \
  --design '## Volatility Axis
[What this component encapsulates — which force/change it isolates]

## Layer: [Manager|Engine|Resource Accessor|Utility]
[Why this layer — what it does and doesn't do]

## Interface Contract
- IN: [operations this component exposes]
- OUT: [operations this component calls on others]

## Responsibility
[1-2 sentences — what it does, what it does NOT do]'

# Set initial stability state
bd set-state <component-id> stability=exploring \
  --reason 'initial decomposition, not yet audited'

# Create edges
bd dep add <downstream> <upstream> --type blocks  # interface dependency
bd relate <node-a> <node-b>                        # non-blocking interaction
```

**Step 4 — Create ADRs:**
For each significant decomposition decision, guide the architect through creating an ADR.

The skill prompts the architect with a draft and asks for validation:
```
I'll draft an ADR for this decision. Review and I'll create the file:

# ADR-NNN: [Decision Title]

## Status
Accepted

## Context
[Forces from Socratic dialogue, neutral language]

## Decision
We will [active voice, full sentences].

## Consequences
- [Positive consequence]
- [Negative consequence / tradeoff]
- [What this enables or constrains for future work]
```

After architect approves, create the file using Write tool:
- Location: doc/arch/adr-NNN.md (create doc/arch/ directory if needed)
- Number sequentially (check existing ADRs first)
- ADRs to create: one per major boundary decision, one per significant tradeoff accepted

**Step 5 — Handoff guidance:**
```
## Graph Summary
[Table of nodes with stability status]

## Recommended Next Steps
- Run /audit-arch to stress-test this decomposition for hidden complection
- [List nodes that might already be stable candidates if simple/obvious]
- After audit: resolve or accept tensions, then stable nodes feed /brainstorm
```

### 4. Create commands/decompose.md
```markdown
---
description: Löwy volatility-based decomposition producing architecture graph in bd with ADRs
---

Use the hyperpowers:volatility-decomposition skill exactly as written
```

### 5. Create skills/common-patterns/adr-template.md
Content should include:
- Nygard format explanation (Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences)
- Status values: proposed, accepted, deprecated, superseded
- Three example ADRs:
  1. Decomposition decision: 'We will isolate [X] because [volatility axis]'
  2. Tension resolution: 'We will [structural change] to resolve [tension]'
  3. Tension acceptance: 'We accept [tension] because [reasoning]. Revisit if [condition].'
- Guidance: consequences of one ADR become context for subsequent ADRs
- File naming: doc/arch/adr-NNN.md, sequential, never reuse numbers

## Examples to Write (4 required)

### Example 1: Good decomposition with clear volatility axes
- Scenario: Developer decomposes an order processing system
- Shows: proper Socratic questioning, 2-3 proposals, architect chooses, bd encoding, ADR creation
- Demonstrates: each node names its volatility axis, layer rules respected, interfaces explicit

### Example 2: BAD — Single recommendation instead of alternatives
- Scenario: Developer proposes one decomposition and says 'this is the right structure'
- Shows: why_it_fails (architect denied agency, missed better options, no tradeoff analysis)
- Correction: present 2-3 alternatives with tradeoffs, let architect choose

### Example 3: BAD — Non-independent volatility axes (functional decomposition in disguise)
- Scenario: Developer creates 'UserService', 'OrderService', 'ProductService' — organized by business function, not volatility
- Shows: why_it_fails (these are functional buckets, not volatility encapsulation — User and Order both change when 'adding subscription support')
- Correction: identify the actual independent axes (payment volatility, fulfillment volatility, pricing rules volatility) and encapsulate those

### Example 4: Good revision of an existing graph
- Scenario: After audit found tension, architect re-invokes decompose to restructure
- Shows: loading existing graph, targeted Socratic questions about the restructuring, proposing 2-3 revision options, updating bd nodes, creating ADR for the structural change

## Key Behavioral Constraints to Encode in Critical Rules
- ALWAYS present 2-3 alternatives, NEVER single recommendations
- Every node MUST name its volatility axis explicitly ('this encapsulates changes in [X]')
- Challenge when two nodes change for the same reason (independence violation)
- Challenge when interfaces carry more than one concern (leaky abstraction)
- Enforce Löwy layer rules: no upward deps, Managers have no business logic, Engines don't access resources directly
- Scale-agnostic vocabulary: adapt 'component/module/service' to context
- Think in components and boundaries, NOT classes and functions
- Distinguish volatility (architectural) from variability (code-level) — only volatility drives decomposition

## Key Considerations (ADDED BY SRE REVIEW)

**Edge Case: Pure Greenfield (no codebase, no existing graph)**
- Step 0 finds nothing — no epic, no codebase
- Skill must work entirely from Socratic dialogue
- All evidence comes from architect's domain knowledge
- Questions should be more exploratory: 'Tell me about this system. What does it do? Who uses it?'
- MUST NOT skip Step 0 and assume greenfield — always check first

**Edge Case: Revision Mode (existing graph)**
- Step 0 loads existing epic + nodes
- Socratic questions shift to: 'What feels wrong? Where is the structure fighting you?'
- Proposals are revisions, not replacements — may affect only 2-3 nodes
- Must update existing nodes, not create duplicates
- Use bd supersede if a node is being replaced, not just edited
- Create ADR for the structural change explaining why the revision was needed

**Edge Case: Architect Wants to Skip to Encoding**
- Architect says 'I already know the decomposition, just encode it'
- Skill should still ask key validation questions:
  - 'Can you name the volatility axis for each component?' (if they can't, the decomposition isn't volatility-based)
  - 'Are any two of these components likely to change for the same reason?' (independence check)
- If answers are solid, proceed to encoding. If not, push back gently.

**Edge Case: Architect Gives Minimal Answers**
- Some architects answer with one word or 'yes/no'
- Skill should provide concrete follow-up options via AskUserQuestion
- Never print open-ended questions and wait — always provide multiple-choice with an 'Other' option

**Edge Case: bd set-state Not Available**
- bd is evolving fast — set-state may not exist in all versions
- Fallback: use labels for stability tracking (label: arch:exploring, arch:stable)
- Document the fallback in the skill

**Edge Case: Fractal Sub-Decomposition**
- Architect runs /decompose on a component that's already in an existing architecture graph
- Must create sub-architecture within the existing node
- Sub-components are children of the component node (hierarchical IDs: bd-a3f8.1)
- Architecture of the sub-decomposition follows same rules (layers, volatility axes, ADRs)

## Anti-Patterns for This Task
- ❌ NO writing the skill from memory — must study brainstorming/SKILL.md first to match structure
- ❌ NO skipping examples — all 4 examples are required (skills without examples get ignored)
- ❌ NO vague example corrections — corrections must show exact corrected output
- ❌ NO placeholder text in any section ('[detailed above]', '[will be added]')
- ❌ NO writing the ADR template without concrete examples (abstract templates are useless)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] skills/volatility-decomposition/SKILL.md exists with complete skill structure (all 10 sections listed in Implementation step 2)
- [ ] commands/decompose.md exists with frontmatter + single-line invocation (matching commands/brainstorm.md pattern)
- [ ] skills/common-patterns/adr-template.md exists with Nygard format + 3 concrete example ADRs
- [ ] Process includes all 6 steps (0: context detection, 1: Socratic questioning, 2: structure proposal, 3: bd encoding, 4: ADR creation, 5: handoff guidance)
- [ ] Step 1 uses AskUserQuestion tool exclusively (never prints questions and waits)
- [ ] Step 2 presents exactly 2-3 alternatives with tradeoffs table format, NEVER a single recommendation
- [ ] Step 3 shows exact bd create commands with all flags (--type, --label, --parent, --design)
- [ ] Step 4 shows ADR drafting + Write tool file creation pattern
- [ ] All 4 examples written with scenario/code/why_it_fails/correction structure
- [ ] Example 3 specifically demonstrates functional decomposition anti-pattern (organized by business function, not volatility)
- [ ] Critical rules section encodes all 8 behavioral constraints listed above
- [ ] Verification checklist includes: 'every node names its volatility axis', 'all alternatives presented', 'ADRs created for key decisions'
- [ ] Edge cases documented: greenfield, revision mode, skip-to-encoding, minimal answers, bd fallback, fractal sub-decomposition
- [ ] Skill tested with subagent: give subagent scenario 'decompose a notification system for a SaaS product', verify it produces 2-3 alternatives (not 1), encodes in bd format, and drafts an ADR
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

title: Task 3: Decouple model from classification in writing-plans and sre-task-refinement
design:

title: Task 6: writing-plans seam — startup-only routing claim, gate stated on both sides
description: Fix writing-plans' mid-execution integration claim to match executing-plans' startup-only routing; state the approval-gate consequence on both sides (R7)
design:
## Goal
writing-plans' integration claims match executing-plans' actual routing: executing-plans routes spec-less tasks to writing-plans at STARTUP only; mid-flight gap-fixes are authored inline by the lead. Both sides state the approval-gate relationship: writing-plans carries a mandatory per-expansion user-approval gate, which is why it never runs mid-flight.

## Why
Epic bd-k9aa R7. writing-plans claims it is called by executing-plans mid-execution ("gap-fix path — a task surfaces mid-execution without a complete spec", L478; "A reviewer surfaced a gap-fix task without a complete spec", L39), but executing-plans routes to writing-plans only at startup (L32, L53) and authors gap-fix specs inline at its GAPS FOUND path (L253). If a session believed writing-plans' claim, its mandatory AskUserQuestion gate (L135-159) would insert user-approval stops into executing-plans' autonomous loop.

## Changes
All sites verified 2026-07-08. NOTE: Task 3 (bd-qn3z) edits other parts of executing-plans first — locate these sites by quoted text, not line number.

1. skills/writing-plans/SKILL.md L39: replace the symptom bullet "A reviewer surfaced a gap-fix task without a complete spec" with "Executing-plans startup verification found tasks missing specs (routed here before execution begins)".
2. skills/writing-plans/SKILL.md L478: replace "hyperpowers:executing-plans (gap-fix path — a task surfaces mid-execution without a complete spec)" with "hyperpowers:executing-plans (startup routing only — spec-less tasks found during startup verification, before execution begins; mid-flight gap-fixes are authored inline by the lead per executing-plans' GAPS FOUND path, because this skill's per-expansion approval gate must not interrupt the execution loop)".
3. skills/executing-plans/SKILL.md L32 (when_to_use): extend "...route the spec-less tasks through writing-plans (the off-mainline spec repair utility) first." with " Writing-plans carries a mandatory per-expansion user-approval gate — routing happens at startup only, never mid-flight."
4. skills/executing-plans/SKILL.md L53 (Startup): extend "...route the spec-less tasks through writing-plans (the off-mainline spec repair utility)." with " Its per-expansion approval gate runs before execution starts — never route to it mid-flight."

## Verification
- `grep -n "mid-execution" skills/writing-plans/SKILL.md` → zero matches
- `grep -n "gap-fix path" skills/writing-plans/SKILL.md` → zero matches
- `grep -n "approval gate\|approval-gate" skills/writing-plans/SKILL.md skills/executing-plans/SKILL.md` → at least one match per file at the seam sites
- executing-plans' GAPS FOUND path (L253 area) unchanged: `git diff` for that section shows no edits
- Pre-commit hooks pass on the task's commit

## Boundaries
- Only modify skills/writing-plans/SKILL.md and skills/executing-plans/SKILL.md at the four sites listed in Implementation
- Leave writing-plans frontmatter description unchanged: its phrasing (gap-fix tasks, mid-flight amendments, externally created tasks) describes user-invoked repair scenarios, not the executing-plans seam — verified consistent at SRE review 2026-07-08
- Do not touch executing-plans dispatch templates or closure lines (Task 3 bd-qn3z owns them)

title: Task 1: Restructure executor commit flow and message format
design:
## Goal
Modify agents/executor.md to move commit from per-deliverable (inside TDD cycle) to per-task (gate before bd close), and update the task completion message format to include commit hashes.

## Effort Estimate
1-2 hours (markdown-only edits to a single file)

## Implementation

### 1. Study existing code
- agents/executor.md:58-91 — TDD cycle section ("2. Execute with TDD") containing Steps 1-6
- agents/executor.md:83-91 — Step 6 (Commit) inside TDD cycle — THIS IS WHAT WE REMOVE
- agents/executor.md:93-98 — Section "3. Verify completeness"
- agents/executor.md:100-106 — Section "4. Close the task" — commit gate goes BEFORE this
- agents/executor.md:131-159 — Message Protocol > Task Completion Message — add Commits section here
- agents/executor.md:242-261 — "Rules (No Exceptions)" section — add anti-skip rule here

### 2. Remove commit from TDD cycle
In the "2. Execute with TDD" section (line 56), remove Step 6 (Commit) at lines 83-91. The TDD cycle becomes Steps 1-5 only:
- Step 1 — Write failing test (RED) (line 60)
- Step 2 — Run test, confirm FAIL (line 63)
- Step 3 — Implement minimal code (GREEN) (line 70)
- Step 4 — Run test, confirm PASS (line 73)
- Step 5 — Refactor (line 80)

NOTE: These are TDD steps nested inside Execution Loop section "2. Execute with TDD". Do NOT confuse with Execution Loop top-level sections (1. Plan substeps, 2. Execute with TDD, 3. Verify completeness, etc.).

Also update the introductory text at line 13 which says "red-green-refactor-commit cycle" — change to "red-green-refactor cycle" since commit is no longer per-deliverable.

### 3. Add commit as gate before bd close
Between section "3. Verify completeness" (lines 93-98) and section "4. Close the task" (lines 100-106), insert a new section. Renumber: current "4. Close the task" becomes "5. Close the task", current "5. Propose next task" becomes "6. Propose next task".

New section to insert:

### 4. Commit

Commit all work for this task. This is MANDATORY — NEVER run bd close without committing first.

```bash
git add <specific files changed in this task>
git commit -m "<descriptive message summarizing all task work>

bd: <task-id>"
```

**CRITICAL: Committing is a prerequisite to task closure. If you skip the commit, work is not saved and may be lost. NEVER rationalize skipping — not "I'll commit later", not "finish-branch will handle it", not "it's just a small change".**

### 4. Update task completion message format
In the Message Protocol section (lines 131-159), update the Task Completion Message format to include a Commits section between Done and Learned:

```
## Task <id> Complete

### Done
- [1-3 bullet summary of what was implemented]
- [Key files created or modified]

### Commits
- [short hash]: [commit message summary]

### Learned
- [Discoveries that affect future tasks]
- [Things that differed from assumptions]
- [Or "None — executed as planned"]

### Changed from plan
- [What deviated from the task description and why]
- [Or "None — executed as planned"]

### Proposed next task
Title: [task title]
Goal: [what it delivers — one clear outcome]
Approach: [how to implement, informed by learnings from this task]
SRE refined: yes
Key considerations: [corner cases identified by SRE refinement]
```

### 5. Add anti-skip rule
In the "Rules (No Exceptions)" section starting at line 242, add a new numbered rule:

10. **Always commit before closing a task.** Run git add and git commit before bd close. NEVER defer commits to "later" or "finish-branch". Each task's work must be committed before closure. Include the commit hash(es) in your task completion message to the lead.

## Anti-Patterns (Task-Specific)
- ❌ Do NOT change TDD cycle behavior (RED-GREEN-REFACTOR stays exactly the same)
- ❌ Do NOT change startup protocol, escalation triggers, completion protocol, or any behavior other than commit timing
- ❌ Do NOT add commit back into the TDD cycle — it goes ONLY before bd close
- ❌ Do NOT use generic commit messages — message must describe all task work

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Step 6 (Commit) at lines 83-91 removed from TDD cycle section
- [ ] Line 13 intro text updated from "red-green-refactor-commit" to "red-green-refactor"
- [ ] New section "4. Commit" added between Verify completeness and Close task
- [ ] Sections after the new Commit section renumbered (Close=5, Propose=6)
- [ ] Commit step has explicit anti-skip language with specific rationalizations called out
- [ ] Task completion message format (lines 137-159) includes ### Commits section between Done and Learned
- [ ] New rule #10 added to "Rules (No Exceptions)" section about mandatory commit before close
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

title: Task 1: Restructure brainstorming Step 6 with Haiku-first philosophy
design:
## Goal
Rewrite brainstorming SKILL.md Step 6 to add a Decomposition Strategy Proposal sub-step, update classification to Haiku-first philosophy with compressed caps, raise sizing gate to ~20, and add task summary to Step 8.

## Why
This is the primary task — all other tasks replicate what this one establishes. The current Step 6 creates tasks without human strategy approval and uses classification that treats file count as a complexity signal, leading to expensive Sonnet tasks on mechanical work.

## Context
- skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md — Step 6 is at lines 276-361, Step 8 is at lines 380-403
- Current Step 6 flow: 6a sizing gate (threshold 10) → 6b create task tree
- Current classification (line 356-359): Simple = rename/config/single-file; Medium = new component/cross-file/logic
- The quick_reference table (lines 14-25) also references Step 6 and Step 8
- Line 294: opening sentence of 6b with old caps "simple (2-10 min, Haiku) or medium (10-30 min, Sonnet)"
- Line 393: Step 8 handoff text with old caps "Haiku for simple 2-10 min tasks, Sonnet for medium 10-30 min tasks"

## Implementation
1. **Step 6a — Sizing gate**: Change threshold from 10 to ~20 tasks. Keep 3+ components and multiple-deliverables checks.

2. **Step 6b — Decomposition Strategy Proposal** (NEW sub-step): Before creating tasks, the planner presents a decomposition strategy to the human via AskUserQuestion:
   - How many simple vs medium tasks
   - What the split rationale is (by file group, by operation, by component, etc.)
   - Why any tasks need Sonnet (must justify each one)
   - Human approves, adjusts, or redirects before any bd create

3. **Step 6c — Create complete task tree** (renumbered from 6b): Update classification guide:
   - Simple (2-5 min, Haiku): Mechanical changes with exact known edits. No judgment required. Complexity of the work determines model, not file count.
   - Medium (5-15 min, Sonnet): Changes requiring judgment or design decisions. Reserved for irreducible complexity.
   - Hard ceiling: 15 minutes. No exceptions.
   - Also update line 294 (opening sentence of this section) to use new caps.

4. **Haiku-first philosophy** — Add explicit framing near classification guide:
   - Default assumption: every task is Haiku
   - Sonnet requires explicit justification (what judgment does this task require?)
   - The planner's job is to minimize Sonnet usage by decomposing aggressively into mechanical Haiku tasks
   - For refactors: the pattern is defined during planning; execution is cheap Haiku tasks applying it

5. **Step 8 — Task summary**: Add a one-liner-per-task summary the human reviews before execution handoff. Format: task number, classification, description. Also update line 393 handoff text to use new caps.

6. **Quick reference table** (lines 14-25): Update Step 6 and Step 8 descriptions to reflect new sub-steps.

7. **Critical rules section** (lines 422-443): Update rule 6 to mention decomposition strategy approval. Add rule about Haiku-first default.

## Tests
None — this is a documentation-only plugin with no test suite.

## Verification
- [ ] Step 6 has three sub-steps: 6a sizing gate (~20), 6b decomposition strategy proposal, 6c task tree creation
- [ ] Classification uses complexity-based model selection, not file count
- [ ] Simple = 2-5 min Haiku, Medium = 5-15 min Sonnet
- [ ] Haiku-first philosophy is explicitly stated
- [ ] Step 8 includes one-liner task summary
- [ ] Quick reference table updated
- [ ] Critical rules updated
- [ ] grep "2-10" skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md returns no matches
- [ ] grep "10-30" skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md returns no matches
- [ ] grep "30 min" skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md returns no matches (unless in sizing gate context about task count)
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks passing

## Boundaries
- Do NOT change Steps 1-5 or Step 7
- Do NOT change other skill files (tasks 2-6 handle those)
- Do NOT add prescriptive decomposition patterns — philosophy and principles only
close_reason: Step 6 restructured with 3 sub-steps, Haiku-first philosophy, task summary in Step 8, sizing gate raised to ~20. All grep checks clean.

## Goal

Update skills/ponder/SKILL.md's 6 stale Mode 1/Mode 2 cross-references to /intuition (lines 53, 124, 212, 232, 258, 392) — these refer to /intuition's old Mode 1/Mode 2 terminology that no longer exists. Bump .claude-plugin/plugin.json minor version (3.7.0 → 3.8.0). Run final epic-level success-criteria verification across all 16 boxes from epic bd-wxj.

CLAUDE.md and README.md are already clean (0 matches for Mode 1/Mode 2/--scope/--epic/10 passes/Pass 7|10 — surveyed by lead before this task). Do not touch them.

## This task does NOT touch
- skills/intuition/SKILL.md (already trimmed in bd-428, bd-bkv, bd-5p1)
- skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md (already updated in bd-ufl)

- /ponder's own internal mode terminology (UPDATE / BOOTSTRAP / REVIEW are /ponder's own mode names; do NOT change those)

## This task DOES touch
- skills/ponder/SKILL.md (6 cross-references to /intuition's old Mode 1/Mode 2 terminology)
- .claude-plugin/plugin.json (version bump)

## Effort Estimate
1-2 hours.

## Anti-patterns (FORBIDDEN in this task)
- ❌ NO touching /ponder's own mode names (UPDATE / BOOTSTRAP / REVIEW). These are /ponder's internal terminology, not /intuition's deprecated Mode 1/Mode 2.
- ❌ NO version bump skipped (saved feedback: "ALWAYS bump plugin.json version before final push; user has had to remind multiple times")
- ❌ NO regressing prior trims (run full epic verification at end to catch regressions)
- ❌ NO unilateral trimming to hit ~1280 line target — current is 1391 lines (221 saved vs 332 target). The picked trims yielded 221; do NOT add new trims not in scope. Note the gap in the completion report; reviewer/architect decides whether to extend the epic.
- ❌ NO partial commits (commit only when all changes verified)
- ❌ NO bumping major version (minor only: 3.7.0 → 3.8.0)
- ❌ NO touching CLAUDE.md or README.md (already clean per lead survey)

## Implementation

### 1. Study existing code

- Read skills/ponder/SKILL.md lines 50-60, 120-130, 210-235, 255-265, 388-395 (the 6 specific Mode 1/Mode 2 references to understand context)
- Read .claude-plugin/plugin.json (current version 3.7.0)
- Read epic bd-wxj success criteria (16 boxes) to plan final verification

### 2. Write tests first (TDD — RED phase)

Save as /tmp/verify-bd-task5.sh, chmod +x, run pre-edit (must FAIL) then post-edit (must PASS):

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -e

P=skills/ponder/SKILL.md
J=.claude-plugin/plugin.json
I=skills/intuition/SKILL.md
B=skills/brainstorming/SKILL.md

echo "=== /ponder: stale Mode 1/Mode 2 cross-refs to /intuition removed ==="
! grep -E '\bMode 1\b' "$P" || { echo "FAIL: 'Mode 1' phrase still in /ponder"; exit 1; }
! grep -E '\bMode 2\b' "$P" || { echo "FAIL: 'Mode 2' phrase still in /ponder"; exit 1; }

echo "=== /ponder: own UPDATE/BOOTSTRAP/REVIEW mode names preserved ==="
grep -E 'UPDATE mode|UPDATE:' "$P" > /dev/null || { echo "FAIL: /ponder UPDATE mode terminology missing"; exit 1; }
grep -E 'BOOTSTRAP mode|BOOTSTRAP:' "$P" > /dev/null || { echo "FAIL: /ponder BOOTSTRAP mode terminology missing"; exit 1; }
grep -E 'REVIEW mode|REVIEW:' "$P" > /dev/null || { echo "FAIL: /ponder REVIEW mode terminology missing"; exit 1; }

echo "=== plugin.json: version bumped to 3.8.0 ==="
grep -F '"version": "3.8.0"' "$J" > /dev/null || { echo "FAIL: plugin.json version not bumped to 3.8.0"; exit 1; }
! grep -F '"version": "3.7.0"' "$J" || { echo "FAIL: plugin.json still on 3.7.0"; exit 1; }

echo "=== /intuition: prior trim work intact (regression checks) ==="
! grep -E '\bMode [12]\b' "$I" || { echo "FAIL: Mode 1/Mode 2 reappeared in /intuition"; exit 1; }
! grep -F -- '--scope' "$I" || { echo "FAIL: --scope reappeared"; exit 1; }
! grep -F 'Dynamic View Drift' "$I" || { echo "FAIL: Pass 7 content reappeared in /intuition"; exit 1; }
grep -E 'Run ALL 9 passes' "$I" > /dev/null || { echo "FAIL: 'Run ALL 9 passes' missing"; exit 1; }
! grep -F 'Mode: BOOTSTRAP' "$I" || { echo "FAIL: BOOTSTRAP template reappeared in /intuition"; exit 1; }
grep -F 'Optional: Prose Focus' "$I" > /dev/null || { echo "FAIL: Prose Focus subsection missing"; exit 1; }
grep -F 'NO recommendations' "$I" > /dev/null || { echo "FAIL: rule 1 missing"; exit 1; }

echo "=== /brainstorm: prose-focus update intact ==="
grep -E 'prose focus' "$B" > /dev/null || { echo "FAIL: brainstorm prose-focus reference missing"; exit 1; }
! grep -F -- '--scope' "$B" || { echo "FAIL: brainstorm has --scope flag"; exit 1; }

echo "All Task 5 checks passed."
```

### 3. Implementation checklist

**Section A: Update /ponder Mode cross-references**

For each of the 6 lines, REPLACE the stale Mode 1/Mode 2 reference with /intuition's current terminology:

- [ ] skills/ponder/SKILL.md:53 — `Intuition Step 4 first Resolve on Mode 2 audit` → `Intuition Step 4 first Resolve on audit without architecture model`
- [ ] skills/ponder/SKILL.md:124 — `Note: "Future audits will run in Mode 1"` → `Note: "Future audits will load the model"`
- [ ] skills/ponder/SKILL.md:212 — `Intuition Step 4: architect resolves first tension with "Resolve" on Mode 2 audit.` → `Intuition Step 4: architect resolves first tension with "Resolve" on audit without architecture model.`
- [ ] skills/ponder/SKILL.md:232 — `Source: Intuition Mode 2 audit findings + architect input` → `Source: Intuition audit (no architecture model) findings + architect input`
- [ ] skills/ponder/SKILL.md:258 — `Architecture model bootstrapped. Future audits will run in Mode 1.` → `Architecture model bootstrapped. Future audits will load the model.`
- [ ] skills/ponder/SKILL.md:392 — `Step 4: first Resolve on Mode 2 → /ponder bootstrap → model created → future Mode 1` → `Step 4: first Resolve on audit without model → /ponder bootstrap → model created → future audits load the model`

NOTE: Line numbers may have shifted slightly — use grep `\bMode [12]\b skills/ponder/SKILL.md` to find current locations if line refs are off. Do NOT update /ponder's own UPDATE/BOOTSTRAP/REVIEW mode names — those are /ponder's internal terminology.

**Section B: Bump plugin version**

- [ ] Read .claude-plugin/plugin.json
- [ ] Edit `"version": "3.7.0"` → `"version": "3.8.0"` (minor bump; epic adds prose-focus mechanism + consolidates ownership which are user-facing changes)

**Section C: Final epic-level verification**

Run `bd show bd-wxj` and verify each success criterion:

- [ ] Run all prior tasks' verification scripts (or equivalent grep checks) to confirm no regressions:
  - `bash /tmp/verify-bd-428.sh` (Mode Gate, scope, freshness, prose focus)
  - `bash /tmp/verify-bd-task2.sh` (Pass 7, renumber)
  - `bash /tmp/verify-bd-task3.sh` (Bootstrap simplification)
  - `bash /tmp/verify-bd-task4.sh` (brainstorm prose focus)
  - `bash /tmp/verify-bd-task5.sh` (this task)
- [ ] Verify each of the 16 epic success criteria. For criteria already verified by prior task scripts, note "verified via task verification". For novel checks (e.g., line count vs target), note actual value:
  - [ ] /intuition no Mode 1/Mode 2 — verified via /tmp/verify-bd-428.sh
  - [ ] /intuition no --scope/--epic — verified via /tmp/verify-bd-428.sh
  - [ ] /intuition has 9 passes — verified via /tmp/verify-bd-task2.sh
  - [ ] /intuition documents Optional Prose Focus — verified via /tmp/verify-bd-428.sh
  - [ ] /intuition no Model Freshness check — verified via /tmp/verify-bd-428.sh
  - [ ] /intuition Step 4 Resolve no longer templates /ponder bootstrap input — verified via /tmp/verify-bd-task3.sh
  - [ ] Pass 7 logic codified in /ponder REVIEW — verified via /tmp/verify-bd-task2.sh
  - [ ] /brainstorm uses prose focus — verified via /tmp/verify-bd-task4.sh
  - [ ] Pass references renumbered — verified via /tmp/verify-bd-task2.sh
  - [ ] Cross-references explicit — verify via this task (after /ponder Mode refs updated)
  - [ ] Anti-recommendation rules verbatim — verified via every prior task verification
  - [ ] Resolution Protocol unchanged — manual spot-check in this task
  - [ ] Architect Questions unchanged — manual spot-check in this task
  - [ ] CLAUDE.md / README.md updated — note: lead survey showed both already clean; no edits needed; criterion considered met
  - [ ] plugin.json version bumped — verified via /tmp/verify-bd-task5.sh
  - [ ] Reduces /intuition SKILL.md by ~300+ lines — actual: 221 lines (1612 → 1391). Below target (~330) but the picked trims were all completed; further reduction would require unscoped changes.

**Section D: Report final line count and any open concerns in completion report**

- [ ] Note: actual /intuition line count is ~1391 (started 1612). Reduction is 221 lines, which is below the "~300+ lines" target. The trims explicitly picked by the architect during brainstorm yielded this; no additional in-scope trims remain.
- [ ] If reviewer raises this gap, options are: (a) accept reality and revise the criterion; (b) extend the epic with a follow-up task to find more trims; (c) close the epic with this gap noted.

**Section E: Pre-commit + verify**

- [ ] Run `bash /tmp/verify-bd-task5.sh` against pre-edit state; confirm it FAILS (RED phase).
- [ ] Make edits per Sections A-B.
- [ ] Run `bash /tmp/verify-bd-task5.sh` against post-edit state; confirm "All Task 5 checks passed."
- [ ] Run all prior task verification scripts; confirm no regressions.
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks pass.
- [ ] Commit (single commit; do not amend).

## Success Criteria

**Verification grep checks (must all pass):**
- [ ] `! grep -E '\bMode 1\b' skills/ponder/SKILL.md` succeeds (no /intuition Mode 1 cross-ref)
- [ ] `! grep -E '\bMode 2\b' skills/ponder/SKILL.md` succeeds (no /intuition Mode 2 cross-ref)
- [ ] `grep -E 'UPDATE mode' skills/ponder/SKILL.md` succeeds (own mode names preserved)
- [ ] `grep -E 'BOOTSTRAP mode' skills/ponder/SKILL.md` succeeds
- [ ] `grep -E 'REVIEW mode' skills/ponder/SKILL.md` succeeds
- [ ] `grep -F '"version": "3.8.0"' .claude-plugin/plugin.json` succeeds
- [ ] `! grep -F '"version": "3.7.0"' .claude-plugin/plugin.json` succeeds

**Regression checks (all prior task verifications must still pass):**
- [ ] /intuition: no Mode 1/Mode 2, no --scope/--epic, no Dynamic View Drift, "Run ALL 9 passes" present, no Mode: BOOTSTRAP template, Optional: Prose Focus present, NO recommendations rule present
- [ ] /brainstorm: prose focus referenced, no --scope flag

**Manual checks:**
- [ ] Resolution Protocol Step 4 in /intuition still has 5 options (Accept, Resolve, Brainstorm, Investigate, Skip)
- [ ] Architect Questions in /intuition Step 3 still present (Meadows / Alexander / Rao)
- [ ] Anti-recommendation self-check rules 1-4 in /intuition Critical Rules verbatim
- [ ] Pre-commit hooks pass

**Final epic verification (report results in completion):**
- [ ] All 16 epic success criteria verified (or noted with status if not strictly met)
- [ ] Line count gap noted: 1391 actual vs ~1280 target; 221 line reduction vs ~330 target

## Edge Cases & Failure Modes

**/ponder's own UPDATE/BOOTSTRAP/REVIEW mode names accidentally edited:**
- The Mode 1/Mode 2 grep patterns (`\bMode [12]\b`) deliberately use word boundaries and digit constraints to avoid matching /ponder's own UPDATE/BOOTSTRAP/REVIEW. Verify that the renumber-style verification doesn't accidentally match these.

**Line numbers shifted from prior survey:**
- The lead surveyed at lines 53, 124, 212, 232, 258, 392 BEFORE this task. After other commits in this epic, line numbers may have shifted. Use grep to find current locations rather than relying on line numbers.

**Major version desired instead of minor:**
- Per epic requirements: "minor bump". Do NOT bump major (3.7.0 → 4.0.0). 3.8.0 is correct.

**Reviewer flags line-count gap:**
- Anticipated. Document the gap clearly in the completion report. The picked trims yielded 221 lines; reaching ~330 would require additional in-scope trims. Architect can decide.

**CLAUDE.md or README.md actually have references that the survey missed:**
- Re-grep them in this task as a sanity check. Survey was: `grep -cE '\bMode [12]\b|--scope|--epic|10 passes|Pass (7|10)\b'` → both 0. If repeat survey shows new matches, address them as in-scope; otherwise the criterion "CLAUDE.md and README.md mentions of /intuition modes/scopes updated" is met by absence (nothing to update).

**Concurrent edits:**
- Single-author refactor; no concurrency concerns.

## Notes for next task
After this task completes, the epic is done (or has a documented line-count gap). The lead should dispatch the reviewer agent for final verification.

If line-count gap is unacceptable to architect: a follow-up task could find additional trims (most likely candidates: Pass 6 State/Identity, Pass description verbosity, Examples compression). But that decision belongs to the architect.

description: Final task for epic bd-6uw. Three coordinated cleanups: bump plugin.json 3.8.0→3.8.1, remove sre-task-refinement from agents/executor.md skills frontmatter (flagged as follow-up in bd-3nj learnings), run cross-file consistency grep across agents/skills/commands/docs to verify no stale executor-side SRE references remain.
